# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydro Electric Power Plant — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RESERVOIR\nWATER STORAGE] --> A2[INTAKE\nSTRUCTURE]
    A2 --> A3[TRASH RACKS\nDEBRIS FILTER]
    A3 --> A4[PENSTOCK\nHIGH PRESSURE PIPE]
    A4 --> A5[TURBINE\nPELTON OR FRANCIS]
    A5 --> A6[GOVERNOR\nSPEED CONTROL]
    A5 --> A7[GENERATOR\nSYNCHRONOUS]
    A7 --> A8[STEP UP\nTRANSFORMER]
    A8 --> A9[TRANSMISSION\nLINE]
    A5 --> A10[TAILRACE\nWATER DISCHARGE]
    A11[COOLING AND\nAUXILIARY SYSTEM] -.-> A7
    A12[CONTROL ROOM\nAND SWITCHYARD] -.-> A8
```

**Reading Guide:** Water flows from the **reservoir** (top-left) through intake → trash racks → penstock → turbine → tailrace (bottom-right). The turbine mechanically drives the **generator**, whose electrical output is stepped up for transmission. The dashed lines represent auxiliary services (cooling, control, switchyard).

---

## 4.2 Thermal (Steam) Power Plant — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[COAL YARD\nAND BUNKER] --> B2[COAL MILL\nPULVERIZER]
    B2 --> B3[BOILER\nFURNACE]
    B4[FORCED DRAFT\nAIR SUPPLY] --> B3
    B3 --> B5[SUPERHEATER\nSTEAM AT 540 C]
    B5 --> B6[HP TURBINE\nHIGH PRESSURE]
    B6 --> B7[IP TURBINE\nINTERMEDIATE]
    B7 --> B8[LP TURBINE\nLOW PRESSURE]
    B8 --> B9[CONDENSER\nVACUUM CHAMBER]
    B9 --> B10[CONDENSATE\nPUMP]
    B10 --> B11[DEAERATOR\nOXYGEN REMOVAL]
    B11 --> B12[FEEDWATER\nPUMP]
    B12 --> B3
    B8 --> B13[GENERATOR\nSYNCHRONOUS]
    B13 --> B14[STEP UP\nTRANSFORMER]
    B14 --> B15[GRID\nTRANSMISSION]
    B3 --> B16[ECONOMIZER\nFEEDWATER HEAT]
    B3 --> B17[FLUE GAS\nAND ESP]
    B17 --> B18[STACK\nCHIMNEY EMISSION]
    B13 -.-> B19[EXCITATION\nAND CONTROL]
```

**Reading Guide:** Coal + air enter the **boiler** (top). The hot gases transfer heat to water (right side loop: condenser → pump → deaerator → feedwater pump → back to boiler). Steam expands through HP → IP → LP turbines (centre column) and ends in the condenser. The turbine drives the generator (right). Flue gases leave via the **ESP (Electrostatic Precipitator)** to the chimney.

---

## 4.3 Nuclear Power Plant — Block Schematic (PWR Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[FUEL ASSEMBLY\nUO2 PELLETS] --> C2[REACTOR CORE\nFISSION ZONE]
    C3[CONTROL RODS\nBORON CARBIDE] --> C2
    C2 --> C4[REACTOR COOLANT\nPRIMARY LOOP]
    C4 --> C5[STEAM GENERATOR\nHEAT EXCHANGER]
    C5 --> C6[SECONDARY LOOP\nSTEAM TO TURBINE]
    C6 --> C7[HP TURBINE]
    C7 --> C8[LP TURBINE]
    C8 --> C9[CONDENSER]
    C9 --> C10[FEEDWATER PUMP]
    C10 --> C5
    C8 --> C11[GENERATOR\nSYNCHRONOUS]
    C11 --> C12[TRANSFORMER]
    C12 --> C13[GRID]
    C5 --> C14[PRESSURIZER\nPRESSURE CONTROL]
    C2 --> C15[CONTAINMENT\nSTRUCTURE]
    C2 --> C16[SPENT FUEL POOL\nCOOLING STORAGE]
    C16 --> C17[DRY CASK\nLONG TERM STORAGE]
    C11 -.-> C18[INSTRUMENTATION\nAND CONTROL]
```

**Reading Guide:** Note the **two distinct loops** — the **primary loop** (radioactive coolant from the reactor) and the **secondary loop** (clean steam driving the turbine). They are physically separated by the steam generator, ensuring **no radioactive material reaches the turbine**. Spent fuel is moved to the spent fuel pool and later to dry cask storage.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Process Flow — All Three Sources

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[SUN AND\nWATER CYCLE] --> D2[HYDRO\nGENERATION]
    D3[COAL FROM\nMINES] --> D4[THERMAL\nGENERATION]
    D5[URANIUM\nFROM MINES] --> D6[NUCLEAR\nGENERATION]
    D2 --> D7[STEP UP\nTRANSFORMER]
    D4 --> D7
    D6 --> D7
    D7 --> D8[HIGH VOLTAGE\nTRANSMISSION GRID]
    D8 --> D9[STEP DOWN\nDISTRIBUTION]
    D9 --> D10[CONSUMER\nLOADS]
```

**Reading Guide:** All three paths converge at the **step-up transformer** and enter the common **grid**. From the perspective of the consumer, electricity from all three sources is **physically identical** — the difference lies in the upstream environmental, economic, and ethical footprint.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Differentiate between renewable and non-renewable energy sources. Give two examples each.**

**Model Answer (Expected for 3 marks):**

| Feature | Renewable | Non-Renewable |
|:--------|:----------|:--------------|
| Definition | Replenished by natural cycles | Finite, exhaustible |
| Replenishment rate | Faster than consumption | Slower than consumption (geological) |
| Examples | Hydro, Solar, Wind, Tidal | Coal, Natural Gas, Uranium, Oil |
| Carbon footprint | Low/Zero | High |
| Sustainability | Sustainable long-term | Unsustainable long-term |

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark for each correct point + 0.5 for each example = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Why is nuclear energy classified as non-renewable even though it does not directly emit CO₂?**

**Model Answer:**
Nuclear energy is classified as non-renewable because **(i) the fuel U-235 is finite** and **depletes with use**; **(ii)** the **regeneration time of usable fissile material is in millions of years**; **(iii)** the **spent fuel remains hazardous for 10,000 to 240,000 years**, creating a long-term liability. Hence, despite low-carbon operation, it is treated as a finite resource.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark for each valid reason = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block schematic, explain the working of a **hydroelectric power plant**. Discuss the different types of turbines used based on head and flow conditions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A hydroelectric plant has an effective head of 100 m, flow rate of 50 m³/s, and overall efficiency of 85%. Calculate: (i) the power generated in MW, (ii) the daily energy production in kWh, and (iii) the volume of water required per day. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- Water stored in a reservoir at a height $H$ possesses gravitational potential energy.
- It is released through intake and penstock to a turbine that converts hydraulic energy to mechanical (rotational) energy.
- The turbine drives a synchronous generator that produces electrical energy.
- The schematic is shown below (refer to SECTION 4.1).
- Water exits via tailrace.

**Types of Turbines (4 marks):**

| Turbine Type | Category | Head Range | Flow Rate |
|:-------------|:---------|:-----------|:----------|
| **Pelton Wheel** | Impulse | High (> 300 m) | Low |
| **Francis Turbine** | Reaction (mixed flow) | Medium (30–300 m) | Medium |
| **Kaplan / Propeller** | Reaction (axial) | Low (< 30 m) | High |

*(Marking scheme: 3 marks for schematic, 2 marks for working, 2 marks for turbine classification = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
$H = 100$ m, $Q = 50$ m³/s, $\eta = 0.85$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $t = 24$ h.

**(i) Power Generated:**

$$P = \eta \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

$$P = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 50 \times 100$$

$$P = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 49050 = 4.169 \times 10^7 \text{ W} = 41.69 \text{ MW}$$

**(ii) Daily Energy Production:**

$$E = P \times t = 41.69 \times 10^3 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 1,000,560 \text{ kWh} \approx 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh}$$

**(iii) Volume of Water Required:**

$$V = Q \times t = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 4.32 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with units = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain with a block schematic the working of a **thermal (steam) power plant**. State the functions of the economizer, superheater, and condenser. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A thermal power plant uses coal with calorific value 25,000 kJ/kg. The plant produces 200 MW of electrical output with an overall efficiency of 35%. Calculate: (i) the heat input rate, (ii) the coal consumption per hour, and (iii) the heat rejected to the condenser per second. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working (3 marks):**
Coal is pulverized and burned in a boiler furnace with preheated air. The heat converts water into high-pressure superheated steam, which expands in HP/IP/LP turbines coupled to a generator. Exhaust steam is condensed in a condenser and recycled via a feedwater pump. The schematic (see SECTION 4.2) shows the closed loop.

**Component Functions (4 marks):**

- **Economizer:** Recovers residual heat from flue gases to pre-heat feedwater, **improving boiler efficiency by 5–10%**.
- **Superheater:** Raises saturated steam temperature above its saturation point (e.g., 540 °C), **increasing Rankine cycle efficiency** and reducing turbine blade erosion.
- **Condenser:** Condenses spent steam to water at very low pressure (vacuum ≈ 0.1 bar), **maximizing turbine work output** and providing reusable feedwater.

*(Marking scheme: 3 marks for schematic, 4 marks for component functions = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
$P_{\text{el}} = 200$ MW, $\eta = 0.35$, $\text{CV} = 25{,}000$ kJ/kg.

**(i) Heat Input Rate:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{el}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}} \Rightarrow \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{el}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{200}{0.35} = 571.43 \text{ MW}$$

In kJ/s: $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 571.43 \times 10^3 \text{ kJ/s} = 5.7143 \times 10^5$ kJ/s

**(ii) Coal Consumption per Hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}}{\text{CV}} = \dfrac{5.7143 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ/s}}{25000 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 22.86 \text{ kg/s}$$

Per hour: $22.86 \times 3600 = 82{,}286$ kg/h ≈ **82.3 tonnes/hour**

**(iii) Heat Rejected to Condenser:**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{el}} = 571.43 - 200 = 371.43 \text{ MW}$$

Or equivalently: $\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} (1 - \eta) = 571.43 \times 0.65 = 371.43$ MW ✅

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii) = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points in Module 1 Generation Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert MW to W (×10⁶) or hours to seconds (×3600). A 1-unit error here costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Missing Schematic:** KTU requires a *neat labelled block diagram* in any 7+ mark generation question. **3 marks are reserved** for the diagram alone. Drawing it without labels gives only 1 mark.
> 3. **No Mention of Losses/Real vs. Ideal:** Writing only the Carnot formula without mentioning Rankine cycle limitations loses **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Energy and Power:** Mixing $E$ (kWh) with $P$ (kW) is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **No Environmental Context:** For 14-mark questions, KTU 2024 explicitly allocates **2–3 marks for environmental/ethical factors** (per NEP 2020 CO mapping). Skipping this loses easy marks.
> 6. **Numerical Rounding:** Always carry **2 decimal places minimum** and write units at every step. Examiner-friendly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (Save This for Last-Minute Prep):**

### ✅ Quick Definitions
- **Renewable source:** Naturally replenished on human timescales (hydro, solar, wind).
- **Non-renewable source:** Finite, geological-timescale replenishment (coal, uranium, oil).
- **Hydro:** Direct conversion of gravitational PE to electricity.
- **Thermal:** Chemical energy of coal → heat → steam → mechanical → electrical.
- **Nuclear:** Binding energy of U-235 → heat → steam → mechanical → electrical.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
- $P = \eta \rho g Q H$ (Hydro)
- $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_2/T_1$ (Thermal ceiling)
- $E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$ (Nuclear)
- $m_{\text{coal}} = P / (\eta \cdot \text{CV})$ (Fuel rate)

### ✅ Constants to Remember
- $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $1 \text{ u} = 931.494$ MeV/c²
- $1$ fission ≈ $200$ MeV

### ✅ Advantages & Disadvantages Snapshot

**Hydro:** ✅ Clean, renewable, low O&M, long life, fast ramping | ❌ High capital, large land, displacement issues, drought-dependent.

**Thermal:** ✅ Mature tech, low capital, fuel widely available, dispatchable | ❌ High emissions, fly ash, slow start-up, coal price volatility.

**Nuclear:** ✅ High energy density, low CO₂, reliable baseload | ❌ Radioactive waste, high capital, long build, safety/public perception.

### ✅ Environmental & Ethical Factors (2–3 mark favourite)
- **Hydro:** Displacement of tribal/forest communities, seismic risk from reservoir loading, downstream ecosystem change, methane from submerged biomass.
- **Thermal:** CO₂ (climate change), SO₂/NOₓ (acid rain), fly ash (heavy metals, lung disease), thermal pollution of rivers.
- **Nuclear:** Long-term waste storage, radiation risk (Chernobyl, Fukushima), proliferation of fissile material, inter-generational ethical burden (we benefit, future generations manage the waste).

### ✅ Numerical Practice Topics (Priority Order for KTU 2024)
1. Hydro power from given $H$, $Q$, $\eta$ — **MUST practice**.
2. Carnot efficiency with K conversion — **MUST practice**.
3. Coal consumption from given CV and efficiency — **MUST practice**.
4. Fission energy / mass defect calculation — **Good-to-have**.

> **Final Tip:** Always pair a **diagram** with your answer. In KTU's outcome-based valuation, a labelled diagram is often the difference between a 10/14 and a 14/14.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar and Wind (Block Schematic Details, Environmental Factors, Regulations, Advantages, Disadvantages)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar and Wind (Block Schematic Details, Environmental Factors, Regulations, Advantages, Disadvantages)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Energy Conversion Systems — Formal Definition

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Power Generation System** is an arrangement of semiconductor devices (typically silicon-based cells) that directly convert incident solar irradiance into direct current (DC) electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect**, as described by the quantum mechanical interaction of photons with P-N junction semiconductor material. The generated DC power is then conditioned through power electronic converters (DC-DC with Maximum Power Point Tracking and DC-AC inverter) and stepped up to grid-compatible voltage levels for delivery to the load or the utility grid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must clearly identify the role of *each block* in the solar PV schematic — from the PV array to the point of common coupling (PCC). Examiners frequently ask "Explain with a neat block diagram" for 7 or 14 mark questions.

## 1.2 Wind Energy Conversion Systems — Formal Definition

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is a mechanical-electrical assembly that extracts kinetic energy from moving air masses using aerodynamically designed turbine blades, converts this kinetic energy into rotational mechanical energy on the shaft, and subsequently transforms the mechanical energy into electrical energy through a generator (most commonly a Doubly-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG) or a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG)). The variable-frequency, variable-voltage output is then processed by power electronic converters and synchronized with the grid.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Wheel vs. Sun Umbrella" Comparison**

**Imagine a Water Wheel in a River (Wind):** A wind turbine behaves like a water wheel placed in a flowing river. The faster the river flows (higher wind speed), the more spin you get on the wheel (more mechanical torque). The water wheel's buckets cannot catch *all* the water — some spills past unused. This "spillage" in a wind turbine is captured mathematically by the **power coefficient (Cp)**, which has a theoretical upper limit known as the **Betz Limit = 0.593 (or 59.3 %)**.

**Imagine a Sun Umbrella Charging a Phone (Solar):** A solar panel is like a smart umbrella that, instead of blocking sunlight, traps it and uses the energy to charge your phone. The brighter the sun and the larger the umbrella surface, the more charge you get per hour. However, a hot umbrella (overheated solar cell) charges slower than a cool one — this is why **temperature inversely affects solar panel efficiency**.

Both systems then send the "captured energy" to a **translator box** (the power electronic inverter), which converts the raw, "wild" form (DC for solar, variable-frequency AC for wind) into the **clean, grid-standard 50 Hz, 415 V AC** that your home appliances understand.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Standard Test Conditions (STC) for Solar:** Irradiance = **1000 W/m²**, Cell Temperature = **25 °C (298 K)**, Air Mass = **AM 1.5**
> - **Betz Limit (Wind):** Maximum extractable power fraction = **0.5926 (≈ 59.3 %)**
> - **Air Density at sea level (15 °C):** $\rho$ = **1.225 kg/m³**
> - **Grid Frequency in India:** **50 Hz**
> - **Rated Wind Speed (typical):** **12 m/s**
> - **Cut-in / Cut-out Wind Speed:** **3 m/s / 25 m/s**
> - **Power Loss Factor due to Temperature (Solar):** Approximately **−0.4 % to −0.5 % per °C** above 25 °C

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar PV System — Block-by-Block Operational Logic

A grid-connected solar PV system comprises the following sequential blocks:

1. **PV Array (Generator Block):** Multiple PV modules connected in series (to raise voltage) and parallel (to raise current) to meet the inverter DC-link requirement. Each cell produces approximately **0.5 V to 0.7 V** open-circuit.
2. **DC-DC Boost Converter with MPPT:** Steps up the low PV voltage to a higher DC-link voltage. The **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** algorithm (Perturb & Observe or Incremental Conductance) continuously adjusts the duty cycle to harvest peak available power under varying irradiance.
3. **DC-AC Voltage Source Inverter (VSI):** Converts DC to 50 Hz AC using PWM (Pulse Width Modulation). Usually a two-level or three-level IGBT-based topology.
4. **LC / LCL Filter:** Suppresses high-frequency switching harmonics generated by the inverter, ensuring the output current waveform meets IEEE 519 harmonic standards.
5. **Step-Up Transformer:** Raises the low inverter voltage (e.g., 415 V) to the grid's HT level (e.g., 11 kV or 33 kV).
6. **Point of Common Coupling (PCC) / Grid:** The interface with the utility network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is MPPT essential?** A PV cell's V-I curve has a unique "knee point" where the product V × I is maximum. This point shifts with temperature and irradiance. Without MPPT, the system may operate far below its true potential — losing **20 % to 30 %** of harvestable energy.

## 2.2 Wind Energy Conversion System — Block-by-Block Operational Logic

1. **Rotor Blades (Aerodynamic Block):** Capture kinetic energy of wind. Typically three blades for horizontal-axis designs (HAWT), per **IEC 61400** design standards.
2. **Gearbox (Mechanical Coupling):** Steps up the slow rotor speed (~15–20 rpm) to the high generator shaft speed (~1500 rpm for a 4-pole 50 Hz machine). Modern direct-drive PMSG systems eliminate the gearbox.
3. **Generator (IG, DFIG, or PMSG):** Converts mechanical to electrical energy.
4. **Power Electronic Converter (AC-DC-AC for Type-3/4 turbines):** Rectifier → DC-link → Inverter. The grid-side inverter regulates active and reactive power, and the generator-side converter performs MPPT on the wind turbine curve.
5. **Filter & Step-up Transformer:** Cleans harmonics and raises voltage for grid injection.
6. **Grid / Microgrid / Isolated Load:** Final destination.

## 2.3 Environmental Factors Affecting Power Generation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Impact Matrix for KTU Exam**

| Energy Source | Critical Environmental Factors | Effect on Power Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar PV** | Solar Irradiance (W/m²) | Output power is **directly proportional** to irradiance. Doubling G doubles P (linear relationship). |
| **Solar PV** | Cell Temperature (°C) | Higher temperature **decreases** voltage by ~2 mV/°C/cell and **reduces** efficiency. |
| **Solar PV** | Dust, Soiling, Shading | Can cause **5 % to 30 %** output loss. Shading may trigger hotspot damage without bypass diodes. |
| **Solar PV** | Humidity, Cloud Cover, Air Mass | Higher air mass and cloud cover reduce spectral energy reaching the cell. |
| **Wind** | Wind Speed (m/s) | Output power is **proportional to the cube** of wind speed. Doubling v gives **8×** more power! |
| **Wind** | Air Density (kg/m³) | Higher altitude (lower ρ) and higher temperature reduce power output linearly. |
| **Wind** | Tower Height | Wind speed follows the **power law**: $v(h) = v_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{h_{ref}}\right)^\alpha$ where $\alpha$ = terrain coefficient (1/7 for open terrain). |
| **Wind** | Turbulence, Wind Shear | Affects fatigue life, blade stress, and power quality (flicker). |

## 2.4 Regulatory Standards (Must-Know for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulations Governing Renewable Energy in India and Globally**

- **IEEE 1547** — Standard for interconnecting distributed resources (including solar and wind) with electric power systems.
- **IEEE 519** — Harmonic control in electrical power systems.
- **IEC 61215** — Design qualification and type approval for crystalline silicon terrestrial PV modules.
- **IEC 61727** — Photovoltaic (PV) systems — Utility interface characteristics.
- **IEC 61400** — Design requirements for wind turbines (Part 1: Design requirements; Part 21: Measurements and assessment of power quality).
- **CEA (Central Electricity Authority) Regulations, 2019** — Technical standards for connectivity of the distributed generation resources to the grid in India.
- **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy) Guidelines** — Policy framework, tariffs, and renewable purchase obligation (RPO) norms in India.
- **Indian Electricity Act, 2003** — Legal framework for generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity.
- **CERC / SERC Regulations** — Central and State Electricity Regulatory Commissions govern tariff determination.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for Board Exam**

| Formula | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **PV Module Power** | $P_{pv} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta_{pv}$ | A = area (m²), G = irradiance (W/m²), η = conversion efficiency |
| **Maximum Power (PV Cell)** | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ | FF = Fill Factor (typically 0.7 to 0.85) |
| **Fill Factor** | $FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Measure of cell "squareness" |
| **Temperature Effect on Voltage** | $V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + \beta \cdot (T - 25)$ | β = temperature coefficient (negative, ~−0.3 %/°C) |
| **Wind Power Available** | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3}$ | ρ = air density, A = swept area, v = wind speed |
| **Wind Power Captured** | $P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3} \cdot C_{p}$ | Cp = power coefficient of turbine |
| **Betz Limit** | $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | Theoretical maximum |
| **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** | $\lambda = \frac{\omega \cdot R}{v}$ | ω = angular velocity (rad/s), R = rotor radius |
| **Power Coefficient (Cp)** | $C_{p} = f(\lambda, \beta)$ | β = blade pitch angle |
| **Wind Speed Power Law** | $\frac{v}{v_{0}} = \left(\frac{h}{h_{0}}\right)^{\alpha}$ | α = terrain roughness coefficient |
| **Swept Area of Turbine** | $A = \pi \cdot R^{2}$ | R = rotor blade radius |
| **Capacity Utilization Factor** | $CUF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760}$ | Actual annual energy / maximum possible |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solar and wind systems form the cornerstone of **Distributed Generation (DG)** and **Microgrid** architectures. The output of these systems is inherently intermittent; hence, they rely on power electronic interfaces for grid integration, **storage systems (batteries, supercapacitors)** for smoothing, and **forecasting algorithms** for scheduling. Modern 500 MW solar parks (e.g., Bhadla Park in Rajasthan) and offshore wind farms (e.g., off the coast of Gujarat and Tamil Nadu) demonstrate the scalability of these technologies in the Indian renewable energy mission targeting **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Extractable Wind Power (Betz Limit)

**Step 1: Write the Kinetic Energy of an Air Mass**

The kinetic energy of a mass of air $m$ moving with velocity $v$ is:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}$$

**Step 2: Express Mass in terms of Density and Volume**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$

where $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³), $A$ = swept area (m²), and $L$ = length of air column passing per second.

**Step 3: Substitute L = v (length per second)**

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$$

**Step 4: Write the Total Available Wind Power**

The power is kinetic energy per unit time, so substituting $m$ into $E_k$:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3}$$

This is the **raw aerodynamic power** present in the wind stream. A turbine can only extract a fraction of this.

**Step 5: Apply the Continuity and Bernoulli Principles**

For an ideal turbine (inviscid, incompressible flow), the air stream slows from upstream velocity $v_1$ to downstream velocity $v_2$ after passing through the rotor at velocity $v$. Applying Euler's turbine equation, the power extracted is:

$$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot (v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2})$$

**Step 6: Express in terms of the Induction Factor 'a'**

Define the axial induction factor $a$ such that $v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$. Then $v_1 = \frac{v}{1-a}$ and $v_2 = (1-a) \cdot v$ (substituting and rearranging gives $v_1 = v(1+a)/(1-a)$ when using actuator disk theory).

**Step 7: Formulate the Power Coefficient**

$$C_{p} = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$

**Step 8: Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$**

To find the maximum, differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4 \cdot \left[(1-a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 0$$

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4 \cdot (1-a) \cdot \left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

**Step 9: Solve for the Optimal Induction Factor**

Setting the non-trivial solution to zero:

$$1 - 3a = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 10: Substitute back to find the Maximum Power Coefficient**

$$C_{p,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ or } 59.3\%}$$

This is the **Betz Limit** — the absolute theoretical maximum a wind turbine can extract from the wind. No real turbine exceeds this, and practical turbines operate in the range of **25 % to 45 %**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Solar PV Output

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 1 m² solar panel operates at 800 W/m² irradiance and 35 °C cell temperature. Given: $V_{oc}$ at STC = 22 V, $I_{sc}$ at STC = 5 A, Fill Factor = 0.75, Temperature coefficient $\beta$ = −0.3 %/°C. Calculate the operating $V_{oc}$ and the power output.

**Step 1: Apply the irradiance correction to $I_{sc}$**

Short-circuit current scales linearly with irradiance:

$$I_{sc,actual} = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \frac{G_{actual}}{G_{STC}} = 5 \cdot \frac{800}{1000} = 4.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Apply the temperature correction to $V_{oc}$**

Temperature rise above STC: $\Delta T = 35 - 25 = 10$ °C

$$\beta_{absolute} = -0.3\% \cdot \frac{22}{100} = -0.066 \text{ V/°C}$$

$$V_{oc,actual} = V_{oc,STC} + \beta \cdot \Delta T = 22 + (-0.066)(10) = 22 - 0.66 = 21.34 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Compute Maximum Power Point Power**

Assuming FF remains approximately constant for simplicity:

$$P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc,actual} \cdot I_{sc,actual} = 0.75 \cdot 21.34 \cdot 4.0 = 64.02 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Compute Conversion Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{A \cdot G} = \frac{64.02}{1 \cdot 800} = 0.080 \text{ or } 8.0\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 1 m² panel rated at STC would be ~100 W. At 800 W/m² and elevated temperature, real output drops — illustrating **derating due to environmental factors** that KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Wind Turbine Power Output

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A wind turbine has rotor diameter 80 m, wind speed 12 m/s, air density 1.225 kg/m³, and power coefficient 0.40. Calculate (a) Mechanical power, (b) Annual energy generation assuming 25 % capacity factor.

**Step 1: Compute Swept Area**

$$A = \pi \cdot R^{2} = \pi \cdot (40)^{2} = 5026.55 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Compute Available Wind Power**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot (12)^{3}$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728 = 5{,}320{,}143 \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3: Compute Mechanical (Captured) Power**

$$P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \cdot 5.32 = 2.128 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4: Compute Annual Energy**

Hours in a year = 8760 h

$$E_{annual} = P_{rated} \cdot CUF \cdot 8760 = 2.128 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 0.25 \cdot 8760$$

$$E_{annual} = 4.66 \cdot 10^{9} \text{ Wh} = 4.66 \text{ GWh/year}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solar PV Grid-Connected System — Block Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional block architecture of a grid-tied solar PV system, showing the energy and signal flow from irradiance capture to grid synchronization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight Irradiance G] --> B[PV Array Series Parallel Configuration]
    B --> C[DC DC Boost Converter with MPPT Controller]
    C --> D[DC Link Capacitor for Ripple Filtering]
    D --> E[Voltage Source Inverter VSI with PWM]
    E --> F[LCL Filter for Harmonic Suppression]
    F --> G[Step Up Transformer 415V to 11kV]
    G --> H[Point of Common Coupling PCC]
    H --> I[Utility Grid 50 Hz]
    E --> J[PLL Synchronization Unit]
    J --> I
    C --> K[MPPT Algorithm P and O or Incremental Conductance]
    K --> C
    I --> L[Grid Monitoring and Protection Relays]
    L --> H
```

## 4.2 Wind Energy Conversion System — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Kinetic Energy Source] --> B[Rotating Turbine Blades with Pitch Control]
    B --> C[Gearbox Mechanical Speed Increaser]
    C --> D[Generator PMSG or DFIG]
    D --> E[Generator Side Converter AC to DC]
    E --> F[DC Link Capacitor]
    F --> G[Grid Side Inverter DC to AC]
    G --> H[LC Filter]
    H --> I[Step Up Transformer]
    I --> J[Point of Common Coupling]
    J --> K[Utility Grid 50 Hz]
    B --> L[Anemometer and Wind Vane Sensors]
    L --> M[Pitch Controller and Yaw Motor]
    M --> B
    G --> N[PLL for Grid Synchronization]
    N --> J
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Solar vs. Wind Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOLAR [SOLAR PV SYSTEM]
        SA[PV Array] --> SB[MPPT Boost Converter]
        SB --> SC[DC Link]
        SC --> SD[Inverter VSI]
    end
    subgraph WIND [WIND TURBINE SYSTEM]
        WA[Turbine Rotor] --> WB[Gearbox or Direct Drive]
        WB --> WC[Generator]
        WC --> WD[Rectifier AC to DC]
        WD --> WE[DC Link]
        WE --> WF[Grid Side Inverter]
    end
    SD --> SX[Common AC Filter and Step Up Transformer]
    WF --> SX
    SX --> SY[Grid PCC]
```

## 4.4 Energy Conversion Chain Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Energy Source] --> B[Conversion Stage 1 Mechanical or Photonic]
    B --> C[Conversion Stage 2 Electrical Raw]
    C --> D[Power Conditioning Converter]
    D --> E[Filter and Transformer]
    E --> F[Grid or Load]
    A -.Environmental Factors.-> B
    D -.Regulatory Compliance IEEE IEC CEA.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematics for Board Exams:** Always start your 14-mark answer by drawing a clean, labeled block diagram, then explain **each block's function** in 2-3 sentences. Mention the relevant **standards (IEEE/IEC)** where applicable to fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

### **Question 1: Define Fill Factor of a Solar Cell. Mention its typical range.**

**Model Answer:**
The Fill Factor (FF) of a solar cell is the ratio of the maximum power delivered by the cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) and short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$). Mathematically:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

where $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$ are the voltage and current at the Maximum Power Point (MPP). The Fill Factor is a measure of the "squareness" of the I-V curve of the solar cell. Typical values of FF for commercial crystalline silicon cells range between **0.70 and 0.85**, with higher values indicating better cell quality and lower series resistance losses. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Question 2: What is the Betz Limit? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Betz Limit** is the theoretical maximum fraction of kinetic energy in a wind stream that a wind turbine can extract. Its value is:

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ (or 59.3\%)}$$

It is derived using the actuator disk theory by applying conservation of mass, momentum, and energy to an ideal inviscid flow through a turbine. Its significance lies in the fact that **no practical wind turbine, regardless of design, can convert more than 59.3 %** of the wind's kinetic energy into mechanical energy. Real-world turbines operate at power coefficients between **25 % and 45 %** due to aerodynamic, mechanical, and electrical losses. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

### **Question A (Choice 1):** Explain with a neat block diagram the components of a grid-connected Solar PV system. Discuss the major environmental factors affecting its performance. (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — Block Schematic and Functional Explanation (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A grid-connected solar PV system consists of the following major components, arranged in the sequence shown in the block diagram below:

**Block 1 — PV Array:**
The PV array is the heart of the system, comprising multiple PV modules connected in series (to achieve the desired DC voltage) and in parallel (to achieve the desired DC current). Each cell is a P-N junction diode that converts incident photons into electron-hole pairs. Under STC, a typical crystalline silicon cell produces an open-circuit voltage of about **0.6 V** and a short-circuit current density of about **35 mA/cm²**.

**Block 2 — DC-DC Boost Converter with MPPT:**
The PV array output voltage is variable and often low. A boost converter (step-up chopper) using an IGBT/MOSFET switch, inductor, diode, and capacitor raises this voltage to the inverter's DC-link level (typically 400 V to 800 V for residential systems). The MPPT controller continuously adjusts the duty cycle to operate the array at its Maximum Power Point. Common MPPT algorithms are **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** and **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)**.

**Block 3 — DC-AC Voltage Source Inverter (VSI):**
The VSI converts DC to 50 Hz AC using Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM) or Space Vector Modulation (SVM). IGBTs are the preferred switching devices in the 5–20 kHz range for medium-power systems.

**Block 4 — LCL Filter:**
The PWM switching generates high-frequency harmonics. An LCL filter (Inductor-Capacitor-Inductor) attenuates these to comply with **IEEE 519** harmonic standards (individual harmonic < 3 %, THD < 5 %).

**Block 5 — Step-up Transformer and PCC:**
A transformer steps up the inverter's low AC voltage (e.g., 415 V) to the grid's HT level (11 kV, 22 kV, or 33 kV). The **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** is the electrical interface with the utility grid, equipped with circuit breakers, anti-islanding protection, and revenue metering.

**Part (b) — Environmental Factors (7 Marks)**

The performance of a solar PV system is highly sensitive to environmental conditions:

1. **Solar Irradiance (G):** Power output is **directly proportional** to irradiance. On cloudy days, output may fall to 10–25 % of rated capacity.
2. **Cell Temperature:** Higher temperatures **reduce** the open-circuit voltage (~2 mV/°C per cell), decreasing efficiency by 0.4–0.5 %/°C above 25 °C. The current increases marginally, but voltage drop dominates.
3. **Dust and Soiling:** A dirty panel can lose **5 % to 30 %** of its output. Periodic cleaning is essential in arid regions like Rajasthan.
4. **Shading:** Partial shading of a module can cause **hotspot heating** and severe output reduction. Bypass diodes mitigate this.
5. **Humidity and Air Mass:** High humidity absorbs some solar spectrum. Higher air mass (AM) at sunrise/sunset reduces spectral intensity.
6. **Spectral Distribution:** PV cells respond differently to different wavelengths. Modern cells are tuned to the AM 1.5 standard spectrum.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write "more temperature = more power" because they confuse the effect on current vs. voltage. **Voltage decreases with temperature; current slightly increases; net power decreases.** Draw the I-V curve if asked, and clearly mark the MPP shift with temperature. Failing to mention **MPPT** or **Bypass Diodes** in the block diagram will cost 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2):** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS). Discuss the environmental factors and regulations governing wind power generation. (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Operation (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Wind Energy Conversion System converts the kinetic energy of wind into electrical energy through the following blocks:

**Block 1 — Rotor with Blades:**
A typical Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) has three blades of aerodynamic airfoil design. The blades capture wind kinetic energy, with the **Tip Speed Ratio (λ = ωR/v)** and **Blade Pitch Angle (β)** determining the power extraction. Modern blades are made of fiberglass-reinforced plastic or carbon fiber composite.

**Block 2 — Gearbox (Optional in Direct-Drive Systems):**
The rotor spins at 15–20 rpm, which is too low for direct generation. A gearbox steps this up to ~1500 rpm (for a 4-pole, 50 Hz machine). Direct-drive PMSG systems eliminate the gearbox for higher reliability.

**Block 3 — Generator:**
Three main types are used: (i) Squirrel Cage Induction Generator (SCIG) for fixed-speed turbines, (ii) Doubly-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG) for variable-speed partial-converter systems, and (iii) Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG) for full-converter variable-speed systems.

**Block 4 — Power Electronic Converters (AC-DC-AC):**
A generator-side converter (rectifier) and a grid-side converter (inverter) connected by a DC-link. The grid-side inverter controls active and reactive power, ensuring grid code compliance.

**Block 5 — Filter, Transformer, and Grid:**
Harmonic filtering, voltage step-up, and synchronization via PLL complete the system.

**Part (b) — Environmental Factors and Regulations (7 Marks)**

**Environmental Factors:**

1. **Wind Speed (v):** Power varies as the **cube of wind speed** ($P \propto v^3$). Doubling v increases power by 8 times.
2. **Air Density (ρ):** Higher altitude and higher temperature reduce ρ, reducing power output.
3. **Tower Height:** Wind speed increases with height per the power law: $v/v_0 = (h/h_0)^\alpha$ where α is the terrain roughness coefficient (1/7 for open terrain, 0.10 for smooth hard ground, 0.40 for forested areas).
4. **Turbulence and Wind Shear:** Cause fatigue loading, mechanical wear, and power quality issues (flicker).
5. **Wake Effects:** In wind farms, downstream turbines experience reduced wind speed due to upstream turbine wakes.

**Regulations and Standards:**

- **IEC 61400 (Part 1 to Part 27):** Comprehensive design, performance, and safety standards for wind turbines.
- **IEEE 1547:** Interconnection of distributed resources with the grid.
- **CEA (Technical Standards for Connectivity of Distributed Generation Resources) Regulations, 2013 (amended 2019):** Governs Indian grid integration.
- **MNRE Guidelines:** Indian Ministry of New and Renewable Energy policy framework.
- **CERC / SERC Tariff Regulations:** Determine feed-in tariffs and RPO compliance.
- **Indian Electricity Act, 2003:** Legal basis for renewable energy generation and sale.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently forget to mention the **Power Law for wind speed variation with height** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question in the KTU module test. Also, do not confuse **Type 1 (SCIG, fixed speed) with Type 3 (DFIG, variable speed) turbines** — clearly specify the type and its converter topology. Failing to mention **PLL synchronization** at the PCC will cost marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Solar and Wind**

- **Solar PV Block Sequence:** PV Array → MPPT Boost Converter → DC Link → Inverter (VSI) → LCL Filter → Step-up Transformer → PCC → Grid.
- **Wind WECS Block Sequence:** Rotor Blades → Gearbox (or Direct Drive) → Generator → Rectifier (for PMSG) → DC Link → Grid-side Inverter → Filter → Step-up Transformer → PCC → Grid.
- **Key Solar Formula:** $P_{pv} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta$; $P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$.
- **Key Wind Formula:** $P_{mech} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3} \cdot C_{p}$.
- **Betz Limit = 16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (59.3 %)** — maximum theoretical efficiency for any wind turbine.
- **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / v$ — controls where the turbine operates on its Cp-λ curve.
- **Wind Power Law:** $v(h) = v_0 \cdot (h/h_0)^\alpha$ — wind speed increases with tower height.
- **Temperature Effect:** Solar cell voltage **decreases** with temperature; current **slightly increases**; net efficiency **decreases** (~0.4 %/°C).
- **STC Conditions:** G = 1000 W/m², T = 25 °C, AM 1.5.
- **Critical Standards:** IEEE 1547, IEEE 519, IEC 61215, IEC 61400, CEA Regulations 2019, Indian Electricity Act 2003.
- **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF):** Solar in India ≈ 18–22 %; Wind in India ≈ 20–35 %.
- **Intermittency:** Both solar and wind are variable; hence, **energy storage, forecasting, and grid-friendly inverters (with LVRT/HVRT capability)** are essential.
- **Advantages (Solar):** No fuel cost, low maintenance, modular, long life (~25 years), no noise.
- **Disadvantages (Solar):** Intermittent, high initial cost, low energy density, requires large land area (~5 acres/MW), manufacturing involves hazardous chemicals.
- **Advantages (Wind):** High energy yield per area, no fuel cost, mature technology, can be offshore (higher and more consistent wind).
- **Disadvantages (Wind):** Intermittent, noise pollution, visual impact, bird/bat mortality, requires wind resource assessment, offshore installation is expensive.
- **Diagram Drawing Tip:** Always draw block diagrams **left to right**, label **each block**, mention the **signal/energy type** (DC, AC, mechanical, etc.), and indicate the **grid/load** at the rightmost end.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Storage Systems as Alternative Energy Sources - BESS, CESS, Thermal SS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Storage Systems as Alternative Energy Sources - BESS, CESS, Thermal SS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy Storage Systems (ESS) — The Big Picture

An **Energy Storage System (ESS)** in the context of power engineering is a technology or a set of technologies designed to **capture electrical (or thermal) energy produced at one time, store it for a duration, and dispatch it when required**. In the modern grid, ESS acts as a critical buffer that decouples generation from demand — a function that is indispensable when integrating intermittent renewable energy sources such as solar and wind.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Energy storage systems are physical or electrochemical devices that absorb energy when supply exceeds demand and release it when demand exceeds supply, thereby enabling **load leveling, peak shaving, frequency regulation, and renewable energy time-shifting** in the grid.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery Bucket" of the Grid

Think of an ESS as a **water tank on top of a building**. A water pump (the generator) fills the tank whenever water is cheap or abundant (off-peak hours or sunny midday). When the taps are opened during peak hours (mornings/evenings), gravity-fed water flows out. The tank ensures that the pump and the taps never have to operate at the exact same rate.

Similarly, an ESS:
- **Charges** during surplus generation (low price, high renewable output).
- **Stores** this energy in a form (chemical, mechanical, thermal, electrical).
- **Discharges** during deficit periods (peak price, low renewable output).

## 1.2 Classification of Bulk Electrical Energy Storage

The most widely accepted classification in power-system literature (and aligned with KTU Module 1) groups bulk ESS into four families:

| Family | Form of Storage | Maturity | Typical Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical (Pumped Hydro, **CAES**, Flywheel) | Kinetic / Potential | Mature (PHS), Commercial (CAES) | 10 MW – 3000 MW |
| Electrochemical (**BESS**, Flow batteries) | Chemical | Rapidly maturing | 1 kW – 400 MW |
| Thermal (**Thermal SS**) | Heat (sensible / latent) | Commercial | 1 kW – 500 MW |
| Electrical (Supercapacitors, SMES) | Field energy | Niche | kW – MW (short duration) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 1, the syllabus specifically focuses on **BESS, CESS, and Thermal SS** as the three alternative energy-source/storage families.

## 1.3 Battery Energy Storage System (BESS)

A **Battery Energy Storage System (BESS)** is an electrochemical ESS that stores energy in the form of chemical potential within one or more rechargeable battery cells and converts it back to electrical energy via reversible electrochemical reactions.

A typical grid-scale BESS is **not just a battery** — it is an integrated system comprising:

- **Battery modules/cells** (the electrochemical core, e.g., Li-ion, Lead-acid, NaS, Flow).
- **Power Conversion System (PCS)** — a bidirectional inverter that converts AC ↔ DC.
- **Battery Management System (BMS)** — monitors state-of-charge (SOC), state-of-health (SOH), temperature, and cell balancing.
- **Energy Management System (EMS)** — the supervisory controller that decides charge/discharge dispatch.
- **Thermal management** (liquid or air cooling) to maintain cell temperature.

> [!TIP]
> **Why BESS dominates KTU papers:** The global installed capacity of BESS grew from ~1 GW in 2015 to over **160 GW by 2024**, making it the fastest-growing ESS technology and a favorite exam topic.

## 1.4 Compressed Air Energy Storage System (CESS / CAES)

A **Compressed Air Energy Storage System (CESS or CAES)** is a large-scale mechanical ESS that stores energy by **compressing air and storing it in an underground cavern, abandoned mine, or high-pressure vessel**. When electricity is needed, the compressed air is released, heated (optionally with natural gas or stored heat), and expanded through a turbine to drive a generator.

Two principal architectures exist:
1. **Diabatic CAES** (e.g., Huntorf Germany 290 MW, McIntosh USA 110 MW) — heat of compression is wasted; natural gas is burned before expansion.
2. **Adiabatic / Isothermal CAES (A-CAES / I-CAES)** — the heat of compression is captured in a Thermal SS and reused during expansion, eliminating fossil-fuel use. This is the *pure storage* concept.

## 1.5 Thermal Energy Storage System (Thermal SS)

A **Thermal Energy Storage System** stores energy in the form of **heat (sensible, latent, or thermochemical)** in a storage medium for later conversion back to electricity or for direct thermal use. It is a critical companion to Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants and Combined Heat & Power (CHP) systems.

The three principal physical modes are:

- **Sensible Heat Storage (SHS):** Temperature change of a solid or liquid (e.g., molten salt, water, concrete, sand).
- **Latent Heat Storage (LHS):** Phase Change Material (PCM) at constant temperature (e.g., paraffin, salt hydrates).
- **Thermochemical Storage (TCS):** Reversible chemical reactions (e.g., CaO ⇌ Ca(OH)₂, metal hydrides).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway for KTU:** BESS, CESS, and Thermal SS together form the *bulk-electrical-and-thermal* storage triad that complements renewable generation, allowing the modern grid to remain stable, dispatchable, and economic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BESS — Operational Principle & Architecture

A BESS operates on the universal law of **Faraday's electrolysis in reverse**: during discharge, a spontaneous redox reaction releases electrons at the anode and consumes them at the cathode, producing DC current. During charge, an external voltage source forces the reaction in the reverse direction.

### Working Steps of a Grid-Connected BESS

1. **Charging mode (AC → DC → Chemical):**
   - Grid AC is rectified by the PCS into DC.
   - BMS regulates cell voltage and current to safe limits.
   - Electrochemical reactions store energy as chemical potential.
2. **Standby (Storage):**
   - Energy remains in the form of chemical bonds.
   - Self-discharge losses occur (small for Li-ion, larger for flow batteries).
3. **Discharging mode (Chemical → DC → AC):**
   - Reversed electrochemical reaction releases electrons.
   - DC is inverted by the PCS back to grid-frequency AC.
   - Transformer steps voltage up to transmission level.

### Why BESS is the "Workhorse" of Modern Grids

- **Round-trip efficiency** of Li-ion BESS: **85–95 %**.
- **Response time**: tens of **milliseconds** (fastest among bulk storage).
- **Modular scalability**: from 100 kWh (behind-the-meter) to 4 GWh (utility-scale).
- **Dual-use capability**: energy arbitrage + ancillary services (frequency, voltage).

> [!NOTE]
> **State of Charge (SOC)** is the KTU's most-tested BESS parameter:
> $$\text{SOC} = \frac{E_{\text{stored}}}{E_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$
> where $E_{\text{stored}}$ is the energy currently held in the battery and $E_{\text{rated}}$ is its nominal rated capacity (in kWh or MWh).

## 2.2 CESS — Thermodynamic Operating Principle

A CESS is best understood as a **closed-loop gas-turbine cycle with a "pressure reservoir" replacing the natural-gas fuel input**.

### Step-by-Step Operation of a Diabatic CAES Plant

1. **Compression phase (off-peak):**
   - Motor-driven compressor raises air from atmospheric pressure $P_1$ to high pressure $P_2$.
   - The compression work input is the *stored* energy.
2. **Storage phase:**
   - Compressed air is held in a cavern (typically 50–80 bar).
3. **Expansion phase (peak):**
   - Compressed air is released, mixed with natural gas, combusted, and expanded through a high-pressure turbine → low-pressure turbine → generator.
4. **Re-pressurization** of the cavern using residual pressure, ready for the next cycle.

### The Two Key Thermodynamic Processes

- **Compression (charging):** Closely follows an **adiabatic** or **polytropic** process, $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where $n \approx 1.4$ for diatomic air in a fast compressor.
- **Expansion (discharging):** Ideally isentropic ($n = \gamma = 1.4$) through the turbine.

## 2.3 Thermal SS — Modes and Heat-Transfer Mechanism

A Thermal SS uses a **storage medium** to absorb and release thermal energy. The governing equation depends on the mode.

### Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)

Energy is stored by raising the temperature of a material:
$$Q_{\text{SHS}} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$$
where $m$ is mass, $c_p$ is specific heat capacity, and $\Delta T$ is the temperature swing. Common media: molten salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃, $c_p \approx 1.55$ kJ/kg·K), water, packed-rock, concrete.

### Latent Heat Storage (LHS)

Energy is stored during a **phase change** (typically solid ↔ liquid) at nearly constant temperature:
$$Q_{\text{LHS}} = m \, L$$
where $L$ is the specific latent heat of fusion. PCMs enable very high energy density at a single temperature.

### Thermochemical Storage (TCS)

Energy drives a **reversible endothermic/exothermic reaction**:
$$Q_{\text{TCS}} = n \, \Delta H_r \, \eta_r$$
where $\Delta H_r$ is reaction enthalpy and $\eta_r$ is reaction extent.

### Combined Storage (SHS + LHS)

In practice (e.g., CSP molten-salt tanks with PCM augmentation):
$$Q_{\text{total}} = m \, c_{p,\text{solid}} (T_m - T_{\min}) + m \, L + m \, c_{p,\text{liquid}} (T_{\max} - T_m)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy stored in BESS (energy basis) | $E = P \cdot t$ | $P$: Power (kW or MW), $t$: duration (h) → $E$ in kWh or MWh | Sizing a BESS for a given load |
| 2 | BESS energy (charge basis) | $E = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot C_{\text{Ah}} \cdot N_{\text{cells}}$ | $V_{\text{nom}}$: nominal voltage (V), $C_{\text{Ah}}$: capacity (Ah) | Battery pack sizing |
| 3 | State of Charge | $\text{SOC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}}}{E_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless (%) | BMS reporting |
| 4 | Depth of Discharge | $\text{DOD} = 1 - \text{SOC}$ | Dimensionless | Cycle-life estimation |
| 5 | CESS — Isothermal compression work | $W_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = n R T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³, $T$ in K | Theoretical maximum storage |
| 6 | CESS — Polytropic compression work | $W_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{poly}}}{n_{\text{poly}}-1} \, P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n_{\text{poly}}-1}{n_{\text{poly}}}} - 1\right]$ | Same as above | Realistic compressor input |
| 7 | CESS — Energy density (volumetric) | $w_v = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{(\gamma - 1)}$ (adiabatic) | J/m³ | Comparing storage media |
| 8 | CESS — Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{\text{CAES}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{exp}}}{W_{\text{comp}}}$ | Typically 42–55 % (diabatic), 60–70 % (adiabatic) | Plant design |
| 9 | Thermal SS — Sensible heat | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $m$ in kg, $c_p$ in J/kg·K, $\Delta T$ in K | Molten-salt tank sizing |
| 10 | Thermal SS — Latent heat | $Q = m \, L$ | $L$ in J/kg | PCM sizing |
| 11 | Thermal SS — Storage capacity | $E_{\text{thermal}} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | in Wh or kWh | CSP with TES |
| 12 | Round-trip efficiency (general) | $\eta_{\text{RT}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless (%) | Comparing all ESS |
| 13 | Self-discharge rate | $\text{SD} = \dfrac{E_{\text{initial}} - E_{\text{after } t}}{t}$ | %/month or %/day | Long-term storage suitability |
| 14 | Energy density (gravimetric) | $\rho_g = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Wh/kg | Mobile/transport use |
| 15 | Power density | $\rho_p = \dfrac{P}{m}$ | W/kg | Burst-power capability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Always convert units consistently. A common KTU error is mixing minutes with hours or kWh with MJ ($1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Systems

- **BESS** — Hornsdale Power Reserve (Australia, 194 MW/194 MWh), Moss Landing (USA, 750 MW/3000 MWh), Kerala's KSEB pilots (10 MWh at Braemore).
- **CESS** — Huntorf (Germany, 290 MW — world's first, 1978), McIntosh (USA, 110 MW), Hydrostor (Australia, A-CAES pilot).
- **Thermal SS** — Andasol 1, 2, 3 (Spain, 50 MW solar + 7.5 h molten-salt storage), Crescent Dunes (USA, 110 MW with 10 h TES), Shouhang Dunhuang (China, 100 MW with 11 h TES).

These systems directly contribute to the KTU-relevant pillars of **grid stability, renewable integration, and decarbonization of Kerala's and India's power sector.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Energy Stored in a CESS Under Isothermal Compression

We start from the **first law of thermodynamics** for a closed system undergoing a reversible isothermal compression of an ideal gas.

The incremental work done **on** the gas during an infinitesimal volume change $dV$ at pressure $P$ is:
$$dW_{\text{on}} = -P \, dV$$

The negative sign appears because work is done *on* the gas when $dV$ is negative (compression).

For an ideal gas at constant temperature $T$:
$$PV = n R T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P = \dfrac{n R T}{V}$$

Substitute into the work integral:
$$W_{\text{on}} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{n R T}{V} \, dV$$

Because $T$ is constant, factor it out:
$$W_{\text{on}} = -n R T \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{dV}{V}$$

Integrate:
$$W_{\text{on}} = -n R T \big[ \ln V \big]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -n R T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

Apply the isothermal relation $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$:
$$W_{\text{on}} = n R T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

In terms of initial state $P_1, V_1$:
$$W_{\text{on}} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

This is the **theoretical energy stored** in a CESS under isothermal compression. Note that $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ is the **pressure ratio** $r_p$, so the equation can be written compactly as:
$$W_{\text{stored}} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r_p)$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Polytropic Compression Work in a Real CESS

In a real compressor, the process is neither isothermal nor fully adiabatic; it follows a **polytropic law**:
$$P V^{n} = \text{constant} = P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$$

The compression work for a polytropic process is:
$$W_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \, P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Starting from $dW = -P\,dV$ and the polytropic relation $P = P_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V}\right)^n$:
$$W = -P_1 V_1^{n} \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

Integrate $V^{-n}$:
$$\int V^{-n} \, dV = \dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n}$$

So:
$$W = -P_1 V_1^{n} \cdot \dfrac{V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}}{1-n} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{n}}{n-1} \left(V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}\right)$$

Using $V_1^{1-n} = V_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1}$ and the pressure ratio $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^n$:
$$W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left(r_p^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right)$$

**Limiting cases (KTU favorites):**
- $n = 1$ (isothermal) → $\displaystyle \lim_{n \to 1} W = P_1 V_1 \ln r_p$ ✓ (matches Section 3.1).
- $n = \gamma = 1.4$ (adiabatic) → $\displaystyle W_{\text{adiabatic}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right)$.

## 3.3 Derivation — Energy Stored in a Sensible Thermal SS

A Thermal SS tank of mass $m$ is heated from $T_1$ to $T_2$ by a fluid of specific heat $c_p$. The energy stored equals the enthalpy rise:
$$Q = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} m \, c_p(T) \, dT$$

If $c_p$ is constant (a good assumption for molten salt between 290 °C and 565 °C):
$$Q = m \, c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

For a **two-tank molten-salt system** (the most-examined KTU case), the cold tank at $T_c$ supplies salt that is heated in the receiver/charger to $T_h$, then stored in the hot tank:
$$E_{\text{TES}} = m_{\text{salt}} \, c_{p,\text{salt}} (T_h - T_c)$$

Substituting typical values ($c_p = 1.55$ kJ/kg·K, $T_h = 565$ °C, $T_c = 290$ °C):
$$E_{\text{TES}} = m_{\text{salt}} \times 1.55 \times (565 - 290) = 426.25 \, m_{\text{salt}} \text{ kJ}$$

So **1 kg of molten salt stores ~426 kJ of thermal energy**, equivalent to ~118 Wh — a useful number for back-of-envelope KTU problems.

## 3.4 Derivation — BESS Energy and Equivalent Round-Trip Efficiency

A battery pack has $N_s$ cells in series and $N_p$ in parallel. Each cell has nominal voltage $V_c$ and capacity $C_c$ (Ah).

**Total nominal voltage** (series addition of EMFs):
$$V_{\text{pack}} = N_s V_c$$

**Total capacity** (parallel addition of charges):
$$C_{\text{pack}} = N_p C_c$$

**Energy stored in pack:**
$$E_{\text{pack}} = V_{\text{pack}} \times C_{\text{pack}} = N_s V_c \cdot N_p C_c$$

**Round-trip efficiency** is the ratio of energy recovered during discharge to energy supplied during charge:
$$\eta_{\text{RT}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{discharge}}}{E_{\text{charge}}} = \dfrac{\int_0^{t_d} V_d(t) I_d(t) \, dt}{\int_0^{t_c} V_c(t) I_c(t) \, dt}$$

For constant-current operation (a common KTU assumption):
$$\eta_{\text{RT}} \approx \dfrac{V_{d,\text{avg}}}{V_{c,\text{avg}}}$$

because the discharge voltage is lower than the charge voltage, leading to inherent energy loss even before considering resistive ($I^2R$) dissipation.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A CESS plant compresses $V_1 = 50$ m³ of air at $P_1 = 1$ bar and $T = 300$ K isothermally to $P_2 = 80$ bar. Find (a) the energy stored, (b) the storage mass of air, and (c) the volumetric energy density.

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5$ Pa
- $P_2 = 80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $V_1 = 50$ m³
- $T = 300$ K
- $R_{\text{air}} = 287$ J/kg·K

**Part (a):** Energy stored (isothermal):
$$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$
$$W = 10^5 \times 50 \times \ln(80)$$
$$W = 5 \times 10^6 \times 4.382 = 2.191 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 21.91 \text{ MJ}$$

**Part (b):** Mass of air:
$$m = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R T} = \dfrac{10^5 \times 50}{287 \times 300} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^7}{86100} = 58.07 \text{ kg}$$

**Part (c):** Gravimetric and volumetric energy densities:
$$\rho_g = \dfrac{W}{m} = \dfrac{2.191 \times 10^7}{58.07} = 3.77 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg} = 377 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$w_v = \dfrac{W}{V_1} = \dfrac{2.191 \times 10^7}{50} = 4.38 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m³} = 438 \text{ kJ/m³}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer Style:** Always state the formula, substitute values with units, and box the final numerical answer. Examiners explicitly award a step-mark for the formula, a step-mark for substitution, and a step-mark for the correct final answer.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
**Define Battery Energy Storage System (BESS). List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) is an electrochemical device that stores electrical energy in chemical form and releases it as DC, which is then converted to AC via a power conversion system (PCS) for grid use. Its main components are:
1. **Battery modules** — the electrochemical cells (Li-ion, Lead-acid, etc.) where energy is stored (1 mark).
2. **Power Conversion System (PCS)** — a bidirectional inverter-rectifier enabling AC ↔ DC conversion (1 mark).
3. **Battery Management System (BMS) and Energy Management System (EMS)** — for monitoring SOC, SOH, protection, and dispatch optimization (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Set A]
**Distinguish between Sensible Heat Storage (SHS) and Latent Heat Storage (LHS) in a Thermal SS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | SHS | LHS |
|---|---|---|
| Storage mechanism | Temperature change of a medium | Phase change (solid ↔ liquid) at constant temperature |
| Governing equation | $Q = m\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | $Q = m\,L$ |
| Storage density | Moderate (depends on $c_p$ and $\Delta T$) | High (at the phase-change temperature) |
| Examples | Molten salt, water, concrete, sand | Paraffin, salt hydrates, ice (1 mark for table). |

SHS uses temperature swing in a single phase; LHS exploits the latent heat of a phase change, offering higher density and near-isothermal operation (1 mark for clear explanation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts**, typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. KTU provides an **internal choice** between two complete questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of a Compressed Air Energy Storage System (CESS) with a neat block diagram. Compare diabatic and adiabatic CAES.

**Model Solution:**

**Working principle of CESS (4 marks):**
A CESS stores energy in the form of **high-pressure compressed air** in an underground cavern or vessel. During off-peak hours, an electric motor drives a multi-stage compressor that draws in ambient air and compresses it from $P_1$ to $P_2$ (typically 50–80 bar). The compressed air is stored in a sealed geological formation (salt cavern, depleted gas field, or aquifier).

During peak hours, the compressed air is released through throttle valves, mixed with natural gas in a combustion chamber (diabatic type), and expanded through a high-pressure turbine followed by a low-pressure turbine coupled to a synchronous generator. The generator feeds the grid.

The CESS is essentially a **gas-turbine plant where the fuel's chemical energy is replaced by the stored compressed-air's mechanical energy**.

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.3) — 2 marks for the diagram and 1 mark for labeling.

**Comparison: Diabatic vs Adiabatic CAES** (1 mark):

| Parameter | Diabatic CAES | Adiabatic CAES |
|---|---|---|
| Heat of compression | Wasted (cooled) | Stored in a Thermal SS |
| Fuel needed at discharge | Yes (natural gas) | No (uses stored heat) |
| Efficiency | 42–55 % | 60–70 % (target) |
| CO₂ emissions | Yes | Near zero |

#### (b) [7 Marks] A CESS stores 60 m³ of air at 1 bar and 290 K isothermally compressed to 70 bar. Calculate the energy stored, the mass of air, and the equivalent electrical energy if round-trip efficiency is 50 %.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P_1 = 1 \times 10^5$ Pa, $P_2 = 70 \times 10^5$ Pa, $V_1 = 60$ m³, $T = 290$ K, $R = 287$ J/kg·K, $\eta_{\text{RT}} = 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Mass of air** (1 mark):
$$m = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R T} = \dfrac{10^5 \times 60}{287 \times 290} = \dfrac{6 \times 10^6}{83230} = 72.09 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored (isothermal work)** (3 marks):
$$W_{\text{stored}} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$
$$W_{\text{stored}} = 10^5 \times 60 \times \ln(70)$$
$$W_{\text{stored}} = 6 \times 10^6 \times 4.2485 = 2.549 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 25.49 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent electrical energy output** (2 marks):
$$E_{\text{out}} = \eta_{\text{RT}} \times W_{\text{stored}} = 0.5 \times 25.49 = 12.75 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh for clarity** (1 mark):
$$E_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{12.75 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 3.54 \text{ kWh}$$

**Result:** The CESS can store $\sim$25.49 MJ of mechanical energy and deliver $\sim$3.54 kWh of electrical energy per cycle.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Set B]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the construction and working of a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) with a functional block diagram. Discuss the role of the Power Conversion System (PCS) and Battery Management System (BMS).

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of a BESS (3 marks):**
A grid-scale BESS is an integrated system comprising:
- **Battery modules** — racks of cells (commonly LiFePO₄ or NMC Li-ion), each module with a rated voltage (e.g., 48 V, 100 V) and capacity (e.g., 100 Ah).
- **Power Conversion System (PCS)** — a bidirectional voltage-source converter (VSC) using IGBTs/SiC-MOSFETs, including filters and grid-side LCL filter.
- **Battery Management System (BMS)** — per-module electronics that monitor cell voltage, current, temperature, balance cells, and provide protection.
- **Energy Management System (EMS)** — a plant-level controller that decides charge/discharge scheduling.
- **Thermal Management System** — liquid cooling or HVAC to maintain cells at 15–35 °C.
- **Containerised housing** with fire suppression, gas venting, and SCADA.

**Working (2 marks):**
During charging, the PCS rectifies grid AC to DC and regulates current into the battery via BMS-controlled buck/boost action. The cells undergo a reduction at the cathode and oxidation at the anode, storing energy as chemical potential. During discharge, the reverse electrochemical reaction drives electrons through the external circuit; the PCS inverts the DC to grid-synchronized AC (often with a step-up transformer).

**Role of PCS and BMS (2 marks):**
- The **PCS** enables bidirectional power flow, provides reactive-power support (Q), low-voltage ride-through (LVRT), and grid-synchronization (PLL).
- The **BMS** ensures cell-level safety (over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, over-temperature), state-of-charge estimation (Coulomb counting + Kalman filter), and cell balancing (passive or active) — extending cycle life.

(Refer to Section 4.2 for the block diagram — 1 mark.)

#### (b) [7 Marks] A Thermal SS uses 5000 kg of molten salt (specific heat 1.55 kJ/kg·K) heated from 290 °C to 565 °C. Find the thermal energy stored. If this energy is used in a steam Rankine cycle with 35 % efficiency, calculate the electrical energy generated.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 5000$ kg, $c_p = 1.55$ kJ/kg·K, $T_1 = 290$ °C, $T_2 = 565$ °C, $\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = 0.35$.

**Step 1 — Temperature difference** (1 mark):
$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 565 - 290 = 275 \text{ K (or °C)}$$

**Step 2 — Thermal energy stored** (2 marks):
$$Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$$
$$Q = 5000 \times 1.55 \times 275 = 2.131 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ} = 2.131 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh for intuition** (1 mark):
$$Q = \dfrac{2.131 \times 10^6 \times 10^3}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 591.94 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Electrical energy output** (2 marks):
$$E_{\text{elec}} = \eta_{\text{Rankine}} \times Q = 0.35 \times 591.94 = 207.18 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Power if discharged over 4 hours** (1 mark):
$$P = \dfrac{E_{\text{elec}}}{t} = \dfrac{207.18}{4} = 51.8 \text{ kW (continuous)}$$

**Result:** The Thermal SS can deliver $\sim$207 kWh of electrical energy, sufficient to power 50 average Kerala households for 4 hours.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Pattern):**
> 1. **Unit errors** — Forgetting to convert kJ to J, or bar to Pa, before substitution. (–1 mark per error)
> 2. **Mixing isothermal and adiabatic formulas** — Many students use $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ for adiabatic processes. Always check whether the problem says *isothermal* or *adiabatic* or *polytropic*. (–2 marks)
> 3. **BESS answers that omit BMS/PCS** — A BESS without PCS and BMS is *not* a BESS. Examiners specifically look for these in the block diagram. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Thermal SS answers that confuse sensible and latent heat** — A two-part answer requiring *both* SHS and LHS will be penalized if only one mode is explained. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting to state assumptions** — In derivation problems, always state "assuming isothermal/ideal gas/polytropic with $n = 1.4$" before substituting. (–1 mark)
> 6. **No block diagram in 7-mark theory questions** — KTU explicitly allocates 1–2 marks for a *neat, labeled* block diagram. Skipping it guarantees a deduction.
> 7. **Round-trip efficiency not stated** — In comparison questions, the round-trip efficiency is the most-tested KTU metric; always quote a numerical range.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BESS** is the fastest-responding, highest-efficiency, most modular ESS — based on **electrochemical** cells, requiring PCS + BMS + EMS. Typical **Li-ion η = 85–95 %**.
- **CESS / CAES** is a **mechanical** bulk ESS based on **compressed air in a cavern**; isothermal work = $P_1 V_1 \ln(r_p)$, adiabatic work = $\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 (r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1)$. **Diabatic needs gas; adiabatic is fossil-free.**
- **Thermal SS** stores **heat** in a medium — sensible ($Q = m c_p \Delta T$), latent ($Q = m L$), or thermochemical. Most common: **two-tank molten-salt storage** at 290–565 °C.
- All three ESS support **renewable integration, load leveling, peak shaving, and ancillary services** in modern grids.
- **Round-trip efficiency** is the KTU's key comparative metric: BESS > Thermal SS (with Rankine) > CESS.
- **Response time** order: BESS (ms) > CESS/Thermal SS (minutes).
- **Storage duration** order: Thermal SS / CESS (8–24 h) > BESS (1–4 h).
- **Always** state the formula → substitute with units → compute the numerical answer in KTU problems.
- **Always** include a neat, labeled block diagram in 7-mark theory questions.
- **Remember the unit conversions**: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa; °C differences = K differences.
