# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Load Curve (Daily Load Curve)

**Formal Definition:** A **Load Curve** is a graphical plot showing the variation of electrical load on a power station versus time (usually over a 24-hour period). The area under the load curve represents the total energy generated (in kWh) by the station during that period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Load curves are fundamental in power system planning. The Y-axis is **Load in kW or MW**, and the X-axis is **Time in hours (0 to 24)**. The **peak (crest) of the curve** is the **Maximum Demand**.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Approach
Imagine you are filling a water tank throughout the day at varying flow rates. If you plot the *flow rate* (litres/hour) against *time*, the area under the curve is the **total water filled**. Similarly, in a power station, the **area under the load curve = Total Energy Generated (kWh)**.

---

## 1.2 The Load Duration Curve (LDC)

**Formal Definition:** A **Load Duration Curve (LDC)** is obtained by rearranging the load elements of a daily load curve in **descending order of magnitude**. The Y-axis shows load values, while the X-axis shows the **number of hours** that particular load persists (instead of the chronological time of occurrence).

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — "Sorting the Peaks"
Think of a class test where 30 students score different marks. The load curve is like a chart of marks *in the order the students appear*. The LDC is like a *leaderboard sorted from highest to lowest*. The highest load (peak) is on the **left**, and the smallest load is on the **right**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The LDC always lies **on or below** the load curve because chronological sequence is removed. Both curves enclose the **same area = Total Energy**.

---

## 1.3 The Six Performance Factors

| # | Factor | Simple Intuition |
|---|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Load Factor** | How *flat* is the demand curve? A flat curve is good (e.g., industrial load running 24/7). |
| 2 | **Diversity Factor** | Not all consumers use peak load *at the same time* — this captures that "spreading". |
| 3 | **Demand Factor** | What fraction of the *connected* load is *actually demanded* simultaneously. |
| 4 | **Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)** | How *hard* is the plant working compared to its full installed capability? |
| 5 | **Plant Use Factor (PUF)** | How intensively is the plant used *during the hours it is in operation*? |
| 6 | **Reserve Capacity** | The safety margin installed beyond the maximum demand. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Load Factor (LF) — Operational Insight

The **Load Factor** is a measure of the **flatness** of the load curve. A load factor of 1.0 means constant demand 24/7 (rare; only in continuous process industries). A low load factor means demand is highly variable, leading to poor asset utilization.

**Operational Logic:**
- High LF → Power station equipment operates near its rated capacity for longer periods → **Higher efficiency**.
- Low LF → Equipment sits idle for long durations → **Lower economic return**.

## 2.2 Diversity Factor (DF) — Engineering Utility

**Diversity Factor = Sum of Individual Maxima / Coincident Maximum.**

The diversity factor is **always ≥ 1.0** because individual peaks do NOT occur simultaneously in most cases. It allows the power utility to **install a smaller capacity** than the sum of all individual consumer peaks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Trick:** The Diversity Factor uses the **sum of individual maximum demands of consumers**, while the **Demand Factor** uses the **connected load of a single consumer**. Do NOT confuse them in numericals.

## 2.3 Demand Factor (DF) — Per-Consumer View

The Demand Factor is always **less than or equal to 1.0** because the **maximum demand** of a consumer is always less than or equal to its **connected load** (not all connected appliances run simultaneously, e.g., a geyser + washing machine + AC rarely all run at once in a household).

## 2.4 Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)

The **PCF** indicates how much energy is *actually* generated compared to the **maximum possible** generation if the plant ran at its full installed capacity for the entire period.

## 2.5 Plant Use Factor (PUF)

The **PUF** only counts the hours the plant was **in service** (not the entire day/year). It is **always greater than or equal to PCF** because the denominator (hours of use) is **smaller**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule:** $PCF \le PUF$, always. The **denominator of PCF is larger** (full period) compared to PUF (only hours of operation).

---

## 📋 KTU FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Range | Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | Average Load | $L_{avg} = \dfrac{\text{Energy generated in a period}}{\text{Number of hours in the period}}$ | $0 < L_{avg} \le L_{max}$ | kW |
| 2 | **Load Factor (LF)** | $LF = \dfrac{L_{avg}}{L_{max}}$ | $0 < LF \le 1$ | per unit (pu) or % |
| 3 | **Diversity Factor (DF)** | $DF = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Individual Max Demands})}{L_{max}(\text{on station})}$ | $DF \ge 1$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Demand Factor** | $DF_{dmd} = \dfrac{L_{max}(\text{consumer})}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | $0 < DF_{dmd} \le 1$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)** | $PCF = \dfrac{\text{Energy actually generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times T_{period}}$ | $0 < PCF \le 1$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Plant Use Factor (PUF)** | $PUF = \dfrac{\text{Energy actually generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times T_{use}}$ | $PUF \ge PCF$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Reserve Capacity | $RC = \text{Plant Capacity} - L_{max}$ | $RC \ge 0$ | kW or MW |
| 8 | Units Generated (Area under LDC) | $E = \int_{0}^{T} L(t) \, dt$ | — | kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention in KTU:** Plant capacity is given in **MW**, time in **hours (h)**, and energy in **MWh or million units (MU)**. $1 \, MU = 10^6 \, kWh = 10^3 \, MWh$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Problem Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy from Load Curve

Consider a load curve $L(t)$ plotted from $t = 0$ to $t = T$ hours.

The instantaneous power at any time $t$ is $L(t)$ (in kW). The infinitesimal energy in time $dt$ is:

$$dE = L(t) \cdot dt$$

Total energy generated over period $T$ is found by integration:

$$E = \int_{0}^{T} L(t) \, dt$$

For a **discrete (stepped) load curve** with loads $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n$ lasting for $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$ hours:

$$E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \cdot t_i \quad \text{[kWh]}$$

The **Average Load** is then:

$$L_{avg} = \dfrac{E}{T} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i}$$

And the **Load Factor** becomes:

$$LF = \dfrac{L_{avg}}{L_{max}} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \cdot t_i}{L_{max} \cdot \sum t_i}$$

---

## 3.2 Solved Numerical Problem #1 (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem Statement:** The daily load on a thermal power station is given below. Calculate (a) Average Load, (b) Load Factor, (c) Total Energy generated per day, and (d) Draw the load curve and the load duration curve.

| Time (hrs) | 0–6 | 6–10 | 10–12 | 12–16 | 16–20 | 20–22 | 22–24 |
|------------|-----|------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Load (MW)  | 20  | 50   | 80    | 100   | 60    | 40    | 20    |

### Step 1: Identify Maximum Demand

The peak of the load curve is the **Maximum Demand**.

$$L_{max} = 100 \text{ MW (occurring between 12:00 and 16:00 hrs)}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Energy Generated in Each Time Block

Using $E_i = L_i \times t_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= 20 \text{ MW} \times 6 \text{ h} = 120 \text{ MWh} \\
E_2 &= 50 \text{ MW} \times 4 \text{ h} = 200 \text{ MWh} \\
E_3 &= 80 \text{ MW} \times 2 \text{ h} = 160 \text{ MWh} \\
E_4 &= 100 \text{ MW} \times 4 \text{ h} = 400 \text{ MWh} \\
E_5 &= 60 \text{ MW} \times 4 \text{ h} = 240 \text{ MWh} \\
E_6 &= 40 \text{ MW} \times 2 \text{ h} = 80 \text{ MWh} \\
E_7 &= 20 \text{ MW} \times 2 \text{ h} = 40 \text{ MWh}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Total Energy Generated

$$E_{total} = 120 + 200 + 160 + 400 + 240 + 80 + 40 = 1240 \text{ MWh}$$

### Step 4: Average Load

$$L_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{\text{Total Time}} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ MWh}}{24 \text{ h}} = 51.667 \text{ MW}$$

### Step 5: Load Factor

$$LF = \dfrac{L_{avg}}{L_{max}} = \dfrac{51.667}{100} = 0.5167 \text{ pu} = 51.67\%$$

### ✅ Final Answers (Problem 1)
- **(a)** Average Load = **51.67 MW**
- **(b)** Load Factor = **0.5167 (51.67%)**
- **(c)** Energy generated per day = **1240 MWh = 1.24 Million Units**

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem #2 (Diversity & Demand Factor)

> **Problem Statement:** A power station has the following four consumers with their individual maximum demands and connected loads. The maximum demand on the station is recorded as 1500 kW. Calculate the **(a) Diversity Factor** and **(b) the Demand Factor of each consumer**.

| Consumer | Max Demand (kW) | Connected Load (kW) |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| A        | 400             | 600                 |
| B        | 500             | 800                 |
| C        | 300             | 500                 |
| D        | 600             | 900                 |

### Step 1: Sum of Individual Maxima

$$\sum L_{max,i} = 400 + 500 + 300 + 600 = 1800 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2: Diversity Factor

$$DF = \dfrac{\sum L_{max,i}}{L_{max}(\text{station})} = \dfrac{1800}{1500} = 1.20$$

### Step 3: Demand Factor of Each Consumer

Using $DF_{consumer} = \dfrac{L_{max}(\text{consumer})}{\text{Connected Load}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
DF_A &= \dfrac{400}{600} = 0.667 \\
DF_B &= \dfrac{500}{800} = 0.625 \\
DF_C &= \dfrac{300}{500} = 0.600 \\
DF_D &= \dfrac{600}{900} = 0.667
\end{aligned}$$

### ✅ Final Answers (Problem 2)
- **(a)** Diversity Factor = **1.20**
- **(b)** Demand Factors: $A = 0.667$, $B = 0.625$, $C = 0.600$, $D = 0.667$

---

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Problem #3 (Plant Capacity Factor & Plant Use Factor)

> **Problem Statement:** A thermal power station has an installed capacity of 200 MW. During a year (8760 hours), the station generated 1100 Million Units (MU). The plant was actually in operation for 7000 hours during the year. Calculate (a) Plant Capacity Factor, (b) Plant Use Factor, and (c) Average Load on the station.

### Step 1: Convert Energy to Consistent Units

$$E_{gen} = 1100 \text{ MU} = 1100 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh} = 1.1 \times 10^6 \text{ MWh}$$

### Step 2: Plant Capacity Factor

$$PCF = \dfrac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times T_{period}}$$

$$PCF = \dfrac{1.1 \times 10^6 \text{ MWh}}{200 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{1.1 \times 10^6}{1.752 \times 10^6} = 0.6279$$

$$PCF = 62.79\%$$

### Step 3: Plant Use Factor

$$PUF = \dfrac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times T_{use}}$$

$$PUF = \dfrac{1.1 \times 10^6 \text{ MWh}}{200 \text{ MW} \times 7000 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{1.1 \times 10^6}{1.4 \times 10^6} = 0.7857$$

$$PUF = 78.57\%$$

### Step 4: Average Load on the Station

$$L_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{gen}}{T_{period}} = \dfrac{1.1 \times 10^6 \text{ MWh}}{8760 \text{ h}} = 125.57 \text{ MW}$$

### ✅ Final Answers (Problem 3)
- **(a)** Plant Capacity Factor = **0.6279 (62.79%)**
- **(b)** Plant Use Factor = **0.7857 (78.57%)**
- **(c)** Average Load = **125.57 MW**

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (Golden Rule):** $PUF = 78.57\% \ge PCF = 62.79\%$ ✅ Confirmed.

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Problem #4 (Combined KTU Pattern)

> **Problem Statement:** A generating station has a maximum demand of 80 MW. The annual load factor is 0.50 and the plant capacity factor is 0.40. Find (a) Average Load, (b) Energy generated per year, (c) Plant capacity installed, (d) Hours of actual use of the plant in a year (Plant Use Factor).

### Step 1: Average Load from Load Factor

$$L_{avg} = LF \times L_{max} = 0.50 \times 80 = 40 \text{ MW}$$

### Step 2: Energy Generated per Year

$$E = L_{avg} \times T = 40 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 3,50,400 \text{ MWh} = 350.4 \text{ MU}$$

### Step 3: Plant Capacity from PCF

$$PCF = \dfrac{E}{\text{Capacity} \times T} \implies \text{Capacity} = \dfrac{E}{PCF \times T}$$

$$\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{3,50,400}{0.40 \times 8760} = \dfrac{3,50,400}{3504} = 100 \text{ MW}$$

### Step 4: Plant Use Factor (Compute $T_{use}$)

Since $PUF$ was not directly given, we deduce it from PCF and the ratio of hours.

$$PUF = \dfrac{E}{\text{Capacity} \times T_{use}}$$

$$T_{use} = \dfrac{E}{\text{Capacity} \times PUF}$$

But the problem does not give PUF, only asks us to find hours of use. From the relation between PCF and PUF:

$$PUF = \dfrac{PCF \times T_{period}}{T_{use}}$$

Without PUF, we can find $T_{use}$ if we assume a reasonable PUF or note: typical $PUF \ge PCF$. Let us assume the question intends the **reserve capacity = 20 MW** (Capacity – Max Demand) and find $PUF$ using another piece.

> **Reinterpretation:** If the plant ran for the full year (8760 h) — but at reduced loading — we get a direct PCF. The "Hours of Use" definition:

$$T_{use} = \dfrac{E}{\text{Capacity}} = \dfrac{3,50,400 \text{ MWh}}{100 \text{ MW}} = 3504 \text{ h}$$

This is the number of hours the plant would have to run at **full installed capacity** to produce the energy it actually did. Hence:

$$PUF = 1.0 \text{ (full hours-of-use interpretation)}$$

### ✅ Final Answers (Problem 4)
- **(a)** Average Load = **40 MW**
- **(b)** Energy generated = **350.4 MU**
- **(c)** Installed Plant Capacity = **100 MW**
- **(d)** Hours of Use at full capacity = **3504 h/year**

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship $T_{use} = E / \text{Capacity}$ is the **hours of equivalent full-load operation** — a key concept often asked in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Load Curve vs. Load Duration Curve — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph DAILY_LOAD_CURVE["DAILY LOAD CURVE - Chronological"]
        direction LR
        timeA["0:00 to 6:00 -- 20 MW"] --> timeB["6:00 to 10:00 -- 50 MW"]
        timeB --> timeC["10:00 to 12:00 -- 80 MW"]
        timeC --> timeD["12:00 to 16:00 -- 100 MW PEAK"]
        timeD --> timeE["16:00 to 20:00 -- 60 MW"]
        timeE --> timeF["20:00 to 22:00 -- 40 MW"]
        timeF --> timeG["22:00 to 24:00 -- 20 MW"]
    end
    subgraph LDC_DESC["LOAD DURATION CURVE - Sorted Descending"]
        direction LR
        sortA["0 to 4 h -- 100 MW"] --> sortB["4 to 6 h -- 80 MW"]
        sortB --> sortC["6 to 14 h -- 60 MW"]
        sortC --> sortD["14 to 18 h -- 50 MW"]
        sortD --> sortE["18 to 20 h -- 40 MW"]
        sortE --> sortF["20 to 24 h -- 20 MW"]
    end
    timeD ==> sortA
    timeC ==> sortB
    timeE ==> sortC
    timeB ==> sortD
    timeF ==> sortE
    timeA ==> sortF
    timeG ==> sortF
```

> [!NOTE]
> Both curves enclose **identical area = 1240 MWh**. The LDC always lies **on or below** the daily load curve.

---

## 4.2 Performance Factors — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Connected Load of Consumer] --> B[Maximum Demand]
    B --> C[Demand Factor = Max Demand / Connected Load]
    A2[Sum of Individual Maxima] --> D[Diversity Factor]
    B2[Max Demand on Station] --> D
    E[Energy Generated per period T] --> F[Average Load = E / T]
    F --> G[Load Factor = Avg Load / Max Load]
    E --> H[Plant Capacity Factor PCF]
    I[Installed Plant Capacity] --> H
    J[Time period T] --> H
    E --> K[Plant Use Factor PUF]
    I --> K
    L[Time of actual use Tu] --> K
    H -.PUF >= PCF.-> K
    I --> M[Reserve Capacity = Plant Cap - Max Demand]
    B2 --> M
```

---

## 4.3 Typical 24-Hour Load Curve (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X["Time (hours)"] ~~~ Y["Load (MW)"]
    X1[0 hr] --> X2[6 hr]
    X2 --> X3[12 hr]
    X3 --> X4[18 hr]
    X4 --> X5[24 hr]
    Y1[20] --> Y2[50]
    Y2 --> Y3[80]
    Y3 --> Y4[100 PEAK]
    Y4 --> Y5[60]
    Y5 --> Y6[40]
    Y6 --> Y7[20]
    X1 -.paired.-> Y1
    X2 -.paired.-> Y3
    X3 -.paired.-> Y4
    X4 -.paired.-> Y5
    X5 -.paired.-> Y7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Define Load Curve and Load Duration Curve. List any three differences between them.

**Model Answer:**

**Load Curve:** It is a plot of the electrical load on a power station versus time (typically 24 hours). The area under this curve gives the total energy generated.

**Load Duration Curve (LDC):** It is the rearrangement of the load elements of the load curve in descending order of magnitude, plotted against the duration of time each load persists.

**Differences:**

| # | Daily Load Curve | Load Duration Curve |
|---|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Plotted in chronological order of time | Plotted in descending order of load |
| 2 | Time sequence is preserved | Time sequence is removed |
| 3 | LDC always lies on or below the load curve | LDC is used for capacity planning and load grouping |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Define Diversity Factor. Why is it always greater than unity?

**Model Answer:**

**Diversity Factor (DF)** is the ratio of the **sum of individual maximum demands** of all the consumers supplied by a power station to the **maximum demand on the station itself**.

$$DF = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Individual Max Demands})}{L_{max}(\text{station})}$$

**Why DF ≥ 1:** Individual maximum demands of consumers occur at **different times of the day** (e.g., domestic peak in the evening, industrial peak in the daytime). Hence, the **sum of individual maxima is always ≥ the coincident maximum on the station**, making DF ≥ 1.

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Board Pattern 1**

> **A 100 MW power station supplies a daily load cycle as follows:**
> 
> | Time (h) | 0–6 | 6–9 | 9–12 | 12–15 | 15–18 | 18–21 | 21–24 |
> |----------|-----|-----|------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
> | Load (MW)| 40  | 70  | 100  | 80    | 60    | 90    | 50    |
> 
> **(a)** Draw the load curve and the load duration curve. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the (i) Average Load, (ii) Load Factor, (iii) Reserve Capacity of the plant, and (iv) Plant Capacity Factor if the plant generated 250 MU in a year. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Solution:**

**(a) Load Curve & LDC Diagram:**

```
Load (MW)
100 |          ____________
 90 |         /            \____
 80 |        /                  \____
 70 |       /                        \____
 60 |      /                              \____
 50 |   __/                                    \____
 40 |  /                                          \____
    +-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    0     3     6     9    12    15    18    21    24  Hours
    DAILY LOAD CURVE
```

The LDC is obtained by sorting the load values in descending order: 100, 90, 80, 70, 60, 50, 40 MW with cumulative durations of 3 h, 3 h, 3 h, 3 h, 3 h, 3 h, 6 h.

**(b) Calculations:**

**(i) Average Load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= (40 \times 6) + (70 \times 3) + (100 \times 3) + (80 \times 3) + (60 \times 3) + (90 \times 3) + (50 \times 3) \\
E &= 240 + 210 + 300 + 240 + 180 + 270 + 150 = 1590 \text{ MWh}
\end{aligned}$$

$$L_{avg} = \dfrac{1590}{24} = 66.25 \text{ MW}$$

**(ii) Load Factor:**

$$LF = \dfrac{L_{avg}}{L_{max}} = \dfrac{66.25}{100} = 0.6625 = 66.25\%$$

**(iii) Reserve Capacity:**

$$RC = \text{Plant Capacity} - L_{max} = 100 - 100 = 0 \text{ MW}$$

> Since maximum demand = plant capacity, there is **no reserve capacity**. In real-world practice, the plant would have been designed with reserve (e.g., 120 MW), but as per the problem data, $RC = 0$.

**(iv) Plant Capacity Factor:**

$$PCF = \dfrac{250 \text{ MU} \times 10^6}{100 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^8}{8.76 \times 10^5} = 0.2854 = 28.54\%$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU Board Pattern 2 (ALTERNATIVE)**

> **A power station has four consumers A, B, C, D with connected loads of 1500 kW, 1200 kW, 800 kW, and 2000 kW respectively. Their individual maximum demands are 1000 kW, 800 kW, 500 kW, and 1200 kW respectively. The maximum demand on the station is 3000 kW. The plant generated 18 MU in a month of 30 days.**
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the (i) Diversity Factor, (ii) Average Demand Factor. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** If the installed capacity is 5 MW and the plant was actually in use for 20 days × 24 h = 480 h in that month, calculate (i) Plant Capacity Factor, (ii) Plant Use Factor. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) Solution:**

**(i) Diversity Factor:**

$$\sum L_{max,i} = 1000 + 800 + 500 + 1200 = 3500 \text{ kW}$$

$$DF = \dfrac{3500}{3000} = 1.167$$

**(ii) Average Demand Factor:**

$$\text{Total Connected Load} = 1500 + 1200 + 800 + 2000 = 5500 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{Avg DF} = \dfrac{\sum L_{max,i}}{\text{Total Connected Load}} = \dfrac{3500}{5500} = 0.6364$$

**(b) Part (b) Solution:**

Energy in consistent units: $18 \text{ MU} = 18{,}000 \text{ MWh}$.

**(i) Plant Capacity Factor (PCF):**

Total period $T = 30 \text{ days} \times 24 = 720 \text{ h}$.

$$PCF = \dfrac{18{,}000 \text{ MWh}}{5 \text{ MW} \times 720 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{3600} = 5.0$$

> **⚠ Examiner's Note:** A value of PCF > 1 is physically impossible for a single month. This means the **load factor on installed capacity is very high**, OR the problem expects the student to recognize the **plant capacity is undersized**. The mathematically computed value is 5.0 (or 500%).

**(ii) Plant Use Factor (PUF):**

$$PUF = \dfrac{18{,}000}{5 \times 480} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{2400} = 7.5$$

> **Interpretation:** The plant had to operate at **7.5× its rated capacity** for the 480 h it was in use, which physically implies the problem is hypothetical, but it tests the student's arithmetic.

---

## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often mix kW, MW, kWh, MWh, MU. Always convert: $1 \, MU = 10^3 \, MWh = 10^6 \, kWh$. The examiner deducts **1–2 marks** for sloppy unit conversions.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Diversity Factor with Demand Factor:**
>    - **Diversity Factor** = Sum of individual Maxima / Max on Station (≥ 1)
>    - **Demand Factor** = Max Demand of consumer / Connected Load (≤ 1)
>    - Forgetting to check whether the value is **≥ 1 or ≤ 1** is a clear sign of conceptual weakness.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Golden Rule $PUF \ge PCF$:** The hours of use $T_{use}$ is **smaller** than the total period $T_{period}$, hence the denominator of PUF is smaller, making PUF larger. If a student calculates $PUF < PCF$, the answer is **definitely wrong**.
> 
> 4. **Misreading "Reserve Capacity":** It is Plant Capacity − Maximum Demand, **NOT** the difference between two maximum demands.
> 
> 5. **Annual Hours:** Always use $T = 8760$ h for one year, $T = 720$ h for 30 days, and $T = 24$ h for one day. Writing $T = 365$ instead of $8760$ loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **LDC Drawing:** The LDC X-axis is **duration (h)**, NOT chronological time. Drawing the LDC with hours starting at 0:00 and ending at 24:00 will be marked **wrong** — it should be 0 h to 24 h of *cumulative load duration*.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Load Curve** = Load vs. Time (chronological, 24 h). Area = Energy (kWh).
- ✅ **Load Duration Curve** = Load vs. Duration (descending order). Area = Energy (same).
- ✅ **Average Load** = $E / T$; **Load Factor** = $L_{avg} / L_{max}$ (≤ 1).
- ✅ **Diversity Factor** = $\sum L_{max,i} / L_{max,station}$ (≥ 1).
- ✅ **Demand Factor** = $L_{max} / \text{Connected Load}$ (≤ 1).
- ✅ **Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)** = $E_{actual} / (\text{Capacity} \times T_{period})$ (≤ 1).
- ✅ **Plant Use Factor (PUF)** = $E_{actual} / (\text{Capacity} \times T_{use})$ (≥ PCF).
- ✅ **Reserve Capacity** = Plant Capacity − Max Demand.
- ✅ **1 MU = 10⁶ kWh = 10³ MWh**; **1 year = 8760 h**.
- ✅ **Golden Rule:** $PUF \ge PCF \ge LF$ (in practice).
- ✅ Diversity Factor is a **station-level** concept; Demand Factor is a **consumer-level** concept.
- ✅ High LF → efficient asset utilization; Low LF → uneconomical operation.
- ✅ Higher DF (diversity) → utility can install smaller generation capacity.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Memorize the formula sheet, draw the LDC in **descending** order, convert units early, and always verify $PUF \ge PCF$.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Transmission System (Electrical Model) - Line Parameters, Resistance, Inductance and Capacitance (Derivation of Three Phase Double Circuit)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Transmission System (Electrical Model) - Line Parameters, Resistance, Inductance and Capacitance (Derivation of Three Phase Double Circuit)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Three-Phase Double Circuit Transmission Line?

A **three-phase double circuit transmission line** is a high-voltage power transmission configuration in which **two independent three-phase circuits** are run on the same tower, sharing the right-of-way (ROW). Each phase is represented by **two conductors (a bundled pair)** per circuit, giving a total of **6 conductors** per tower. This arrangement is universally used for EHV (Extra High Voltage) and UHV (Ultra High Voltage) transmission (typically 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV and above) because it dramatically increases power transfer capability, improves reliability, and reduces line reactance through conductor bundling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A double circuit line is defined as "two parallel three-phase circuits on the same supporting structure, where each phase conductor is split into two (or more) sub-conductors connected in parallel and spaced symmetrically to form a **bundle**."

### 1.2 The Four Fundamental Line Parameters

Every transmission line, no matter how complex, is electrically modeled using four distributed parameters per phase per unit length:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Physical Origin |
|-----------|--------|------|-----------------|
| **Resistance** | R | Ω/km | Ohmic loss in the conductor material |
| **Inductance** | L | H/km | Magnetic field surrounding current-carrying conductors |
| **Capacitance** | C | F/km | Electric field between conductors at different potentials |
| **Conductance** | G | S/km | Leakage current through air (usually neglected for overhead lines) |

> [!NOTE]
> For overhead transmission lines, **G (shunt conductance) ≈ 0** because air is an excellent insulator. The model reduces to **R, L, and C** only.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Highway" of Electricity

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a single-circuit line as a **two-lane road** and a double-circuit line as a **four-lane expressway**.
> - **Bundled conductors** are like vehicles traveling side-by-side in the same lane — they share the load, reducing congestion (current) per conductor and the resulting heating (resistance drop).
> - **GMD (Geometric Mean Distance)** is like the average distance between lanes — the further apart the phases, the less electromagnetic "interference" (inductance) between them.
> - **GMR (Geometric Mean Radius)** is the effective "size" of the conductor for magnetic field calculations — a larger effective radius means the magnetic field can spread out more easily, lowering inductance.

### 1.4 Why Double Circuits? The Engineering Motivation

1. **Increased Power Transfer:** Doubles the ampacity without doubling the right-of-way footprint.
2. **Reduced Line Reactance:** Bundling reduces inductance by approximately **15–25%** compared to a single conductor per phase.
3. **Improved Reliability:** If one circuit trips, the other continues to supply load (N-1 contingency).
4. **Reduced Corona Loss & Radio Interference:** Larger effective bundle radius weakens the surface electric field.
5. **Economic Tower Usage:** One tower carries the equivalent of two separate lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
> - Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
> - Conversion: **2 × 10⁻⁷ = μ₀/π** H/m (appears frequently in inductance formulas)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Line Resistance (R)

The AC resistance of a transmission line conductor is **slightly higher** than its DC resistance due to the **skin effect** (current crowding near the conductor surface at 50/60 Hz) and **proximity effect** (distortion of current distribution due to nearby conductors).

$$R_{ac} = R_{dc} \times k_{skin} \times k_{proximity}$$

For a **double circuit line**, the resistance per phase is halved because two conductors share the phase current in parallel:

$$R_{phase} = \frac{R_{single}}{2}$$

Where R_single is the resistance of one individual conductor (Ω/km).

## 2.2 Flux Linkages and Inductance — Conceptual Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Faraday's Insight:** A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it. When this field links another conductor (or even the same conductor), it induces a voltage that opposes the change in current — this is **inductance**.

For a **single isolated conductor** of radius **r** carrying current **I**, the internal + external flux linkage per meter length gives:

$$L_{single} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \left[ \frac{1}{4} + \ln\frac{D}{r'} \right] \;\; \text{H/m per conductor}$$

Where:
- **r' = r × e^(-1/4) = 0.7788 r** is the **Geometric Mean Radius (GMR)** of a solid round conductor
- **D** = distance to the return path
- The factor **½** (i.e., 1/4 inside the log equivalent) accounts for internal flux linkage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GMR vs Actual Radius:** For inductance calculations, we never use the physical radius **r** directly. We use **r' = 0.7788 r** (for solid non-magnetic conductors) because the internal flux linkage effectively reduces the magnetic size of the conductor. This is the single most common mistake students make in KTU exams.

## 2.3 The GMD-GMR Method (Foundation of Multi-Conductor Analysis)

For a system of **n conductors** carrying **balanced n-phase currents** (phasor sum = 0), the inductance per phase is:

$$L_{phase} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\left(\frac{GMD}{GMR_{phase}}\right) \;\; \text{H/m per phase}$$

### Definition of GMD (Geometric Mean Distance)
For phases **a, b, c** with conductor positions **D_ab, D_bc, D_ca**:

$$GMD = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} \cdot D_{bc} \cdot D_{ca}}$$

### Definition of GMR_phase for a Bundled/Double Circuit
The equivalent GMR of a phase containing multiple conductors (each with self-GMR **r'**) is the geometric mean of:
- All self-GMRs of the conductors in the phase
- All mutual distances between conductors of the same phase

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Single conductor GMR | $r' = 0.7788\,r$ | m | Only for solid round non-magnetic conductors |
| 2 | Bundled conductor GMR (2-subconductor bundle) | $GMR_{bundle} = \sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}$ | m | $d_s$ = spacing between sub-conductors |
| 3 | Double circuit phase GMR (a1, a2 in same phase) | $GMR_a = \sqrt[4]{r' \cdot d_{a1a2} \cdot r' \cdot d_{a1a2}}$ | m | See detailed derivation in Section 3 |
| 4 | GMD (3-phase) | $GMD = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} \cdot D_{bc} \cdot D_{ca}}$ | m | Symmetric transposition |
| 5 | GMD for Double Circuit (6 conductors) | $GMD_a = \sqrt[5]{D_{a1b1}\cdot D_{a1b2}\cdot D_{a1c1}\cdot D_{a1c2}\cdot D_{a2b1}\cdot ...}$ | m | All cross-phase distances |
| 6 | Inductance per phase | $L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{GMD}{GMR_a}\right)$ | H/m | Double circuit |
| 7 | Capacitance per phase | $C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(GMD/r)}$ | F/m | Uses actual radius **r**, NOT r' |
| 8 | Capacitance for double circuit | $C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(GMD_{eq}/r_{eq})}$ | F/m | $r_{eq} = \sqrt{r \cdot d_s}$ for bundle |
| 9 | Per phase resistance (double circuit) | $R = R_{single}/2$ | Ω/km | Two conductors in parallel per phase |
| 10 | Surge impedance | $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Ω | Characteristic of the line |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1:** Inductance uses **r' = 0.7788r**, but Capacitance uses the **actual radius r**. Mixing these up is the #1 source of wrong answers in KTU board exams.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** For bundled conductors, capacitance uses $r_{eq} = \sqrt{r \cdot d_s}$ (not $\sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}$).

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Line Design:** EHV lines (400 kV, 765 kV) are designed using these GMD-GMR calculations to achieve target surge impedances of ~250–400 Ω.
- **Relay Coordination:** Accurate inductance determines the **X/R ratio**, critical for distance relay settings.
- **Reactive Power Compensation:** Capacitance determines the **Ferranti effect** (voltage rise at light load) and the sizing of shunt reactors.
- **System Stability:** The characteristic impedance $Z_c$ and propagation constant determine the **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** of the line.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Assumed Geometry of the Three-Phase Double Circuit Line

Consider a standard **hexagonal (symmetrical) arrangement** used in practice:

```
              a1
          a2      b1
        b2            c1
          c2      a1 (next span)
              ...
```

For a **6-conductor double circuit**:
- Phase **a**: conductors **a₁** and **a₂** (opposite vertices of hexagon)
- Phase **b**: conductors **b₁** and **b₂** (opposite vertices of hexagon)
- Phase **c**: conductors **c₁** and **c₂** (opposite vertices of hexagon)

The center-to-center distances between adjacent conductors (hexagon side) = **d** (bundle spacing).
The center of the hexagon is the geometric center of the tower.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Result (will be derived):** The GMD between phases reduces to the **distance between phase centers** because the geometric mean of all cross-phase distances equals the distance between the centers of the two hexagon triangles. This is why KTU problems often give you the inter-phase distance **D** directly.

## 3.2 DERIVATION 1: Equivalent GMR of Phase 'a' in a Double Circuit Line

The phase 'a' consists of **two conductors a₁ and a₂**, each with self-GMR = **r'**.
The distance between a₁ and a₂ is the **bundle spacing d_s** (or simply **d** in the hexagonal arrangement, but we use $d_s$ for clarity).

The equivalent GMR of phase a (combining the self-GMRs of a₁, a₂ and their mutual distance) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
GMR_a &= \sqrt[4]{(r'_{a_1}) \cdot (r'_{a_2}) \cdot (D_{a_1,a_2}) \cdot (D_{a_2,a_1})} \\
&= \sqrt[4]{r' \cdot r' \cdot d_s \cdot d_s} \\
&= \sqrt[4]{r'^2 \cdot d_s^2} \\
&= \sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the 4th root of 4 quantities (two self-GMRs and two mutual distances, which are equal), so it simplifies to the **square root** of the product $r' \cdot d_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula $\boxed{GMR_{eq} = \sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}}$ is the **standard result for a 2-conductor bundle** in a double circuit line.

## 3.3 DERIVATION 2: GMD Between Phases in a Double Circuit Line

For the GMD between phase a and phase b, we need the **geometric mean of all 4 distances**:
- $D_{a_1, b_1}$, $D_{a_1, b_2}$, $D_{a_2, b_1}$, $D_{a_2, b_2}$

In the **symmetrical hexagonal arrangement** (which KTU assumes by default), the geometric mean of these four distances equals the **distance between the centers of the two phases** (which we denote **D_ab**). This is a beautiful mathematical property of regular hexagons.

$$\begin{aligned}
GMD_{ab} &= \sqrt[4]{D_{a_1b_1} \cdot D_{a_1b_2} \cdot D_{a_2b_1} \cdot D_{a_2b_2}} \\
&= D_{ab} \quad \text{(by symmetry of hexagon)}
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly, $GMD_{bc} = D_{bc}$ and $GMD_{ca} = D_{ca}$.

The **overall GMD** of the 3-phase system:

$$GMD = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} \cdot D_{bc} \cdot D_{ca}}$$

For an **equilateral (symmetric) tower** with $D_{ab} = D_{bc} = D_{ca} = D$:

$$GMD = D$$

## 3.4 DERIVATION 3: Inductance Per Phase of a Double Circuit Line

Combining the GMR and GMD results:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{phase} &= 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{GMD}{GMR_a}\right) \;\; \text{H/m per phase} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{\sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}}\right) \;\; \text{(symmetric case)} \\
&= 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{D^2}{r' \cdot d_s}\right) \;\; \text{H/m per phase}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> The factor of 2 in the first line becomes a factor of 1 in the exponent of the last line because $\ln(x^{1/2}) = \frac{1}{2}\ln(x)$, and $2 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1$. This is a frequently tested simplification in KTU exams.

## 3.5 DERIVATION 4: Capacitance Per Phase of a Double Circuit Line

The capacitance of a three-phase line is derived from the potential difference between conductors. Using the same GMD-GMR framework, but with the **actual radius r** (NOT r'):

$$C_n = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(GMD/r_{eq})} \;\; \text{F/m per phase}$$

For a **double circuit (bundled)** line, $r_{eq} = \sqrt{r \cdot d_s}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_n &= \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{D}{\sqrt{r \cdot d_s}}\right)} \\
&= \frac{\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{D}{\sqrt{r \cdot d_s}}\right)} \quad \text{(using } 2\pi\varepsilon_0 \text{ in numerator, can be expressed as)} \\
&= \frac{0.02414 \times 10^{-9}}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{\sqrt{r \cdot d_s}}\right)} \;\; \text{F/km per phase}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Shortcut for KTU Exams:**
> $C = \dfrac{0.02414}{\log_{10}(D/r_{eq})} \;\; \mu\text{F/km}$ — this is the form examiners prefer for numerical problems.

## 3.6 DERIVATION 5: General Case (Asymmetric / Untransposed Tower)

If the three inter-phase distances are **different** ($D_{ab} \neq D_{bc} \neq D_{ca}$), and/or the double circuit has **asymmetric sub-conductor spacing**, the general procedure is:

**Step 1:** Compute the equivalent GMR of each phase (a, b, c) using the 4th root of the product of self-GMRs and mutual intra-phase distances.

**Step 2:** Compute the GMD between every pair of phases using the 4th root of all cross-phase conductor distances.

**Step 3:** Compute the overall GMD as the cubic root of the product of the three GMDs.

**Step 4:** Apply the inductance and capacitance formulas.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #3:** In the **asymmetric case**, students often use only the **direct phase-to-phase distances** (D_ab, D_bc, D_ca) and forget the **bundle-to-bundle geometric mean** across 4 conductors. This is a 7-mark deduction in typical KTU valuation.

## 3.7 Summary of the Five Critical Derivation Steps

| Step | Concept | Key Equation |
|------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | Bundle GMR (intra-phase) | $GMR_a = \sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}$ |
| 2 | Phase GMD (inter-phase, symmetric) | $GMD_{ab} = D_{ab}$ |
| 3 | System GMD | $GMD = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} D_{bc} D_{ca}}$ |
| 4 | Inductance per phase | $L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln(GMD/GMR_a)$ H/m |
| 5 | Capacitance per phase (neutral) | $C_n = 2\pi\varepsilon_0 / \ln(GMD/r_{eq})$ F/m |
