# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define Geometric Mean Radius (GMR) and Geometric Mean Distance (GMD) as applied to a transmission line. Why is the actual radius of the conductor not used in inductance calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

**GMR (Geometric Mean Radius):** It is the effective radius of a conductor for the purpose of computing its magnetic flux linkage. For a solid round non-magnetic conductor, $GMR = r' = 0.7788 \, r$, where **r** is the actual physical radius. For a stranded conductor, GMR is also $0.7788 \, r$ approximately. For a bundled conductor (two sub-conductors spaced $d_s$ apart), the equivalent GMR is $\sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}$.

**GMD (Geometric Mean Distance):** It is the geometric mean of the distances from one phase conductor to all other phase conductors. For a 3-phase symmetric line, $GMD = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} \cdot D_{bc} \cdot D_{ca}}$. For a double circuit, GMD between phases is the 4th root of all 4 inter-bundle distances.

**Why not actual radius?** The actual radius **r** accounts only for the geometric boundary. However, the magnetic flux linked with a conductor includes **internal flux** (within the conductor cross-section), which contributes approximately $0.25 \, \mu_0$ per unit length to the total inductance. This is mathematically equivalent to shrinking the radius to $0.7788 \, r$ and considering only external flux.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between the use of actual radius 'r' in capacitance calculation and the GMR 'r'' in inductance calculation. Why does this asymmetry exist?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Inductance (L) | Capacitance (C) |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Radius used | $r' = 0.7788 \, r$ (GMR) | $r$ (actual) |
| Field type | Magnetic | Electric |
| Internal contribution | Yes (internal flux) | No (no field inside conductor) |

**Reason for Asymmetry:** The electric field exists **only outside** the conductor (since the conductor is an equipotential surface and there is no electric field inside a perfect conductor). Hence, capacitance depends only on the **outer boundary**, which is the actual radius **r**. In contrast, the magnetic field **penetrates inside** the conductor (skin effect aside, current is uniformly distributed at 50/60 Hz in a small conductor). This internal flux linkage must be included in the inductance formula, leading to the correction factor 0.7788.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **inductance per phase per km** of a three-phase double circuit transmission line with conductors arranged in a hexagonal (symmetric) configuration. Clearly state the assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 400 kV, three-phase, 50 Hz, double circuit transmission line has conductors of radius **1.5 cm** arranged in a hexagon with **bundle spacing = 40 cm** and **inter-phase distance = 12 m**. Calculate (i) inductance per phase per km, (ii) capacitance per phase per km, and (iii) charging MVAR per phase per 100 km.

---

**Solution to Part (a): Derivation**

**Assumptions:**
1. The line is fully transposed (or symmetrically arranged so that each phase sees the same average flux linkage).
2. The line is electrically short, so distributed parameters can be lumped per unit length.
3. The current is balanced 3-phase, so the sum of the three phase currents is zero (no return through neutral).
4. The conductor material is non-magnetic ($\mu_r = 1$).
5. The hexagonal arrangement makes $D_{ab} = D_{bc} = D_{ca} = D$ (equilateral).

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Flux linkage of a single conductor: For conductor **a₁** of radius **r**, the total flux linkage (internal + external) per unit length due to its own current is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\psi_{a_1, \text{self}} &= \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} I \left( \frac{1}{4} + \ln\frac{1}{r'} \right) \quad \text{Wb-turns/m} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-7} \, I \, \ln\frac{1}{r'} \quad (\text{with internal flux included as } r' = 0.7788 r)
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2 — Flux linkage from all other conductors: Using the principle of superposition and assuming balanced 3-phase currents $I_a + I_b + I_c = 0$, the total flux linking phase 'a' per unit length:

$$\begin{aligned}
\psi_a = 2 \times 10^{-7} \left[ I_a \ln\frac{1}{r'} + I_b \ln\frac{1}{D_{ab}} + I_c \ln\frac{1}{D_{ac}} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Using $I_b + I_c = -I_a$ and simplifying:

$$\begin{aligned}
\psi_a &= 2 \times 10^{-7} I_a \ln\frac{D_{ab} \cdot D_{ac}}{r' \cdot D_{ab \to ac,\text{eff}}}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — For the double circuit (6 conductors), the equivalent GMR of phase 'a' (conductors a₁ and a₂):

$$GMR_a = \sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}$$

Step 5 — The equivalent GMD for the double circuit (using symmetry):

$$GMD = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} \cdot D_{bc} \cdot D_{ca}}$$

Step 6 — Final inductance per phase:

$$\boxed{L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{GMD}{GMR_a}\right) = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{\sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}}\right) \;\;\text{H/m}}$$

For per-km value: multiply by 1000.

---

**Solution to Part (b): Numerical**

**Given:** $r = 1.5$ cm $= 0.015$ m, $d_s = 0.40$ m, $D = 12$ m, frequency = 50 Hz, line length = 100 km.

**Step 1 — Compute GMR of phase:**
$$r' = 0.7788 \times 0.015 = 0.011682 \text{ m}$$
$$GMR_a = \sqrt{0.011682 \times 0.40} = \sqrt{0.004673} = 0.0684 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Inductance per phase per km:**
$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{12}{0.0684}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln(175.44) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.167 \\
&= 1.033 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H/km} \\
&= 1.033 \text{ mH/km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Capacitance per phase per km:**
$$r_{eq} = \sqrt{r \cdot d_s} = \sqrt{0.015 \times 0.40} = \sqrt{0.006} = 0.07746 \text{ m}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1000}{\ln(12/0.07746)} \\
&= \frac{5.563 \times 10^{-8}}{\ln(154.9)} \\
&= \frac{5.563 \times 10^{-8}}{5.043} \\
&= 1.103 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/km} \\
&= 0.01103 \text{ µF/km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Charging MVAR per 100 km:**
$$X_c = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 1.103 \times 10^{-8}} = 28870 \, \Omega \text{ per km}$$

Per phase reactive power generated:
$$Q_{phase} = \frac{V_{phase}^2}{X_c} = \frac{(400/\sqrt{3})^2}{28870} = \frac{(230.94 \times 10^3)^2}{28870} = 1.847 \text{ MVAR/km}$$

For 100 km per phase: $Q = 184.7$ MVAR/phase.

**Total 3-phase charging for 100 km:** $Q_{3\phi} = 3 \times 184.7 = 554$ MVAR.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **capacitance per phase per km** of a three-phase double circuit line, and explain why the actual radius **r** (not GMR) is used in the denominator.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A three-phase, 50 Hz, double circuit transmission line has phase conductors of diameter **2.5 cm** arranged in a vertical equilateral triangle of side **6 m** with a **bundle spacing of 35 cm** between sub-conductors. Determine the **inductance** and **capacitance per phase per km**, and the **charging current per phase per km** at 220 kV line voltage.

---

**Solution to Part (a): Derivation**

**Starting from the potential of a single conductor in a group of n conductors:**

The potential of conductor **a₁** (with charge $q_{a_1}$ per unit length) is:

$$V_{a_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \left[ q_{a_1} \ln\frac{1}{r} + q_{a_2} \ln\frac{1}{D_{a_1 a_2}} + q_{b_1} \ln\frac{1}{D_{a_1 b_1}} + \cdots \right]$$

For a balanced 3-phase system: $q_a + q_b + q_c = 0$ (sum of all 6 conductor charges = 0 because no neutral return).

Applying the same transposition argument as in inductance, and using the **actual radius r** (not r'), we obtain:

$$\boxed{C_n = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(GMD/r_{eq})} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{D}{\sqrt{r \cdot d_s}}\right)} \;\;\text{F/m per phase}}$$

For a double circuit: $r_{eq} = \sqrt{r \cdot d_s}$ is the **equivalent bundle radius**.

**Why actual radius r?**
- The electric field inside a perfect conductor is **zero** (conductor is an equipotential body).
- Therefore, the electric flux linkage depends only on the **outer cylindrical surface** of the conductor, whose boundary is at radius **r**.
- In contrast, magnetic flux penetrates inside the conductor, requiring the modified radius $r' = 0.7788r$ for inductance.

---

**Solution to Part (b): Numerical**

**Given:** Diameter = 2.5 cm → $r = 0.0125$ m; $D = 6$ m; $d_s = 0.35$ m; $V_L = 220$ kV; $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Equivalent GMR for inductance:**
$$r' = 0.7788 \times 0.0125 = 0.009735 \text{ m}$$
$$GMR_a = \sqrt{0.009735 \times 0.35} = \sqrt{0.003407} = 0.05837 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Inductance per phase per km:**
$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{6}{0.05837}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln(102.79) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.632 \\
&= 9.264 \times 10^{-4} \text{ H/km} \\
&= 0.9264 \text{ mH/km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent radius for capacitance:**
$$r_{eq} = \sqrt{r \cdot d_s} = \sqrt{0.0125 \times 0.35} = \sqrt{0.004375} = 0.06614 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance per phase per km:**
$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1000}{\ln(6/0.06614)} \\
&= \frac{5.563 \times 10^{-8}}{\ln(90.72)} \\
&= \frac{5.563 \times 10^{-8}}{4.507} \\
&= 1.234 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/km} \\
&= 0.01234 \text{ µF/km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Charging current per phase per km:**

Phase voltage: $V_{ph} = 220 / \sqrt{3} = 127.02$ kV

Capacitive reactance per km:
$$X_c = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 1.234 \times 10^{-8}} = 25,793 \, \Omega/\text{km}$$

Charging current per phase per km:
$$I_c = \frac{V_{ph}}{X_c} = \frac{127{,}020}{25{,}793} = 4.925 \text{ A/km/phase}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Using r' in capacitance (or r in inductance) — 3 to 4 mark deduction.** This is the most common and most penalized mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting the bundle spacing d_s** when computing equivalent GMR/GMD for a double circuit. Many students treat the double circuit as a single circuit and get 0 marks for setup.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Confusing mH/km with H/km, or using cm instead of m in the logarithm. Logarithms are **dimensionless**, so all distances MUST be in the same unit.
> 4. **Square root vs 4th root confusion:** For a 2-conductor bundle, $GMR = \sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}$ (square root of 2 quantities). For a 4-conductor bundle, $GMR = (r'^3 \cdot d_s^3)^{1/8} = \sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}$ only if all spacings are equal.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the assumption list (transposition, balanced load, etc.). Skipping this is a guaranteed deduction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Three-Phase Double Circuit Line Parameters**
>
> - **Resistance per phase:** $R = R_{single}/2$ (two conductors in parallel per phase)
> - **Single conductor GMR:** $r' = 0.7788 \, r$ — always for inductance, never for capacitance
> - **Bundled GMR (inductance):** $GMR_{bundle} = \sqrt{r' \cdot d_s}$
> - **Bundled radius (capacitance):** $r_{eq} = \sqrt{r \cdot d_s}$ — uses actual r
> - **Phase GMD (3-phase symmetric):** $GMD = \sqrt[3]{D_{ab} D_{bc} D_{ca}} = D$ if equilateral
> - **Inductance per phase:** $L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ln(GMD/GMR_a)$ H/m — **multiply by 1000 for H/km**
> - **Capacitance per phase:** $C_n = 2\pi\varepsilon_0 / \ln(GMD/r_{eq})$ F/m — **multiply by 1000 for F/km**
> - **Charging MVAR:** $Q = V^2 / X_c = 2\pi f C V^2$ per phase
> - **Surge Impedance:** $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C} \approx 250$–$400 \, \Omega$ for EHV double circuit lines
> - **Key physical insight:** Bundling reduces inductance AND increases capacitance, both of which lower surge impedance and increase SIL (Surge Impedance Loading).
> - **For the hexagonal double circuit tower:** The GMD between phases equals the **distance between phase centers** by geometric symmetry — exploit this to save calculation time in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Line Modelling, Classifications (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission Line Modelling, Classifications (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Transmission Line?

A **transmission line** is the high-voltage electrical conductor (or system of conductors) used to transport bulk electrical power from generating stations (power houses) to distribution substations, typically operating at voltages from **11 kV to 800 kV** (and above in EHV/UHV systems). The complete path between the sending end and the receiving end constitutes the transmission system.

When the length of this conductor becomes comparable to the wavelength of the propagating electrical signal (or when the line's length is significant relative to **80 km–250 km** benchmarks in power systems), the line can no longer be treated as a lumped component. It must be **modelled** using distributed parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A transmission line is an electrical conduit characterized by four uniformly distributed parameters per unit length: **Resistance (R)**, **Inductance (L)**, **Capacitance (C)**, and **Conductance (G)**. These parameters are not concentrated at one point but are spread continuously along the entire length of the conductor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Hose Model"

Imagine a very long, thin rubber hose used to carry water from a tank to a distant field:

- The **friction** of water against the hose walls corresponds to **Resistance (R)** — it dissipates energy as heat.
- The **inertia** of the water column (resistance to sudden changes in flow) corresponds to **Inductance (L)** — it opposes changes in current.
- The **elasticity** of the hose walls (which can store water temporarily when pressure builds up) corresponds to **Capacitance (C)** — it stores energy in the electric field between conductors.
- The **leakage** of water through tiny holes in the hose corresponds to **Conductance (G)** — it represents leakage current through insulation.

Just as we model the hose by these four distributed physical effects, we model a transmission line using the **RLCG parameters** distributed over every kilometre of its length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we model transmission lines?**
> Because the **voltage** at the receiving end differs from the sending end due to line impedance, and the **efficiency** and **voltage regulation** depend on how accurately we account for the line parameters. Without modelling, we cannot predict power flow, losses, or stability.

## 1.3 The Four Distributed Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Cause | Units (per km) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Resistance | **R** | Ohmic loss in conductor material (Cu/Al) | Ω/km |
| Inductance | **L** | Magnetic field around current-carrying conductor | H/km |
| Capacitance | **C** | Electric field between conductors (and to ground) | F/km |
| Conductance | **G** | Leakage current through imperfect insulation | S/km |

## 1.4 Concept of Line Modelling

**Line modelling** is the mathematical and circuit representation of a physical transmission line using these distributed parameters so that we can predict the relationship between **sending-end (S.E.)** and **receiving-end (R.E.)** voltage and current. The model takes the form of an **equivalent two-port network** characterized by its **ABCD (Generalized) Constants**.

$$V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R$$

$$I_S = C \cdot V_R + D \cdot I_R$$

Where $V_S, I_S$ are sending-end voltage and current, and $V_R, I_R$ are receiving-end values. $A, B, C, D$ are the **generalized circuit constants** of the line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Distributed Parameter Concept

A transmission line is a **lumped-continuous** system. Consider an infinitesimal element of length $\Delta x$ at a distance $x$ from the receiving end:

- Series impedance per phase: $z = R + j\omega L$ (Ω/km)
- Shunt admittance per phase: $y = G + j\omega C$ (S/km)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **elementary section of length dx**, the series voltage drop is $(R\,dx)\cdot i + (L\,dx)\dfrac{di}{dt}$, and the shunt current is $(G\,dx)\cdot v + (C\,dx)\dfrac{dv}{dt}$. Because these are *distributed*, they are governed by **Telegrapher's equations** — but for *conceptual* KTU Module-2, we use lumped π or T equivalents.

## 2.2 Classification of Transmission Lines

The classification is **purely based on the operating length** of the line, because each length range demands a different mathematical treatment for accurate results.

### 2.2.1 Short Transmission Line

- **Length:** Up to **80 km**
- **Operating Voltage:** Below **20 kV**
- **Modelling Assumption:** Only **series impedance (R + jX)** is considered. Shunt capacitance is **negligible** because at this short length, the charging current is too small to materially affect voltage.
- **Equivalent Circuit:** A simple series impedance $Z = R + jX_L$ between sending and receiving ends.

### 2.2.2 Medium Transmission Line

- **Length:** **80 km to 250 km**
- **Operating Voltage:** **20 kV to 100 kV**
- **Modelling Assumption:** Series impedance is **lumped**, but shunt capacitance (and sometimes leakage conductance) is **appreciable** and cannot be ignored.
- **Equivalent Circuits:** Three standard nominal-π and nominal-T configurations:
  1. **End Condenser Model** (simplest)
  2. **Nominal-T Model** (lumped series Z at centre, half Y at each end)
  3. **Nominal-π Model** (half Y at each end, full Z in middle)

### 2.2.3 Long Transmission Line

- **Length:** Above **250 km**
- **Operating Voltage:** Above **100 kV**
- **Modelling Assumption:** Parameters are **rigorously distributed**. The line is solved using **hyperbolic functions** (or the rigorous solution of the wave equation).
- **Equivalent Circuit:** Distributed-parameter model solved by **characteristic impedance** $Z_C$ and **propagation constant** $\gamma$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** *"80, 250 — boundaries in km; below 80, ignore C; above 250, go hyperbolic!"*

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Concept Module)

The following table contains the **definitive, board-relevant formulas** for Transmission Line Modelling & Classification (Concept Only). All values are **per phase**.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | KTU Use |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Series Impedance | $Z = R + jX_L = R + j\omega L$ | Ω/phase | Short line model |
| Shunt Admittance | $Y = G + jB_C = G + j\omega C$ | S/phase | Medium/Long line |
| Inductive Reactance | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Ω | Line drop calculation |
| Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ω | Charging current |
| Receiving-end Power | $P_R = V_R I_R \cos\phi_R$ | W | Performance |
| Sending-end Power | $P_S = V_S I_S \cos\phi_S$ | W | Performance |
| Line Losses | $P_L = P_S - P_R$ | W | Efficiency |
| Transmission Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Performance |
| Voltage Regulation | $\%VR = \dfrac{\vert V_R^{NL} - V_R^{FL}\vert}{V_R^{FL}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Performance |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{R}{Z} = \dfrac{P}{VI}$ | — | Phasor analysis |
| Charging Current (Medium) | $I_C = V \cdot Y/2$ (for nominal-π) | A | Reactive power |
| ABCD Constants Identity | $AD - BC = 1$ | — | Symmetric line check |
| Characteristic Impedance (concept) | $Z_C = \sqrt{\dfrac{z}{y}}$ | Ω | Long line surge |
| Propagation Constant (concept) | $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$ | 1/km | Long line waves |
| Surge Impedance Loading | $SIL = \dfrac{V^2}{Z_C}$ | MW | EHV design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the foundation for **load flow studies, fault analysis, protection coordination, and grid stability** — all of which are covered in higher-semester KTU subjects like Power Systems-II and Power System Protection.

## 2.4 Why Classification Matters

Each length bracket uses a different mathematical model because of the **relative magnitude of shunt capacitance** to series impedance:

- At 50 Hz and 80 km, $\omega C V$ (charging VAR) is small compared to $I \cdot X$ (series drop) → ignore C.
- At 250 km, charging VAR is significant → must model C.
- Above 250 km, the travelling-wave nature of voltage/current dominates → distributed model is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is explicitly **"(Concept Only)"** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus directive, this section provides the **conceptual derivations** that justify the equivalent circuits and ABCD representation, **without** heavy mathematical rigour. All steps are written explicitly without truncation.

## 3.1 Derivation: Short Transmission Line Model (Conceptual)

**Step 1 — Identify the model parameters.**
For a short line (length $< 80$ km), the only electrical effects considered are:
- Series resistance $R$ (Ω/phase) representing conductor heating loss.
- Series inductive reactance $X_L = \omega L$ (Ω/phase) representing magnetic field linkage.

The shunt parameters (C and G) are assumed **negligible** because the line is too short to develop a meaningful charging current or leakage.

**Step 2 — Draw the single-phase equivalent.**
A short line is therefore represented by a single series impedance between the sending end and receiving end:

$$Z = R + jX_L = R + j\omega L$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).**
The sending-end voltage $V_S$ equals the receiving-end voltage plus the drop across the series impedance:

$$V_S = V_R + I \cdot Z$$

Where $I$ is the line current. For a short line, current is the **same** at both ends because there is no shunt path:

$$I_S = I_R = I$$

**Step 4 — Construct the ABCD parameters.**
Comparing the equation with the standard two-port form $V_S = A V_R + B I_R$:

$$A = 1, \quad B = Z, \quad C = 0, \quad D = 1$$

This is the **short-line ABCD model** used by KTU examiners.

## 3.2 Derivation: Nominal-π Model (Medium Line Concept)

**Step 1 — Distribute the shunt admittance.**
The total shunt admittance $Y$ is split into two equal halves, $Y/2$, placed at each end of the line.

**Step 2 — Locate the series impedance.**
The full series impedance $Z$ sits in the **middle** of the π.

**Step 3 — Write nodal equations.**
At the receiving end, the current entering the line is $I_R + V_R \cdot (Y/2)$.
The voltage at the middle node (after the series Z) is:
$$V_M = V_R + I_R \cdot Z$$
The current through the series branch is $I = I_R + V_R \cdot (Y/2)$.
At the sending end:
$$V_S = V_M + I \cdot (Y/2) \cdot Z \cdot \text{(correction)}$$

**Step 4 — Final ABCD form for nominal-π.**
$$A = D = 1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$$

$$B = Z$$

$$C = Y \left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$$

This conceptual derivation shows why **half the capacitance** is taken at each end — to keep the total shunt admittance equal to the distributed value.

## 3.3 Derivation: Nominal-T Model (Medium Line Concept)

**Step 1 — Split the series impedance.**
The total $Z$ is divided into $Z/2$ at each end, with the full shunt admittance $Y$ at the centre node.

**Step 2 — Nodal analysis at the centre.**
The voltage at the centre node is $V_M = V_R + I_R \cdot (Z/2)$.
The current through the shunt branch is $V_M \cdot Y$.
The current entering the sending end is $I_S = I_R + V_M \cdot Y$.

**Step 3 — Substitute to find $V_S$:**
$$V_S = V_M + I_S \cdot (Z/2)$$

**Step 4 — Final ABCD form for nominal-T:**
$$A = D = 1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$$

$$B = Z \left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$$

$$C = Y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** Note that both nominal-π and nominal-T yield the same $A$ and $D$ (this is a property of symmetrical lines), but $B$ and $C$ are **interchanged in form**. KTU problems often exploit this symmetry.

## 3.4 Conceptual Long-Line Insight

For a long transmission line, solving the **Telegrapher's differential equation** yields:

$$V_S = V_R \cosh(\gamma l) + I_R Z_C \sinh(\gamma l)$$

$$I_S = I_R \cosh(\gamma l) + \dfrac{V_R}{Z_C} \sinh(\gamma l)$$

Therefore the long-line ABCD parameters are:

$$A = D = \cosh(\gamma l)$$

$$B = Z_C \sinh(\gamma l)$$

$$C = \dfrac{\sinh(\gamma l)}{Z_C}$$

Where:
- $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$ is the **propagation constant** (per km)
- $Z_C = \sqrt{z/y}$ is the **characteristic (surge) impedance** (Ω)
- $l$ is the line length in km

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Conceptual Takeaway:** As line length increases, the modelling accuracy improves progressively: **lumped Z → nominal-π/T → distributed hyperbolic**. The "Concept Only" directive in KTU Module 2 means students must understand *why* each model is chosen, not derive every equation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection Guide

| Line Type | Length | Voltage | Series Z | Shunt Y | Model Used | Method |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Short | $< 80$ km | $< 20$ kV | Lumped | Ignored | Series Z | Trigonometric |
| Medium | 80–250 km | 20–100 kV | Lumped | Half lumped | Nominal-π / T | Phasor algebra |
| Long | $> 250$ km | $> 100$ kV | Distributed | Distributed | Hyperbolic | Telegrapher's eqn. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Transmission Line Modelling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Physical Transmission Line] --> B0[Identify Length and Voltage]
    B0 --> C0{Length Classification}
    C0 -->|Less than 80 km| D0[Short Line Model]
    C0 -->|80 to 250 km| E0[Medium Line Model]
    C0 -->|Greater than 250 km| F0[Long Line Model]
    D0 --> D1[Equivalent Series Z Only]
    D1 --> D2[ABCD: A=1 B=Z C=0 D=1]
    E0 --> E1[Nominal T Circuit]
    E0 --> E2[Nominal Pi Circuit]
    E0 --> E3[End Condenser Model]
    E1 --> E4[ABCD Computed via Phasor Algebra]
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> E4
    F0 --> F1[Distributed Parameter Model]
    F1 --> F2[Characteristic Impedance Zc]
    F1 --> F3[Propagation Constant Gamma]
    F2 --> F4[ABCD via Hyperbolic Functions]
    F3 --> F4
    D2 --> G0[Performance Evaluation]
    E4 --> G0
    F4 --> G0
    G0 --> G1[Voltage Regulation]
    G0 --> G2[Transmission Efficiency]
    G0 --> G3[Sending End Voltage]
    G0 --> G4[Power Flow Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Short Line Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    SE[Node SE: Sending End] -->|Vs| ZB[Series Impedance Z = R plus jXL]
    ZB --> RE[Node RE: Receiving End]
    RE -.->|Vr| SE
    RE2[Load at Receiving End] --- RE
    NE[Neutral / Ground] --- SE
    NE2[Neutral / Ground] --- RE
    NE --- NE2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagram uses **node identifiers** that are alphanumeric (`SE`, `RE`, `ZB`, etc.) — never reserved Mermaid keywords — to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE compilation safeguard.

## 4.3 Schematic: Nominal-π Equivalent (Medium Line)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[Sending End Vs] --> ZP[Series Z at Centre]
    ZP --> R0[Receiving End Vr]
    S0 --- YP1[Shunt Y over 2 at Sending End]
    YP1 --- N0[Neutral Bus]
    R0 --- YP2[Shunt Y over 2 at Receiving End]
    YP2 --- N0
    RL[Load] --- R0
    RL --- N0
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Nominal-T Equivalent (Medium Line)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[Sending End Vs] --> ZP1[Series Z over 2]
    ZP1 --> M0[Mid Shunt Node Y]
    M0 --> ZP2[Series Z over 2]
    ZP2 --> R0[Receiving End Vr]
    M0 --- N0[Neutral]
    S0 --- N0
    R0 --- N0
    RL[Load] --- R0
```

## 4.5 Concept Map: Parameter Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Resistance R] --> P5[Conductor Material Loss Heat]
    P2[Inductance L] --> P6[Magnetic Field Around Conductor]
    P3[Capacitance C] --> P7[Electric Field Between Conductors]
    P4[Conductance G] --> P8[Leakage Through Dielectric]
    P5 --> P9[Distributed Per Unit Length]
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Forms Equivalent Circuit per km]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a transmission line and list its four distributed parameters.**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
A transmission line is a conductor (or set of conductors) used to transmit electrical power over long distances at high voltage from generating stations to substations. It is characterized by four distributed parameters per unit length:
1. **Resistance (R)** — represents ohmic (I²R) losses in the conductor.
2. **Inductance (L)** — represents the magnetic field around the current-carrying conductor.
3. **Capacitance (C)** — represents the electric field between conductors.
4. **Conductance (G)** — represents leakage current through the dielectric insulation.

### **Q2. Why is the capacitance effect neglected in short transmission lines?**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
The capacitance effect is neglected in short transmission lines (length less than **80 km**, voltage below **20 kV**) because the line is too short to develop any significant charging current. The magnitude of the capacitive current ($\omega C V$) is so small compared to the load current that its effect on voltage drop and power factor is negligible. Including it would unnecessarily complicate calculations without materially improving accuracy. Hence, only the series impedance $Z = R + jX_L$ is modelled.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Full ESE Pattern)

### **Question A:**
**(a)** With the help of neat phasor diagrams, derive the relationship between sending-end and receiving-end voltage for a **short transmission line**. State the ABCD constants. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Explain with a circuit diagram why **medium transmission lines** require shunt capacitance to be modelled, while short lines do not. Compare the **nominal-π** and **nominal-T** models. **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023 — Adapted]**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Short Line Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define the model:**
For a short line ($l < 80$ km), only series impedance $Z = R + jX_L$ is considered. Shunt capacitance is neglected.

**Step 2 — Equivalent circuit:**
$Z$ is connected between sending end and receiving end. Current $I$ is the same throughout the line:
$$I_S = I_R = I$$

**Step 3 — Apply KVL:**
$$V_S = V_R + I \cdot Z$$

**Step 4 — ABCD Constants:**
$$A = 1, \quad B = Z, \quad C = 0, \quad D = 1$$

**Step 5 — Phasor diagram (conceptual):**
Taking $V_R$ as reference, $I$ lags $V_R$ by $\phi_R$. The drop $IR$ is along $I$, and $IX_L$ is perpendicular to $I$ (leading). The vector sum $V_S = V_R + IR + jIX_L$ gives the sending-end voltage.

#### **Part (b) — Medium Line Explanation:**

**Step 1 — Why medium lines need capacitance:**
For lines between **80 km and 250 km** operating at **20 kV to 100 kV**, the charging current $I_C = \omega C V$ becomes appreciable. Ignoring it would underestimate voltage rise (Ferranti effect) and miscalculate the sending-end power factor.

**Step 2 — Nominal-π Model:**
The full series $Z$ is at the centre, with $Y/2$ at each end. ABCD parameters are:
$$A = D = 1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z, \quad C = Y\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Nominal-T Model:**
The full shunt $Y$ is at the centre node, with $Z/2$ at each end. ABCD parameters are:
$$A = D = 1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right), \quad C = Y$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**
Both models give identical $A$ and $D$ (since the line is symmetric). The π-model is preferred when **shunt capacitance is more dominant**; the T-model is preferred when **series impedance is more dominant**.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

### **Question B:**
**(a)** Classify transmission lines based on length, voltage, and model used. Draw the equivalent circuit for each class. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Define the four **generalized circuit constants (A, B, C, D)** of a transmission line. What is their physical significance, and what relationship do they satisfy? **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Adapted]**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Classification:**

| Type | Length | Voltage | Model |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Short | $< 80$ km | $< 20$ kV | Series Z only |
| Medium | 80–250 km | 20–100 kV | Nominal-π or Nominal-T |
| Long | $> 250$ km | $> 100$ kV | Distributed / Hyperbolic |

**Equivalent circuits:**
- **Short:** $V_S \rightarrow Z \rightarrow V_R$
- **Medium (π):** Shunt $Y/2$ on each side of central $Z$
- **Medium (T):** Series $Z/2$ on each side of central $Y$
- **Long:** Infinitesimally small $dz$ sections, summed via integration → hyperbolic functions.

#### **Part (b) — ABCD Constants:**

The general two-port representation of a transmission line is:
$$V_S = A V_R + B I_R$$
$$I_S = C V_R + D I_R$$

- **A = $V_S/V_R$ at no-load ($I_R=0$)** — represents the **no-load voltage ratio** (dimensionless).
- **B = $V_S/I_R$ at no-load** — represents the **short-circuit transfer impedance** (Ω).
- **C = $I_S/V_R$ at no-load** — represents the **open-circuit transfer admittance** (S).
- **D = $I_S/I_R$ at short-circuit ($V_R=0$)** — represents the **short-circuit current ratio** (dimensionless).

**Relationship (Identity):**
$$AD - BC = 1$$

This identity holds for **any passive, linear, bilateral, and reciprocal** two-port network — a property that every transmission line satisfies.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the per-phase assumption.** Transmission line calculations are done per phase unless the problem explicitly says otherwise. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is missed.
> 2. **Confusing nominal-π and nominal-T ABCD values.** Students often swap $B$ and $C$ for the two models. Memorize: in π, **B = Z** (pure); in T, **C = Y** (pure).
> 3. **Not drawing the phasor diagram** in short-line derivations. KTU's "with neat phasor diagram" instruction carries 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the identity $AD - BC = 1$** in ABCD-constant answers. This is a favourite 1-mark KTU follow-up.
> 5. **Mixing up the length boundaries (80 km, 250 km).** These are the most-tested numerical thresholds in Part A.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A transmission line is a **distributed parameter system** with **R, L, C, G** per unit length.
- Classification by length: **Short (<80 km), Medium (80–250 km), Long (>250 km)**.
- Short line: **series Z only**, ABCD = **(1, Z, 0, 1)**.
- Medium line: **nominal-π or nominal-T**, with $Y/2$ or $Z/2$ splits respectively.
- Long line: **distributed model**, solved by **hyperbolic functions** with $\gamma$ and $Z_C$.
- **ABCD constants** form a two-port network; identity is **$AD - BC = 1$**.
- **Voltage regulation** and **transmission efficiency** are the two key performance metrics.
- **Capacitance is ignored only for short lines**; for medium and long lines it must be modelled.
- **Surge impedance loading (SIL)** = $V^2/Z_C$ is a critical EHV concept derived from $Z_C$.
- KTU Module-2 is **Concept Only** — focus on *why* each model is used, not on heavy computation.
- The **characteristic impedance** $Z_C = \sqrt{z/y}$ and **propagation constant** $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$ are the long-line essentials.
- All line parameters in KTU problems are **per phase** unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Line as Two Port Network, Derivation and Calculation of ABCD Parameters (Derivation and Numerical Problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Transmission Line as Two Port Network, Derivation and Calculation of ABCD Parameters (Derivation and Numerical Problems) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Two-Port Network** is an electrical network with two distinct pairs of terminals: an **input port** (sending end) and an **output port** (receiving end). For a transmission line, the sending end voltages and currents are linearly related to the receiving end voltages and currents through a set of four constants known as the **Generalized Circuit Constants** or **ABCD Parameters** (also called **Transmission Parameters** or **Chain Parameters**). The defining equations of a two-port network representation of a transmission line are: $$V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R$$ $$I_S = C \cdot V_R + D \cdot I_R$$ In matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} V_S \\ I_S \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_R \\ I_R \end{bmatrix}$$ Where: - **$V_S$, $I_S$** = Sending end voltage (phase-to-neutral) and current - **$V_R$, $I_R$** = Receiving end voltage (phase-to-neutral) and current - **A, B, C, D** = Complex dimensionless/ohms/mhos constants of the line > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For any passive, linear, bilateral transmission line, the ABCD parameters are **reciprocal** in nature, satisfying the identity $AD - BC = 1$. Additionally, for a symmetrical line, $A = D$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a transmission line as a **black box translator** between two cities. Imagine you speak into a telephone (sending end: $V_S$, $I_S$) and your friend hears your voice at the other end (receiving end: $V_R$, $I_R$). The telephone cable itself doesn't know what the message is, but it **transforms** the signal in a predictable way. The ABCD parameters act as a **translation key**: - **Parameter A** — Voltage ratio with output open-circuited ($I_R = 0$). Tells you how much voltage "leaks" or is transferred when no current flows. - **Parameter B** — Transfer impedance with output short-circuited ($V_R = 0$). Represents the **opposition** the line offers to current flow. - **Parameter C** — Transfer admittance with output open-circuited ($I_R = 0$). Represents the line's **leakage** (shunt paths) to ground. - **Parameter D** — Current ratio with output short-circuited ($V_R = 0$). Tells you how sending end current relates to receiving end current. ## 1.3 Standard Transmission Line Constants The line is characterized by four distributed parameters per unit length: | Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Description | |-----------|--------|------|-------------| | Resistance | $R$ | $\Omega$/km | Conductor ohmic loss | | Inductance | $L$ | H/km | Magnetic field storage | | Capacitance | $C$ | F/km | Electric field storage | | Conductance | $G$ | S/km | Dielectric leakage loss | Total series impedance: $Z = R + j\omega L$ (Ω) Total shunt admittance: $Y = G + j\omega C$ (S) > [!NOTE] > These are the **per-phase** values for a three-phase line, and all ABCD derivations are done on a **per-phase basis** (phase-to-neutral equivalent). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Significance of Each Parameter The ABCD parameters are not arbitrary — each has a precise physical meaning derivable from open-circuit and short-circuit tests: **Open-Circuit Test ($I_R = 0$):** $$A = \left.\frac{V_S}{V_R}\right|_{I_R=0} \quad \text{(dimensionless voltage ratio)}$$ $$C = \left.\frac{I_S}{V_R}\right|_{I_R=0} \quad \text{(mhos, line-charging admittance)}$$ **Short-Circuit Test ($V_R = 0$):** $$B = \left.\frac{V_S}{I_R}\right|_{V_R=0} \quad \text{(ohms, short-circuit transfer impedance)}$$ $$D = \left.\frac{I_S}{I_R}\right|_{V_R=0} \quad \text{(dimensionless current ratio)}$$ ## 2.2 Operational Logic Flow 1. **Step 1 — Identify Line Model:** Choose between *Short Line* (lumped Z only), *Medium Line* (Nominal-T or Nominal-π), or *Long Line* (exact hyperbolic solution). 2. **Step 2 — Calculate Total Z and Y:** Multiply per-km values by line length $l$. 3. **Step 3 — Apply KVL and KCL:** Use network theorems on the equivalent circuit. 4. **Step 4 — Solve Simultaneous Equations:** Express $V_S$ and $I_S$ purely in terms of $V_R$ and $I_R$. 5. **Step 5 — Extract Coefficients:** Compare with standard ABCD form to obtain A, B, C, D. 6. **Step 6 — Verify Reciprocity:** Check $AD - BC = 1$ (for passive bilateral network). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Line Model | A | B | C | D | |------------|---|---|---|---| | **Short Line** | $1$ | $Z$ | $0$ | $1$ | | **Nominal-T** | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | $Z\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$ | $Y$ | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | | **Nominal-π** | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | $Z$ | $Y\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$ | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | | **Long Line (Exact)** | $\cosh(\gamma l)$ | $Z_c \sinh(\gamma l)$ | $\dfrac{\sinh(\gamma l)}{Z_c}$ | $\cosh(\gamma l)$ | **Supporting Definitions for Long Line:** | Quantity | Formula | Meaning | |----------|---------|---------| | Propagation Constant | $\gamma = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}$ | $\text{km}^{-1}$ | | Characteristic Impedance | $Z_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$ | $\Omega$ | | Surge Impedance Loading | $\text{SIL} = \dfrac{\vert V_L \vert^2}{Z_c}$ | MW (3-phase) | **Universal Identity (for all passive bilateral lines):** $$AD - BC = 1$$ **Symmetry Condition (for uniform lines):** $$A = D$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in engineering:** The ABCD framework enables **cascading** of transmission sections, transformers, and loads through simple matrix multiplication, making it the foundation of modern power system analysis and load-flow studies. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Network Reduction:** Multiple transmission sections, transformers, and series compensators are concatenated as $\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix}_{total} = \begin{bmatrix} A_1 & B_1 \\ C_1 & D_1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} A_2 & B_2 \\ C_2 & D_2 \end{bmatrix}$ - **Voltage Regulation Studies:** Helps compute $\%V.R. = \dfrac{\vert V_R \vert_{NL} - \vert V_R \vert_{FL}}{\vert V_R \vert_{FL}} \times 100$ - **Efficiency Calculation:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100 = \dfrac{V_R I_R \cos\phi_R}{V_S I_S \cos\phi_S} \times 100$ - **Protection Coordination:** Short-circuit MVA at any point is computed from $V_S$ and the ABCD chain. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation for Short Transmission Line (Lumped Z) The short line is modelled as a single series impedance $Z$ with no shunt admittance. **Circuit Equations (by KVL):** $$V_S = V_R + I_R \cdot Z$$ $$I_S = I_R$$ **Converting to ABCD form:** $$V_S = (1) \cdot V_R + (Z) \cdot I_R$$ $$I_S = (0) \cdot V_R + (1) \cdot I_R$$ $$\boxed{A = 1, \quad B = Z, \quad C = 0, \quad D = 1}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation for Medium Transmission Line — Nominal-T Model ### Circuit Configuration Total series impedance $Z$ is split into two halves ($Z/2$ at each end), and the entire shunt admittance $Y$ is lumped at the centre node. ### Step 1: Define the Middle Node Let the middle node be $M$ with voltage $V_M$. The series current flowing from $S$ to $R$ is the same throughout (no shunt diversion except at M): $I_{series} = I_S = I_R$ ... but wait — at node M, current diverts into $Y$. **Correcting this:** Current in the left half (between $S$ and $M$) is $I_S$. Current in the right half (between $M$ and $R$) is $I_R$. Current through the shunt $Y$ is $I_Y$. **KCL at node M:** $$I_S = I_Y + I_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_Y = I_S - I_R$$ ### Step 2: Voltage at the Middle Node Walking from receiving end to middle node: $$V_M = V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$ Walking from middle node to sending end: $$V_M = V_S - I_S \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$ ### Step 3: KVL — Derive $V_S$ Equation $$V_S = V_M + I_S \cdot \frac{Z}{2} = \left(V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2}\right) + I_S \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$ $$V_S = V_R + \frac{Z}{2}(I_R + I_S) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ ### Step 4: KCL — Derive $I_S$ Equation Current through the central shunt admittance: $$I_Y = V_M \cdot Y = \left(V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2}\right) Y$$ Applying KCL at M: $$I_S = I_Y + I_R = V_R Y + I_R \cdot \frac{YZ}{2} + I_R$$ $$I_S = V_R \cdot Y + I_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **From (ii), direct extraction:** $$C = Y, \quad D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}$$ ### Step 5: Substitute $I_S$ in Equation (i) $$V_S = V_R + \frac{Z}{2} \cdot I_R + \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \left[V_R Y + I_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)\right]$$ $$V_S = V_R + \frac{Z}{2} I_R + \frac{ZY}{2} V_R + \frac{Z}{2} I_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)$$ $$V_S = V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \left[\frac{Z}{2} + \frac{Z}{2} + \frac{Z^2 Y}{4}\right]$$ $$V_S = V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \left[Z + \frac{YZ^2}{4}\right]$$ $$V_S = V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \cdot Z \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$ **Extraction of A and B:** $$\boxed{A = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)}$$ ### Step 6: Reciprocity Verification $$AD - BC = \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) - Z\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) \cdot Y$$ $$= 1 + YZ + \frac{Y^2 Z^2}{4} - YZ - \frac{Y^2 Z^2}{4} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ ## 3.3 Derivation for Medium Transmission Line — Nominal-π Model ### Circuit Configuration Total shunt admittance $Y$ is split into two halves ($Y/2$ at each end), and the entire series impedance $Z$ is in the middle. ### Step 1: Define Branch Currents - $I_Z$ = current through the central series $Z$ - $I_{Y1}$ = current through sending-end shunt $Y/2$ - $I_{Y2}$ = current through receiving-end shunt $Y/2$ Branch relations (using Ohm's law on each element): $$I_Z = \frac{V_S - V_R}{Z}, \quad I_{Y1} = V_S \cdot \frac{Y}{2}, \quad I_{Y2} = V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2}$$ ### Step 2: KCL at the Sending End $$I_S = I_Z + I_{Y1} = \frac{V_S - V_R}{Z} + V_S \cdot \frac{Y}{2} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ ### Step 3: KCL at the Receiving End $$I_Z = I_R + I_{Y2} = I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ ### Step 4: Derive $V_S$ from (iv) From (iv): $$V_S - V_R = Z \cdot I_Z = Z \left(I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2}\right) = Z I_R + \frac{YZ}{2} V_R$$ $$V_S = V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + Z \cdot I_R \quad \text{...(v)}$$ **Direct extraction:** $$A = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z$$ ### Step 5: Derive $I_S$ Using (iii) and (v) From (iii): $$I_S = \frac{V_S - V_R}{Z} + V_S \cdot \frac{Y}{2}$$ Substituting $V_S$ from (v): $$V_S - V_R = V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + Z I_R - V_R = V_R \cdot \frac{YZ}{2} + Z I_R$$ Therefore: $$\frac{V_S - V_R}{Z} = V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} + I_R$$ $$I_S = \left(V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} + I_R\right) + V_S \cdot \frac{Y}{2}$$ $$I_S = \frac{Y}{2}(V_R + V_S) + I_R$$ Substituting $V_S = V_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + Z I_R$: $$V_R + V_S = V_R + V_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + Z I_R = V_R \left(2 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + Z I_R$$ $$I_S = \frac{Y}{2}\left[V_R \left(2 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + Z I_R\right] + I_R$$ $$I_S = V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} \left(2 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + \frac{YZ}{2} I_R + I_R$$ $$I_S = V_R \cdot Y \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) + I_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)$$ **Extraction of C and D:** $$\boxed{C = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right), \quad D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}}$$ ### Step 6: Reciprocity Verification $$AD - BC = \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)^2 - Z \cdot Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$ $$= 1 + YZ + \frac{Y^2 Z^2}{4} - YZ - \frac{Y^2 Z^2}{4} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ ## 3.4 Long Transmission Line — Exact Solution (Hyperbolic) For a long line, the solution to the telegrapher's equations yields: $$V_S = V_R \cosh(\gamma l) + I_R Z_c \sinh(\gamma l)$$ $$I_S = V_R \cdot \frac{\sinh(\gamma l)}{Z_c} + I_R \cosh(\gamma l)$$ **Therefore:** $$\boxed{A = D = \cosh(\gamma l), \quad B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma l), \quad C = \frac{\sinh(\gamma l)}{Z_c}}$$ Where: $$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}, \quad \gamma = \sqrt{ZY} = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Two-Port Network Conceptual Block ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeS["SENDING END PORT"] -->|"Vs, Is"| nodeBox["TWO PORT NETWORK
ABCD Parameter Block
A B
C D"] nodeBox -->|"Vr, Ir"| nodeR["RECEIVING END PORT"] nodeL["LOAD"] -.-> nodeR nodeG["GENERATOR"] -.-> nodeS ``` ## 4.2 Nominal-T Model Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Vs"] --- nodeB["Z/2"] nodeB --- nodeM["MIDDLE NODE
V_m"] nodeM --- nodeD["Z/2"] nodeD --- nodeE["Vr"] nodeM --- nodeY["Y Shunt
Total Admittance"] nodeF["Is"] -.-> nodeA nodeH["Ir"] -.-> nodeE ``` ## 4.3 Nominal-π Model Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Vs"] --- nodeM["SERIES Z"] nodeM --- nodeE["Vr"] nodeA --- nodeY1["Y/2
Sending Shunt"] nodeE --- nodeY2["Y/2
Receiving Shunt"] nodeF["Is enters"] -.-> nodeA nodeH["Ir leaves"] -.-> nodeE ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture for ABCD Parameter Determination ```mermaid flowchart TD input1["STEP 1: Identify Line Type"] --> input2["STEP 2: Compute Z and Y"] input2 --> input3["STEP 3: Apply KVL and KCL"] input3 --> input4["STEP 4: Solve for Vs and Is"] input4 --> input5["STEP 5: Extract A, B, C, D"] input5 --> input6["STEP 6: Verify AD minus BC equals 1"] input6 --> output1["FINAL ABCD MATRIX"] style input1 fill:#ffe6e6 style input2 fill:#fff2cc style input3 fill:#d9ead3 style input4 fill:#cfe2f3 style input5 fill:#d9d2e9 style input6 fill:#f4cccc style output1 fill:#b6d7a8 ``` ## 4.5 Cascaded Network Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["LINE SECTION 1
Matrix M1"] -->|"A1 B1 / C1 D1"| node2["LINE SECTION 2
Matrix M2"] node2 -->|"A2 B2 / C2 D2"| node3["TRANSFORMER
Matrix M3"] node3 -->|"A3 B3 / C3 D3"| node4["LOAD"] node1 -->|"Overall M = M1 M2 M3"| node4 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define ABCD parameters of a transmission line. Show that for a passive bilateral network, $AD - BC = 1$.** **Model Answer:** The ABCD parameters (also called generalized circuit constants or transmission parameters) relate the sending end voltage and current to the receiving end voltage and current: $$V_S = A V_R + B I_R \quad ; \quad I_S = C V_R + D I_R$$ **Proof of Reciprocity:** For a passive bilateral network, the reciprocity theorem states that if a voltage $V$ applied at port 1 produces a current $I$ at port 2, then the same voltage $V$ applied at port 2 produces the same current $I$ at port 1. Applying this to the ABCD matrix and its inverse: $$\begin{bmatrix} V_R \\ I_R \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} V_S \\ I_S \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{AD-BC}\begin{bmatrix} D & -B \\ -C & A \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} V_S \\ I_S \end{bmatrix}$$ For reciprocity, this inverse must equal the original matrix structure, which requires $\dfrac{1}{AD-BC} = 1$, hence: $$\boxed{AD - BC = 1}$$ ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Compare the Nominal-T and Nominal-π models of a medium transmission line with reference to ABCD parameters.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Nominal-T | Nominal-π | |---------|-----------|-----------| | Series $Z$ placement | Split as $Z/2$ at each end | Whole $Z$ in the middle | | Shunt $Y$ placement | Whole $Y$ at center | Split as $Y/2$ at each end | | **A** | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | | **B** | $Z\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$ | $Z$ | | **C** | $Y$ | $Y\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$ | | **D** | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | Both models give identical values of $A$ and $D$, ensuring **symmetry** ($A = D$). The B and C parameters are interchanged because of the dual structure. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] A 200 km, 3-phase, 50 Hz transmission line has the following distributed parameters per phase per km: $R = 0.15 \, \Omega$, $L = 1.2 \, \text{mH}$, $G = 0$, $C = 0.01 \, \mu\text{F}$. **(a)** Determine the ABCD parameters using the **Nominal-π model**. (7 marks) **(b)** If the receiving end delivers a load of $50$ MVA at $0.8$ p.f. lagging at $66$ kV (line), find the sending end voltage, current, **efficiency**, and **voltage regulation** of the line. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - $R = 0.15 \, \Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 1.2 \, \text{mH/km} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ H/km - $G = 0$, $C = 0.01 \times 10^{-6}$ F/km - Length $l = 200$ km, Frequency $f = 50$ Hz, $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159$ rad/s **Per-Phase Per-Km Values:** $$z = R + j\omega L = 0.15 + j(314.159)(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.15 + j0.377 \, \Omega/\text{km}$$ $$y = G + j\omega C = 0 + j(314.159)(0.01 \times 10^{-6}) = j3.1416 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{S/km}$$ **Total Values for 200 km:** $$Z = z \cdot l = (0.15 + j0.377)(200) = 30 + j75.4 \, \Omega$$ $$Y = y \cdot l = (j3.1416 \times 10^{-6})(200) = j6.283 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{S}$$ ### Part (a) — ABCD Parameters via Nominal-π **Computing $YZ$ product:** $$YZ = (j6.283 \times 10^{-4})(30 + j75.4)$$ $$= j6.283 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 + j^2 \times 6.283 \times 10^{-4} \times 75.4$$ $$= j0.01885 - 0.04737$$ $$YZ = -0.04737 + j0.01885$$ **Calculating $A$ and $D$:** $$A = D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2} = 1 + \frac{-0.04737 + j0.01885}{2}$$ $$A = D = 1 - 0.02369 + j0.00943 = 0.9763 + j0.00943$$ $$\vert A \vert = \sqrt{0.9763^2 + 0.00943^2} = 0.9763 \quad ; \quad \angle A = 0.553°$$ **Calculating $B$:** $$B = Z = 30 + j75.4 \, \Omega$$ $$\vert B \vert = \sqrt{30^2 + 75.4^2} = \sqrt{900 + 5685.16} = \sqrt{6585.16} = 81.15 \, \Omega$$ $$\angle B = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{75.4}{30}\right) = 68.30°$$ **Calculating $C$:** $$C = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$ $$\frac{YZ}{4} = \frac{-0.04737 + j0.01885}{4} = -0.01184 + j0.00471$$ $$1 + \frac{YZ}{4} = 0.9882 + j0.00471$$ $$C = (j6.283 \times 10^{-4})(0.9882 + j0.00471)$$ $$= j6.209 \times 10^{-4} + j^2 \cdot 2.959 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= -2.959 \times 10^{-6} + j6.209 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{S}$$ $$\vert C \vert \approx 6.21 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{S}, \quad \angle C \approx 90.27°$$ $$\boxed{A = D = 0.9763 \angle 0.553° \quad ; \quad B = 81.15 \angle 68.30° \, \Omega \quad ; \quad C = 6.21 \times 10^{-4} \angle 90.27° \, \text{S}}$$ **Verification:** $AD - BC = (0.9763\angle 0.553°)^2 - (81.15\angle 68.30°)(6.21 \times 10^{-4}\angle 90.27°)$ $\approx 0.9532 \angle 1.106° - 0.0504 \angle 158.57° \approx 1 \angle 0°$ ✓ ### Part (b) — Sending End Quantities, Efficiency & Regulation **Receiving End Per-Phase Values:** $$V_R = \frac{66{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 38{,}105 \, \text{V (phase)}, \quad \angle V_R = 0°$$ **Load Current:** $$I_R = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 66{,}000 \times 0.8} = 546.9 \, \text{A}$$ With lagging p.f. of 0.8: $\cos\phi_R = 0.8$, $\sin\phi_R = 0.6$ $$I_R = 546.9 \angle -36.87° \, \text{A}$$ **Sending End Voltage (per phase):** $$V_S = A V_R + B I_R$$ $$= (0.9763 \angle 0.553°)(38{,}105 \angle 0°) + (81.15 \angle 68.30°)(546.9 \angle -36.87°)$$ $$= 37{,}200 \angle 0.553° + 44{,}381 \angle 31.43°$$ Converting to rectangular: - $V_{R-term} = 37{,}200(0.99995) + j37{,}200(0.00965) = 37{,}197 + j359$ - $V_{B-term} = 44{,}381(0.853) + j44{,}381(0.521) = 37{,}857 + j23,123$ $$V_S = (37{,}197 + 37{,}857) + j(359 + 23{,}123) = 75{,}054 + j23{,}482$$ $$\vert V_S \vert = \sqrt{75{,}054^2 + 23{,}482^2} = 78{,}645 \, \text{V (phase)}$$ **Sending End Line Voltage:** $$V_{S,line} = 78{,}645 \times \sqrt{3} = 136{,}220 \, \text{V} \approx 136.22 \, \text{kV}$$ $$\angle V_S = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{23{,}482}{75{,}054}\right) = 17.39°$$ **Sending End Current:** $$I_S = C V_R + D I_R$$ $$= (6.21 \times 10^{-4} \angle 90.27°)(38{,}105 \angle 0°) + (0.9763 \angle 0.553°)(546.9 \angle -36.87°)$$ $$= 23.66 \angle 90.27° + 533.9 \angle -36.32°$$ Converting to rectangular: - $I_{C-term} = 0 + j23.66$ - $I_{D-term} = 533.9(0.8056) - j533.9(0.5925) = 430.13 - j316.39$ $$I_S = 430.13 + j(-316.39 + 23.66) = 430.13 - j292.73$$ $$\vert I_S \vert = \sqrt{430.13^2 + 292.73^2} = 520.0 \, \text{A}$$ $$\angle I_S = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-292.73}{430.13}\right) = -34.23°$$ **Sending End Power Factor:** $$\cos\phi_S = \cos(17.39° - (-34.23°)) = \cos(51.62°) = 0.6207$$ **Sending End Power:** $$P_S = 3 \cdot V_{S,phase} \cdot I_S \cdot \cos\phi_S = 3 \times 78{,}645 \times 520 \times 0.6207$$ $$P_S = 76.13 \, \text{MW}$$ **Receiving End Power:** $$P_R = 3 \cdot V_{R,phase} \cdot I_R \cdot \cos\phi_R = 3 \times 38{,}105 \times 546.9 \times 0.8$$ $$P_R = 50.0 \, \text{MW}$$ **Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100 = \frac{50.0}{76.13} \times 100 = 65.67\%$$ **Voltage Regulation:** $$\% V.R. = \frac{\vert V_R \vert_{NL} - \vert V_R \vert_{FL}}{\vert V_R \vert_{FL}} \times 100$$ At no load: $V_{R,NL} = \dfrac{\vert V_S \vert}{\vert A \vert} = \dfrac{78{,}645}{0.9763} = 80{,}553$ V (phase) $$\% V.R. = \frac{80{,}553 - 38{,}105}{38{,}105} \times 100 = 111.4\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the √3 factor:** Many students use line voltage directly in per-phase ABCD equations. Always convert to **phase (line-to-neutral)** before applying ABCD. > 2. **Mixing up lagging/leading signs:** Lagging p.f. means current lags voltage (negative angle), leading means current leads (positive angle). > 3. **Unit mistakes in C parameter:** $C$ has units of **siemens (mhos)**, not dimensionless. Report magnitude in S or $\mu$S. > 4. **Forgetting to verify $AD - BC = 1$:** Examiners often award 1-2 marks for this cross-check step. > 5. **Voltage regulation formula:** Use $\dfrac{\vert V_S \vert / \vert A \vert - \vert V_R \vert_{FL}}{\vert V_R \vert_{FL}}$, not sending end values. --- ### Question B (14 Marks Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive the ABCD parameters of a **medium transmission line using the Nominal-T method**. Show all steps. (7 marks) **(b)** A single-circuit 3-phase 100 km line has $R = 0.25 \, \Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 1.0$ mH/km, negligible $G$, and $C = 0.015 \, \mu\text{F/km}$ at 50 Hz. Using the **Nominal-π model**, find: (i) the ABCD parameters, (ii) the sending end voltage when the line delivers 25 MVA at 0.85 p.f. lagging to a 33 kV load. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** ### Part (a) — Derivation (Nominal-T) **Step 1:** Identify the circuit: Series $Z/2$ → Shunt $Y$ (middle) → Series $Z/2$. **Step 2:** Apply KCL at the middle node: $I_S = I_Y + I_R \Rightarrow I_Y = I_S - I_R$ **Step 3:** Express middle node voltage: $V_M = V_R + I_R \cdot \dfrac{Z}{2}$ **Step 4:** Apply KVL from $S$ to $R$: $$V_S = V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2} + I_S \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$ **Step 5:** Apply Ohm's law on shunt: $I_Y = V_M \cdot Y = \left(V_R + I_R \cdot \dfrac{Z}{2}\right)Y$ **Step 6:** Combine: $$I_S = V_R \cdot Y + I_R \cdot \frac{YZ}{2} + I_R = V_R Y + I_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)$$ **Step 7:** Substitute $I_S$ in KVL equation: $$V_S = V_R + \frac{Z}{2}I_R + \frac{Z}{2}\left[V_R Y + I_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)\right]$$ $$V_S = V_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R\left[Z + \frac{YZ^2}{4}\right] = V_R\left(1+\frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \cdot Z\left(1+\frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$ **Extracted ABCD parameters:** $$A = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right), \quad C = Y, \quad D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}$$ **Verification:** $AD - BC = \left(1+\dfrac{YZ}{2}\right)^2 - YZ\left(1+\dfrac{YZ}{4}\right) = 1$ ✓ ### Part (b) — Numerical **Given Data:** $R = 0.25 \, \Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ H/km, $G = 0$, $C = 0.015 \times 10^{-6}$ F/km, $l = 100$ km **Per-km:** $$z = 0.25 + j(314.159)(0.001) = 0.25 + j0.3142 \, \Omega/\text{km}$$ $$y = j(314.159)(0.015 \times 10^{-6}) = j4.712 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{S/km}$$ **Total for 100 km:** $$Z = 25 + j31.42 \, \Omega, \quad Y = j4.712 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{S}$$ **$YZ$ product:** $$YZ = (j4.712 \times 10^{-4})(25 + j31.42) = -0.01481 + j0.01178$$ **(i) ABCD Parameters:** $$A = D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2} = 1 - 0.007405 + j0.00589 = 0.9926 + j0.00589$$ $$A = 0.9926 \angle 0.34°$$ $$B = Z = 25 + j31.42 = 40.02 \angle 51.49° \, \Omega$$ $$C = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) = (j4.712 \times 10^{-4})(1 - 0.00370 + j0.00295)$$ $$1 + \frac{YZ}{4} = 0.9963 + j0.00295 \approx 0.9963 \angle 0.17°$$ $$C = (4.712 \times 10^{-4} \angle 90°)(0.9963 \angle 0.17°) = 4.694 \times 10^{-4} \angle 90.17° \, \text{S}$$ **(ii) Sending End Voltage:** Receiving end phase voltage: $V_R = \dfrac{33{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 19{,}053 \angle 0°$ V Load current: $I_R = \dfrac{25 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 33{,}000} = 437.4$ A With p.f. 0.85 lagging: $I_R = 437.4 \angle -31.79°$ A $$V_S = A V_R + B I_R$$ $$= (0.9926 \angle 0.34°)(19{,}053 \angle 0°) + (40.02 \angle 51.49°)(437.4 \angle -31.79°)$$ $$= 18{,}912 \angle 0.34° + 17{,}505 \angle 19.70°$$ Converting to rectangular: - $V_A = 18{,}912(0.99998) + j18{,}912(0.00593) = 18{,}911.2 + j112.1$ - $V_B = 17{,}505(0.9413) + j17{,}505(0.3373) = 16{,}476.4 + j5{,}904.4$ $$V_S = 35{,}387.6 + j6{,}016.5$$ $$\vert V_S \vert = \sqrt{35{,}387.6^2 + 6{,}016.5^2} = 35{,}896 \, \text{V (phase)}$$ $$V_{S,line} = \sqrt{3} \times 35{,}896 = 62{,}175 \, \text{V} \approx 62.18 \, \text{kV}$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:** - ☐ **Two-port representation** of a transmission line: $V_S = A V_R + B I_R$; $I_S = C V_R + D I_R$ - ☐ **Reciprocity:** $AD - BC = 1$ for all passive bilateral lines — always verify in numericals - ☐ **Symmetry:** $A = D$ for uniform transmission lines - ☐ **Short line:** $A = D = 1$, $B = Z$, $C = 0$ (no shunt admittance) - ☐ **Nominal-T:** $B = Z(1 + YZ/4)$, $C = Y$ (shunt concentrated) - ☐ **Nominal-π:** $B = Z$, $C = Y(1 + YZ/4)$ (series concentrated) - ☐ **Both models give identical $A$ and $D$** - ☐ **Long line (exact):** $A = D = \cosh(\gamma l)$, $B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma l)$, $C = \sinh(\gamma l)/Z_c$ - ☐ **Z_c (characteristic impedance):** $\sqrt{(R + j\omega L)/(G + j\omega C)}$ - ☐ **γ (propagation constant):** $\sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}$ - ☐ **Always work with per-phase (line-to-neutral) values**; convert line voltage using $\div \sqrt{3}$ - ☐ **Lagging p.f. → negative current angle**, leading → positive angle - ☐ **Voltage regulation:** $\%\text{VR} = \dfrac{\vert V_R \vert_{NL} - \vert V_R \vert_{FL}}{\vert V_R \vert_{FL}} \times 100 = \dfrac{\vert V_S/A \vert - \vert V_R \vert_{FL}}{\vert V_R \vert_{FL}} \times 100$ - ☐ **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{3 V_R I_R \cos\phi_R}{3 V_S I_S \cos\phi_S} \times 100 = \dfrac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100$ - ☐ **Cascading rule:** $\mathbf{M}_{total} = \mathbf{M}_1 \cdot \mathbf{M}_2 \cdot \mathbf{M}_3$ (matrix multiplication in order) - ☐ **Units to remember:** A, D → dimensionless; B → Ω; C → S (mhos) > [!NOTE] > **Master Tip for KTU 2024:** The ABCD parameter matrix is the **gateway to advanced power system analysis** — load flow, fault analysis, and stability studies all rely on this two-port framework. Memorize the four standard model formulas and the reciprocity identity — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper on this module.

topics.title: Skin Effect and Ferranti Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Skin Effect and Ferranti Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Skin Effect and Ferranti Effect

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Skin Effect — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Skin Effect** is the phenomenon in which the alternating current (AC) flowing through a conductor is not uniformly distributed over its entire cross-sectional area, but instead tends to concentrate (or crowd) near the **outer surface (skin)** of the conductor, leaving the inner core carrying relatively little current. As a direct consequence, the **effective AC resistance of the conductor becomes higher than its DC resistance.**

This effect becomes significant at power frequencies (**50 Hz** in India, **60 Hz** in the US) when conductor diameters are large, and it is the fundamental reason why hollow conductors (or ACSR — Aluminium Conductor Steel Reinforced) are used in EHV transmission lines.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy for Skin Effect

Imagine a busy multi-lane highway (the conductor's cross-section). During a thunderstorm (high frequency), all drivers want to drive on the **outer lanes** because the inner lanes feel "pressured" by swirling winds (the magnetic field). Similarly, in a conductor carrying AC, the inner filaments "feel" more self-induced EMF opposing their current, so current is pushed to the outer filaments.

A simpler analogy: think of water flowing through a pipe. At low pressure (DC), water fills the pipe uniformly. At high-frequency pulsating pressure (AC), water tends to flow along the walls due to inertia and friction dynamics — analogous to the current crowding at the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constant to remember:** The relative permeability of free space $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ and the relative permeability of copper is approximately **0.999994** (essentially non-magnetic, but the effect still arises from the magnetic field produced by the current itself).

---

### 1.3 Ferranti Effect — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ferranti Effect** is the phenomenon in long transmission lines (especially Extra-High-Voltage / EHV lines and underground cables) where the **voltage at the receiving end (load end)** is observed to be **higher than the voltage at the sending end** under **light-load or no-load conditions** (typically when the line is energized but little or no power is being drawn).

This effect was first observed by **Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti** in the late 19th century during early AC power transmission experiments in London.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy for Ferranti Effect

Picture a long garden hose (the transmission line) with water flowing through it. If the hose is very long and has elastic walls (capacitance), even with the tap (sending end) only slightly open, the water pressure (voltage) at the far end (receiving end) builds up because the hose's elasticity stores energy — sometimes higher than at the tap.

In electrical terms: the line's **shunt capacitance** stores charge, and this charging current flowing through the line's **series inductance** causes a voltage rise at the far end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering metric:** The Ferranti effect becomes practically significant for lines longer than approximately **200 km** operating at voltages above **220 kV**, and is critically important in 400 kV, 765 kV, and 1200 kV UHV systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Skin Effect — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Root Cause: Differential Flux Linkage

Consider a solid cylindrical conductor divided into an infinite number of tiny filaments. Let us analyse why the current distribution becomes non-uniform.

- **Outer filaments** (near the surface) link with only a portion of the total magnetic flux produced by the current.
- **Inner filaments** (near the core) link with the **entire flux** produced by the current — both the flux from outer filaments AND from the inner filaments themselves.
- The **self-inductance** of an inner filament is therefore **greater** than that of an outer filament.
- A higher inductance means a **higher inductive reactance** ($X_L = 2\pi f L$).
- Current naturally distributes inversely to impedance (in parallel paths). Therefore, the inner filaments, having higher impedance, carry **less current**, while the outer filaments carry **more current**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The skin effect is essentially a consequence of **Lenz's Law** — the self-induced EMF in the inner filaments opposes the current more strongly, pushing it outward.

### 2.1.2 Factors Affecting Skin Effect

The severity of skin effect **increases** with:

1. **Frequency** ($f$) — directly proportional; doubling frequency increases the effect significantly.
2. **Diameter of the conductor** — larger diameters mean more pronounced non-uniformity.
3. **Permeability of conductor material** ($\mu$) — higher $\mu$ means higher inductance and stronger effect.
4. **Shape of conductor** — stranded conductors show less effect than solid ones; hollow conductors show very little effect.
5. **Temperature** — has a minor influence via conductivity changes.
6. **Proximity Effect** — current distribution is further distorted when conductors are placed close to each other (carrying AC in opposite directions or same direction).

### 2.1.3 Skin Depth — The Penetration Measure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Skin Depth ($\delta$)** is defined as the distance below the surface of a conductor at which the current density has decayed to **$1/e$** (approximately **36.8%**) of its value at the surface. Equivalently, at depth $\delta$, the current density is about **36.8%** of the surface value.

### 2.1.4 Consequences of Skin Effect in Real Engineering

- **Increased $I^2R$ losses** → reduced transmission efficiency.
- **Reduced effective cross-section** → effective resistance $R_{ac} > R_{dc}$.
- Necessitates use of **ACSR conductors** (steel core for mechanical strength, aluminium strands for current carrying — the steel core contributes little to current flow).
- For very high frequencies (radio, microwave), conductors are often **plated with silver** (highest conductivity) or made hollow.
- Underground cables use **hollow conductors** to save copper.

---

## 2.2 Ferranti Effect — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Root Cause: Charging Current Through Series Inductance

A long transmission line is fundamentally a **distributed parameter network** with:

- **Series resistance** ($R$) and **series inductance** ($L$) along the length.
- **Shunt capacitance** ($C$) between the conductors and to ground.
- **Shunt conductance** ($G$, usually negligible in overhead lines).

Under **no-load or light-load** conditions:

- The receiving-end current $I_R \approx 0$.
- However, the line capacitance draws a **leading (capacitive) charging current** $I_C$ from the sending end.
- This charging current $I_C$ flows **through the series inductance** of the line.
- The voltage drop across the series inductance is $I_C \cdot X_L$, and this drop is in such a direction that it **adds to the sending-end voltage** when referred to the receiving end.
- Result: $V_R > V_S$.

### 2.2.2 Physical Interpretation

Think of the line as a long capacitor being charged from one end. The charging current leads the voltage by 90°. When this leading current passes through an inductive element (the line's series $L$), the resulting voltage drop has a component that **boosts** the voltage at the far end. The line's distributed capacitance acts as a source of reactive power (VARs) under light load, and the receiving end voltage "rises" because this reactive power cannot be absorbed by a small load.

### 2.2.3 Factors Affecting Ferranti Effect

1. **Line length** — longer lines → more pronounced effect (effect $\propto l^2$ approximately).
2. **Operating voltage** — higher voltage → higher capacitance → more charging current.
3. **Load level** — most severe at no-load; decreases as load increases.
4. **System frequency** — affects capacitive charging current.
5. **Conductor configuration** — bundled conductors increase capacitance.

### 2.2.4 Practical Methods to Mitigate Ferranti Effect

- **Shunt Reactors** — inductive loads connected at receiving end (or intermediate points) to absorb excess reactive power.
- **Synchronous Condensers** — over-excited synchronous motors acting as capacitive VAR absorbers (or inductors when under-excited).
- **Static VAR Compensators (SVCs)** — modern power-electronic-based reactive power compensation.
- **Tap-changing Transformers** — adjust sending-end voltage to control receiving-end voltage.
- **Series Compensation** — using series capacitors to reduce effective $X_L$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Tip:** Students often confuse the **direction of voltage rise**. Remember: the Ferranti effect makes $V_R > V_S$ — *not* the other way around. The voltage "rises" from sending to receiving end.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Skin Depth | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | $\delta$ in m; $f$ in Hz; $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ in H/m; $\sigma$ in S/m | Penetration depth of current |
| Skin Depth (alt. form) | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{2\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | $\omega$ in rad/s | Frequency-domain form |
| Surface Current Density | $J(z) = J_0 \, e^{-z/\delta}$ | $z$ = depth from surface; $J_0$ = surface value | Exponential decay into conductor |
| AC/DC Resistance Ratio | $R_{ac}/R_{dc} \geq 1$ | Dimensionless | Quantifies skin effect severity |
| Ferranti Rise (approx., nominal-π) | $\dfrac{V_R}{V_S} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 - \dfrac{X B}{2}}$ | $X$ = series reactance; $B$ = total shunt susceptance | Voltage rise ratio |
| Ferranti Rise (per-unit) | $\Delta V_{pu} \approx \dfrac{X B}{2}$ | Per-unit voltage rise | For small $XB/2$ values |
| Charging Current | $I_C = j \omega C V$ | $C$ in F; $V$ in V; $I_C$ in A | Source of Ferranti effect |
| Nominal-π ABCD Constants | $A = D = 1 + \dfrac{ZY}{2}$, $B = Z$, $C = Y\left(1 + \dfrac{ZY}{4}\right)$ | $Z = R + jX$, $Y = jB$ | Used to derive Ferranti formula |
| Lossless Ferranti Formula | $V_R = V_S \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos(\beta l)}$ | $\beta$ = phase constant; $l$ = line length | Exact for lossless line |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Skin Depth Expression

### Step 1 — Setup the Field Equations

Consider a semi-infinite conducting medium occupying the region $z \geq 0$, with a uniform time-harmonic field applied at the surface ($z = 0$). The fields must satisfy **Maxwell's equations** in differential form.

$$
\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu \vec{H}
$$

$$
\nabla \times \vec{H} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E} \approx \sigma \vec{E}
$$

For a good conductor at power frequency, the conduction current dominates the displacement current, so $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$.

### Step 2 — Assume Plane-Wave Propagation into Conductor

Assume the electric field is along $\hat{x}$ and depends only on $z$ (one-dimensional penetration). Thus:

$$
\vec{E} = E_x(z) \, \hat{x}, \quad \vec{H} = H_y(z) \, \hat{y}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Curl Equation

Taking the curl of $\vec{E}$ and applying it to the assumed field:

$$
\frac{dE_x}{dz} = -j\omega\mu H_y
$$

Taking the curl of $\vec{H}$:

$$
-\frac{dH_y}{dz} = \sigma E_x
$$

### Step 4 — Combine into a Single Equation for $E_x$

Differentiate the first equation with respect to $z$:

$$
\frac{d^2E_x}{dz^2} = -j\omega\mu \frac{dH_y}{dz}
$$

Substitute $\dfrac{dH_y}{dz} = -\sigma E_x$ from the second equation:

$$
\frac{d^2E_x}{dz^2} = j\omega\mu\sigma E_x
$$

### Step 5 — Define the Propagation Constant

Define the complex propagation constant $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu\sigma
$$

Taking the square root (using $\sqrt{j} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$):

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma} = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = (1+j)\alpha
$$

where $\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$ is the **attenuation constant**.

### Step 6 — Solve the Differential Equation

The general solution to the second-order ODE is:

$$
E_x(z) = E_0 \, e^{-\gamma z} = E_0 \, e^{-(1+j)\alpha z} = E_0 \, e^{-\alpha z} \, e^{-j\alpha z}
$$

The current density $J = \sigma E$ therefore has the same form.

### Step 7 — Identify the Skin Depth

The amplitude decays as $e^{-\alpha z}$. By definition, skin depth $\delta$ is the distance at which amplitude falls to $e^{-1}$ of its surface value:

$$
\alpha \delta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}
$$

Rewriting $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{2\pi f \mu\sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu\sigma}}
$$

### Step 8 — Numerical Example for Copper at 50 Hz

For copper: $\mu \approx \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^{7}$ S/m.

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 50 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^{7}}}
$$

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1.144 \times 10^{5}}} \approx 9.4\ \text{mm}
$$

This explains why at 50 Hz, the current penetrates only the outer **~9.4 mm** of a copper conductor — a 25 mm diameter copper bar wastes its inner core!

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ferranti Effect Using Nominal-π Model

### Step 1 — Represent the Line as a Nominal-π Equivalent Circuit

A medium-length transmission line can be represented by lumping the total series impedance $Z$ at the centre and dividing the total shunt admittance $Y$ into two equal halves at each end:

- $Z = R + jX$ (total series impedance of the line)
- $Y = jB = j\omega C$ (total shunt admittance, $G \approx 0$ for overhead lines)

### Step 2 — Write the General Transmission Equation

For a two-port network with ABCD parameters:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} V_S \\ I_S \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_R \\ I_R \end{bmatrix}
$$

For the nominal-π model:

$$
A = D = 1 + \dfrac{ZY}{2}, \quad B = Z, \quad C = Y\left(1 + \dfrac{ZY}{4}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the No-Load Condition

At no-load (or light load approximated as no-load), the receiving-end current is zero:

$$
I_R = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R = A \cdot V_R
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Receiving-End Voltage

$$
V_R = \dfrac{V_S}{A} = \dfrac{V_S}{1 + \dfrac{ZY}{2}}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute for Lossless Line

For a typical overhead line, $R \ll X$ and $G \approx 0$, so:

$$
Z = jX, \quad Y = jB
$$

Compute $ZY$:

$$
ZY = (jX)(jB) = j^2 XB = -XB
$$

Therefore:

$$
A = 1 + \dfrac{-XB}{2} = 1 - \dfrac{XB}{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Derive the Voltage Rise

$$
V_R = \dfrac{V_S}{1 - \dfrac{XB}{2}}
$$

Since $X > 0$ and $B > 0$, the denominator $\left(1 - \dfrac{XB}{2}\right) < 1$ (assuming $XB/2 < 1$, which holds for lines shorter than quarter-wave length).

**Therefore, the magnitude of $V_R$ is GREATER than the magnitude of $V_S$.** This is the Ferranti effect.

### Step 7 — Linearise for Small Voltage Rise

For small values of $\dfrac{XB}{2}$ (typical of practical lines), use the binomial approximation:

$$
\dfrac{1}{1-x} \approx 1 + x + x^2 + \cdots
$$

$$
V_R \approx V_S \left(1 + \dfrac{XB}{2}\right)
$$

### Step 8 — Express the Per-Unit Voltage Rise

$$
\Delta V_{pu} = \dfrac{V_R - V_S}{V_S} \approx \dfrac{XB}{2}
$$

### Step 9 — Substitute Physical Quantities

Recall $X = \omega L$ and $B = \omega C$. For a line of length $l$:

$$
XB = \omega^2 LC \propto l^2
$$

This shows the **Ferranti effect is proportional to the square of the line length**, which is why it becomes a serious problem for very long EHV/UHV lines (typically 400 km and above).

### Step 10 — Phasor Interpretation

At no-load, the charging current $I_C = j\omega C V$ leads $V_S$ by 90°. This current flowing through the series inductance $L$ produces a voltage drop $I_C \cdot jX_L$ that is **in phase with $V_S$** (since $j \times j = -1$ and then with a sign convention, it adds). Hence, $V_R$ exceeds $V_S$ in magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
