# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define skin effect. State any four factors that affect the skin effect in transmission line conductors.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Skin effect is the phenomenon in which alternating current flowing through a conductor is not distributed uniformly over its entire cross-sectional area, but instead tends to crowd towards the outer surface (skin) of the conductor. This results in an increase in the effective AC resistance of the conductor compared to its DC resistance.

**Four factors affecting skin effect:**

1. **Frequency of supply** — Skin effect increases with increase in frequency. At 50 Hz the effect is mild, but at high frequencies (kHz, MHz) it is very pronounced.
2. **Diameter of conductor** — Larger diameter conductors exhibit greater skin effect because the difference in flux linkage between inner and outer filaments is more.
3. **Permeability of conductor material** — Higher permeability ($\mu$) increases the effect since inductance is proportional to $\mu$.
4. **Shape of conductor** — Stranded conductors show less skin effect than solid ones; hollow conductors exhibit the least skin effect.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid (first letters): F-D-P-S** = **F**requency, **D**iameter, **P**ermeability, **S**hape (and also **T**emperature, **P**roximity effect).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is Ferranti effect? Under what operating conditions is it most pronounced?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Ferranti effect is the phenomenon observed in long transmission lines (especially EHV and UHV lines) where the voltage at the receiving end is found to be higher than the voltage at the sending end, under no-load or light-load conditions.

**Conditions for pronounced Ferranti effect:**

- **Long line length** — the effect increases approximately as the square of the line length; hence, it is significant for lines beyond 200 km.
- **High operating voltage** — higher voltage produces higher charging current.
- **No-load or very light load** — when the load cannot absorb the reactive power generated by the line capacitance.
- **High system frequency** — increases capacitive charging current.

The effect is mitigated by using **shunt reactors**, **synchronous condensers**, or **SVCs** at the receiving end.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of skin effect in conductors carrying alternating current. Derive the expression for skin depth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper conductor has a diameter of 2 cm. Given: $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m and $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^{7}$ S/m, calculate the skin depth at 50 Hz. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Explanation of Skin Effect:**

When an alternating current flows through a solid conductor, the current does not remain uniformly distributed across the cross-section. The current density is maximum at the surface and decreases exponentially towards the centre. This happens because the inner filaments of the conductor link with a larger amount of magnetic flux (produced by the current in all other filaments) compared to the outer filaments. This results in a higher self-induced EMF (back EMF) in the inner filaments, which opposes the current flow more strongly there. Consequently, the inner filaments carry less current and the outer filaments carry more current.

**Derivation of Skin Depth:**

Consider a semi-infinite conducting medium. The field equations inside a good conductor are:

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j\omega\mu\sigma \vec{E}
$$

For one-dimensional penetration along $z$:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 E_x}{dz^2} = j\omega\mu\sigma E_x = \gamma^2 E_x
$$

where $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma} = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$.

The general solution is:

$$
E_x(z) = E_0 \, e^{-\gamma z} = E_0 \, e^{-(1+j)\alpha z}
$$

where $\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$.

The amplitude decays as $e^{-\alpha z}$. By definition, skin depth $\delta$ is the depth at which the amplitude falls to $1/e$ of its surface value:

$$
\alpha \delta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Diameter = 2 cm = 0.02 m, so radius = 0.01 m
- $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^{7}$ S/m
- $f = 50$ Hz

**Calculation:**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 50 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^{7}}}
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
\pi \times 50 = 157.08
$$

$$
4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^{7} = 4\pi \times 5.8 = 72.88
$$

$$
157.08 \times 72.88 = 11{,}449.5
$$

$$
\sqrt{11{,}449.5} = 107.0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{107.0} \approx 9.35 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 9.35\ \text{mm}
$$

**Comment:**

The conductor radius is 10 mm, but the skin depth is only 9.35 mm. This means the current effectively penetrates almost the entire radius — but for a 4 cm diameter conductor (radius 20 mm), the inner 10.65 mm of the radius would carry very little current, leading to a substantial increase in effective AC resistance. This is why, for large conductors, **hollow conductors** or **ACSR conductors** (with a non-current-carrying steel core) are economically used.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain Ferranti effect with a neat phasor diagram. Derive the expression for the receiving-end voltage under no-load using a nominal-π model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 400 kV, 400 km long transmission line has a total series reactance of 80 Ω and total shunt admittance of $1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ S. Using the nominal-π model, calculate the receiving-end voltage when the sending-end voltage is 400 kV at no-load. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Explanation with Phasor Diagram:**

The Ferranti effect arises due to the charging current of the line's shunt capacitance flowing through the line's series inductance. Under no-load, the receiving-end current $I_R = 0$, but the shunt capacitance draws a leading current $I_C$. This current passes through the line inductance, producing a voltage drop that adds to the sending-end voltage when viewed from the receiving end.

**Phasor Diagram (verbal description):**
- Take $V_R$ as the reference phasor (horizontal).
- $I_C$ leads $V_R$ by 90° (capacitive).
- Voltage drop across series inductance $I_C \cdot jX_L$ leads $I_C$ by 90°, i.e., it is in anti-phase direction relative to $V_R$ but with magnitude that effectively raises the total.
- $V_S = V_R + I_C \cdot Z$ — but since $I_C$ leads and $Z$ is inductive, the phasor $V_S$ ends up **shorter** than $V_R$, implying $|V_R| > |V_S|$.

**Derivation:**

For nominal-π model, the ABCD constants are:

$$
A = 1 + \dfrac{ZY}{2}, \quad B = Z, \quad C = Y\left(1 + \dfrac{ZY}{4}\right), \quad D = A
$$

Under no-load, $I_R = 0$:

$$
V_S = A V_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_R = \dfrac{V_S}{A}
$$

For a lossless line, $Z = jX$ and $Y = jB$:

$$
A = 1 + \dfrac{(jX)(jB)}{2} = 1 - \dfrac{XB}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_R = \dfrac{V_S}{1 - \dfrac{XB}{2}}
$$

Since $XB/2$ is positive and less than 1, the denominator is less than 1, making $V_R > V_S$. This is the Ferranti effect.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $V_S = 400$ kV
- $X = 80$ Ω (total series reactance)
- $B = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ S (total shunt susceptance)
- No-load condition

**Calculation:**

First, compute $XB/2$:

$$
\dfrac{XB}{2} = \dfrac{80 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \dfrac{0.128}{2} = 0.064
$$

Now compute $V_R$:

$$
V_R = \dfrac{V_S}{1 - 0.064} = \dfrac{400}{0.936} = 427.35\ \text{kV}
$$

**Voltage Rise:**

$$
\Delta V = V_R - V_S = 427.35 - 400 = 27.35\ \text{kV}
$$

**Per-unit rise:**

$$
\Delta V_{pu} = \dfrac{27.35}{400} = 0.0684\ \text{pu} = 6.84\%
$$

**Comment:**

A 6.84% voltage rise is significant for a 400 kV system (acceptable limit is typically ±5%). This illustrates why **shunt reactors** are mandatory at the receiving end of 400 kV and higher voltage lines. The result also confirms that the Ferranti effect is a real engineering concern for EHV transmission.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing skin effect with proximity effect.** Skin effect is internal to a single conductor; proximity effect is the influence of adjacent conductors. Do not interchange the terms.
> 2. **Using $R_{ac} < R_{dc}$:** Some students mistakenly think skin effect reduces resistance. **Always:** $R_{ac} \geq R_{dc}$, with equality only at DC.
> 3. **Direction of voltage in Ferranti effect:** Always $V_R > V_S$ at no-load, NOT the reverse. The voltage rises from sending to receiving end.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the denominator:** When using the nominal-π model, $A = 1 + ZY/2$ has the factor of **2**, not 1. Missing this gives wrong answers.
> 5. **Sign error in $ZY$ product:** $ZY = (jX)(jB) = -XB$ (a negative real number). Forgetting the $j^2 = -1$ leads to wrong polarity and students may end up showing $V_R < V_S$.
> 6. **Units in skin depth:** Final answer must be in **metres** (or mm). Do not leave it in $\sqrt{1/(\text{Hz} \cdot \text{H/m} \cdot \text{S/m})}$ without unit conversion.
> 7. **Proximity effect omission:** When asked about "effects reducing transmission efficiency," always mention both skin AND proximity effects for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Skin Effect & Ferranti Effect**

### Skin Effect — Key Points

- ✔ **Definition:** Non-uniform AC current distribution with concentration at conductor surface.
- ✔ **Cause:** Differential flux linkage → higher inductance in inner filaments → current pushed outward.
- ✔ **Skin depth formula:** $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$.
- ✔ For copper at 50 Hz: $\delta \approx 9.4$ mm.
- ✔ **Consequence:** $R_{ac} > R_{dc}$ → increased $I^2R$ losses.
- ✔ **Mitigation:** Use ACSR conductors, stranded conductors, or hollow conductors for very high frequencies.
- ✔ **Related concept:** Proximity effect (interaction between adjacent conductors).
- ✔ **Affected by:** Frequency, diameter, permeability, shape, temperature.

### Ferranti Effect — Key Points

- ✔ **Definition:** $V_R > V_S$ at no-load/light-load in long EHV lines.
- ✔ **Cause:** Capacitive charging current through series inductance.
- ✔ **Voltage rise formula:** $V_R = \dfrac{V_S}{1 - \dfrac{XB}{2}}$ (nominal-π, lossless).
- ✔ **Per-unit rise:** $\Delta V_{pu} \approx \dfrac{XB}{2} \propto l^2$.
- ✔ **Worst at:** Long lines, high voltage, no-load, high frequency.
- ✔ **Mitigation:** Shunt reactors, synchronous condensers, SVCs, tap-changing transformers.
- ✔ **Critical for:** 400 kV, 765 kV, 1200 kV UHV systems; underground cables.
- ✔ **Phasor direction:** Charging current leads $V_S$ by 90°; voltage drop in $X_L$ adds to $V_S$.

### Quick Numerical Reminders

- ✔ $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- ✔ $\sigma_{copper} \approx 5.8 \times 10^{7}$ S/m
- ✔ $\sigma_{aluminium} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{7}$ S/m
- ✔ Indian power frequency: **50 Hz**
- ✔ Ferranti effect threshold: lines $> 200$ km, voltages $\geq 220$ kV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:**
> - **Skin Effect** = *Current moves to Skin* (surface crowding due to flux)
> - **Ferranti Effect** = *Voltage Rises at Far End* (charging current boosts V at receiving end under no-load)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corona (Qualitative Study Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Corona

> [!NOTE]
> **Corona** is the phenomenon of partial ionization and local breakdown of the surrounding air medium (dielectric) in the immediate vicinity of a high-voltage transmission line conductor, occurring when the potential gradient at the conductor surface exceeds the dielectric strength of air (approximately **30 kV/cm peak** or **21.1 kV/cm rms** under standard atmospheric conditions).

The corona discharge is characterized by a faint **violet-blue glow**, a **hissing sound**, the production of **ozone gas (O₃)**, and the release of **energy in the form of heat, light, and sound**. It represents a continuous, incomplete electrical discharge confined to the region immediately surrounding the conductor, distinct from a complete flashover or arc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates only a **qualitative study** of corona. Numerical derivations and detailed mathematical analysis are **not** required. Focus on conceptual understanding, physical mechanism, governing factors, and practical significance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are inflating a balloon. As the balloon expands, the rubber surface stretches thinner, and tiny cracks or pores start appearing on the surface — air slowly leaks through these imperfections, making a hissing sound. **Corona behaves similarly**: when the electrical "pressure" (voltage gradient) on a conductor surface becomes too intense, the air molecules surrounding the conductor cannot hold back any longer. They **ionize and break apart**, leaking electrical energy in the form of light, heat, sound, and chemical reactions.

A more vivid engineering analogy is the phenomenon of **water droplets evaporating from a hot iron**. The iron surface (conductor) gets so hot (high potential gradient) that it disrupts the surrounding water molecules (air molecules), causing localized activity (corona discharge) at the surface.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard reference values are essential for qualitative understanding:
> - **Dielectric strength of air at NTP**: 30 kV/cm (peak) or 21.1 kV/cm (rms)
> - **Standard atmospheric conditions**: Pressure = 76 cm of Hg, Temperature = 25 °C
> - **Air Density Factor (δ) reference value**: δ = 1.0 at standard conditions
> - **Critical disruptive voltage gradient (g₀)**: 30 kV/cm (peak)
> - **Color of corona discharge**: Violet/Purple (in dark), Hissing sound audible

## 1.4 Classification of Voltage Gradients in Corona

| Voltage Category | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Disruptive Critical Voltage | $V_d$ | Voltage at which corona **just begins** to form (inaudible, invisible) |
| Visual Critical Voltage | $V_v$ | Voltage at which corona becomes **visually observable** as a faint glow |

> [!NOTE]
> $V_v > V_d$. The visual critical voltage is always higher than the disruptive critical voltage because visible light emission requires greater ionization energy than the initial breakdown of air.

## 1.5 Mechanism of Corona Formation — A Qualitative Step-by-Step View

The formation of corona is not instantaneous. It occurs through a sequence of events that students must visualize clearly:

1. **Initial State (No Corona)**: Under normal operating voltages, the alternating electric field around the conductor remains below the dielectric strength of air. Air molecules stay neutral, and no discharge occurs.
2. **Rising Voltage Phase**: As the transmission line voltage is increased, the potential gradient at the conductor surface rises proportionally. The air molecules in the immediate vicinity begin experiencing intense electrical stress.
3. **Ionization Initiation**: When the gradient exceeds approximately **30 kV/cm (peak)**, the free electrons in air gain sufficient kinetic energy. They collide with neutral air molecules (mostly N₂ and O₂), knocking out additional electrons — a process called **avalanche ionization** or **Townsend avalanche**.
4. **Corona Discharge Establishment**: A localized plasma region forms around the conductor. This plasma contains free electrons, positive ions, and excited neutral molecules. The discharge is self-sustaining but incomplete (does not bridge the entire gap to the other conductor).
5. **Steady-State Corona Operation**: Once established, corona continues as long as the voltage gradient remains above the critical value. Energy is continuously dissipated as heat, light, sound, and chemical by-products.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Mechanism of Corona Formation

The corona phenomenon can be decomposed into the following sequential physical stages:

### Stage 1: Electric Field Concentration
- The potential gradient is **not uniform** along the conductor surface.
- It is **maximum at the surface** and decreases with distance from the conductor.
- Gradient at the surface is given by: $g = \dfrac{V}{r \cdot \ln(d/r)}$ (qualitative reference only).

### Stage 2: Ionization of Air
- Air contains a small number of **free electrons** due to cosmic radiation and natural radioactivity.
- When the surface gradient exceeds the dielectric strength, these electrons gain energy and collide with neutral gas molecules.
- Each collision releases more electrons → **chain reaction of ionization**.

### Stage 3: Visual and Audible Manifestation
- **Light emission**: Excited nitrogen and oxygen molecules release photons upon de-excitation → violet glow.
- **Hissing noise**: Rapid movement of ions and production of small pressure waves.
- **Ozone production**: $3O_2 \rightarrow 2O_3$ (endothermic reaction powered by corona energy).

### Stage 4: Heat Dissipation
- Continuous energy loss as heat raises the conductor temperature slightly.
- This heat loss represents **corona power loss**, which is wasted energy.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Corona (Detailed Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following factors as core learning outcomes. Each factor must be understood both individually and in combination.

### 2.2.1 Atmospheric Factors

**A. Air Density (δ)**
- Corona formation depends on the **number of air molecules per unit volume**.
- Higher density → more molecules → harder to ionize → **corona decreases**.
- Lower density (high altitude) → fewer molecules → easier to ionize → **corona increases**.
- $\delta = \dfrac{3.92 \cdot b}{273 + t}$ where $b$ is barometric pressure in cm of Hg and $t$ is temperature in °C.

**B. Temperature (t)**
- Higher temperature → air expands → density decreases → **corona increases**.
- Lower temperature → air contracts → density increases → **corona decreases**.

**C. Humidity**
- Moisture in air provides additional free electrons and ions.
- **Surprisingly, humidity decreases corona** because water vapor molecules have a higher breakdown voltage than dry air and tend to capture free electrons, forming heavy negative ions that are less mobile.

**D. Dust, Smoke, and Pollutants**
- Suspended particles act as **nucleation centers** for ionization.
- They lower the breakdown voltage of air → **corona increases significantly** in polluted industrial areas.

### 2.2.2 Conductor-Related Factors

**A. Conductor Radius (r)**
- Larger radius → lower surface gradient → **corona decreases**.
- This is why EHV/UHV lines use **bundled conductors** (hollow or large-diameter ACSR).

**B. Conductor Surface Condition (Irregularity Factor m)**
- Smooth, polished conductors: $m = 1.0$ (minimum corona).
- Weathered/rough conductors: $m = 0.93$ to $0.98$ (more corona).
- Stranded conductors: $m \approx 0.82$ (maximum corona among standard conductors).

**C. Spacing Between Conductors (d)**
- Greater spacing → lower surface gradient for a given voltage → **corona decreases**.

### 2.2.3 Electrical Factors

**A. Line Voltage**
- Higher operating voltage → higher surface gradient → **corona increases**.

**B. Frequency of Supply**
- Higher frequency → ions have less time to recombine between cycles → cumulative ionization → **corona increases**.

**C. Waveform of Supply**
- Non-sinusoidal (distorted) waveforms have higher peak factors → **corona increases**.

## 2.3 Advantages of Corona

> [!TIP]
> Although corona is generally considered undesirable, it does offer a few limited engineering benefits.

1. **Acts as a Safety Valve**: Corona effectively limits overvoltages (e.g., from switching surges or lightning) by dissipating excess energy as heat and light, preventing flashover.
2. **Reduces Transient Surge Magnitude**: Lightning-induced surges are partially absorbed by the corona envelope, reducing their destructive potential.
3. **Visual Indication of High Voltage**: The corona glow serves as a warning of dangerously high voltage in laboratory experiments.

## 2.4 Disadvantages of Corona

> [!WARNING]
> The disadvantages of corona are far more significant than its advantages, making corona reduction a critical design objective.

1. **Power Loss**: Continuous energy dissipation → reduced transmission efficiency → economic loss.
2. **Interference with Communication Lines**: Corona generates **electromagnetic interference (EMI)**, causing noise in nearby telephone lines and radio receivers.
3. **Production of Ozone (O₃)**: Corrosive gas that damages conductor surfaces and nearby metal equipment.
4. **Audible Noise (AN)**: The hissing sound, combined with vibration, produces objectionable noise near residential areas.
5. **Non-Economic Conductor Size**: To avoid corona, conductors must be designed larger than what current-carrying capacity alone would require → increased material cost.

## 2.5 Methods of Reducing Corona

The mitigation strategies directly address the factors that increase corona:

| Method | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Use of Large-Diameter Conductors** | Reduces surface gradient | EHV transmission lines (400 kV, 765 kV) |
| **Use of Hollow Conductors** | Increased effective radius without using more material | Very high voltage lines |
| **Bundled Conductors** | Multiple sub-conductors effectively increase radius | EHV and UHV systems |
| **Use of Polished/Smooth Conductors** | Maximize irregularity factor $m$ | All voltage levels |
| **Increasing Conductor Spacing** | Reduces gradient at given voltage | Tower design optimization |
| **Use of Corona Rings / Grading Rings** | Distribute potential gradient uniformly | Substation equipment, insulator strings |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (For Qualitative Reference Only)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are presented for **conceptual familiarity** only. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates **qualitative study only** — numerical problem-solving is **not** expected in the exam.

| Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|
| $V_d = m \cdot g_0 \cdot \delta \cdot r \cdot \ln(d/r)$ | Disruptive critical voltage (line-to-neutral) | kV/phase |
| $V_v = m \cdot g_0 \cdot \delta \cdot r \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{0.308}{\sqrt{\delta \cdot r}}\right) \cdot \ln(d/r)$ | Visual critical voltage (line-to-neutral) | kV/phase |
| $\delta = \dfrac{3.92 \cdot b}{273 + t}$ | Air density factor | dimensionless |
| $g_0 = 30$ kV/cm (peak) | Breakdown strength of air at NTP | kV/cm |
| $P = \dfrac{244}{\delta} \cdot (f + 25) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{r}{d}} \cdot (V - V_d)^2 \times 10^{-5}$ | Peek's corona power loss formula (kW/km/phase) | kW/km/phase |

Where:
- $m$ = irregularity factor (dimensionless, $0 \le m \le 1$)
- $\delta$ = air density factor (dimensionless)
- $r$ = radius of conductor
- $d$ = spacing between conductors
- $b$ = barometric pressure in cm of Hg
- $t$ = temperature in °C
- $f$ = supply frequency in Hz
- $V$ = line-to-neutral voltage (rms)
- $V_d$ = disruptive critical voltage (rms, line-to-neutral)

> [!NOTE]
> **Qualitative insights from Peek's formula**:
> 1. Corona loss $P$ is **proportional to $(V - V_d)^2$** → loss skyrockets once voltage exceeds the critical value.
> 2. Loss **increases with frequency** → DC transmission has lower corona loss than AC.
> 3. Loss **decreases with conductor size** ($1/\sqrt{r}$) → larger conductors reduce loss.
> 4. Loss **increases with reduced spacing** → wider tower design helps reduce corona.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The corona phenomenon has direct implications in the design of every modern EHV transmission system:

- **Selection of Conductor Size**: For 400 kV lines, ACSR conductors must be at least 30 mm in diameter; for 765 kV lines, bundled conductors are mandatory.
- **Choice of Transmission Voltage**: Engineers must balance the cost of corona mitigation (larger conductors, wider towers) against the benefits of higher power transfer capability.
- **Right-of-Way Planning**: Corona-induced audible noise and EMI require minimum separation from residential zones.
- **HVDC vs. HVAC Trade-off**: HVDC transmission has significantly lower corona loss than HVAC for the same voltage level — one of the technical advantages favoring HVDC for very long distances.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes a **qualitative study** of corona, this section provides the **logical derivation framework** for Peek's Law to develop conceptual clarity — **not** for numerical problem-solving.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation of Peek's Law (Conceptual Framework)

The derivation of Peek's empirical formula proceeds through the following logical stages. Each stage corresponds to a physical phenomenon:

### Stage 1: Expression for Surface Potential Gradient

For a single conductor of radius $r$ at height $H$ above ground with another conductor at spacing $d$ in a two-wire line, the potential gradient at the surface of one conductor is derived from the line charge density $\lambda$ (C/m) on the conductor.

By solving Laplace's equation in cylindrical coordinates, the potential gradient at the surface of the conductor simplifies to:

$$g = \dfrac{V}{r \cdot \ln(d/r)}$$

where $V$ is the line-to-neutral voltage.

**Physical interpretation**: The gradient is **inversely proportional to $r$** → smaller conductors have higher gradients → more corona.

### Stage 2: Introduction of Air Density Factor (δ)

The dielectric strength of air is **not constant** — it varies with atmospheric conditions. Peek introduced the empirical air density correction factor:

$$\delta = \dfrac{3.92 \cdot b}{273 + t}$$

where $b$ is in cm of Hg and $t$ is in °C.

At standard conditions ($b = 76$ cm Hg, $t = 25$ °C):
- $\delta = 1.0$ (normalized reference value)

**Physical interpretation**: The breakdown gradient at any atmospheric condition is $g_0 \cdot \delta$, where $g_0 = 30$ kV/cm (peak) is the value at NTP.

### Stage 3: Introduction of Irregularity Factor (m)

Real conductors are never perfectly smooth. Surface roughness, dirt, scratches, and strand geometry all lower the effective breakdown voltage. Peek accounted for this through the irregularity factor $m$:

| Surface Condition | Value of $m$ |
|---|---|
| Polished, smooth conductor | $1.00$ |
| Weathered (aged) conductor | $0.93 - 0.98$ |
| Stranded (standard ACSR) cable | $0.80 - 0.87$ |
| 7-strand cable | $0.83 - 0.87$ |

### Stage 4: Disruptive Critical Voltage Equation

Corona initiates when the surface gradient equals the effective breakdown strength of air. Setting $g = g_0 \cdot m \cdot \delta$ and solving for $V$:

$$V_d = m \cdot g_0 \cdot \delta \cdot r \cdot \ln(d/r)$$

**Line-by-line logical flow**:
1. Start with the surface gradient equation: $g = \dfrac{V}{r \cdot \ln(d/r)}$
2. Substitute the critical condition: $g = g_0 \cdot m \cdot \delta$
3. Equate: $\dfrac{V_d}{r \cdot \ln(d/r)} = g_0 \cdot m \cdot \delta$
4. Solve for $V_d$: $V_d = m \cdot g_0 \cdot \delta \cdot r \cdot \ln(d/r)$

### Stage 5: Visual Critical Voltage Equation

For the **visual** onset of corona, a higher gradient is required. Peek empirically determined the correction term $\left(1 + \dfrac{0.308}{\sqrt{\delta \cdot r}}\right)$:

$$V_v = m \cdot g_0 \cdot \delta \cdot r \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{0.308}{\sqrt{\delta \cdot r}}\right) \cdot \ln(d/r)$$

**Physical interpretation**: The additional term represents the extra energy needed to produce **visible light** in the discharge. This term is **larger for thinner conductors** (small $r$) and **smaller for denser air** (large $\delta$).

### Stage 6: Corona Power Loss (Peek's Empirical Formula)

When $V > V_d$, corona loss occurs. Peek's empirical loss formula per phase per km:

$$P = \dfrac{244}{\delta} \cdot (f + 25) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{r}{d}} \cdot (V - V_d)^2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kW/km/phase}$$

**Qualitative analysis of each term**:
- $\dfrac{1}{\delta}$: Higher density → fewer ionizations per unit time → loss decreases with $\delta$.
- $(f + 25)$: Higher frequency → more ionization cycles per second → loss increases with $f$.
- $\sqrt{r/d}$: Larger $r$ or smaller $d$ → loss increases, but the $d$ effect is stronger.
- $(V - V_d)^2$: Loss is a **quadratic** function of voltage excess above critical value.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Effect on Corona | Effect on $V_d$ | Effect on $V_v$ | Effect on Power Loss $P$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase in conductor radius $r$ | Decreases | Increases | Increases | Decreases |
| Increase in spacing $d$ | Decreases | Increases | Increases | Decreases |
| Increase in air density $\delta$ | Decreases | Increases | Increases | Decreases |
| Increase in temperature $t$ | Increases | Decreases | Decreases | Increases |
| Decrease in pressure $b$ | Increases | Decreases | Decreases | Increases |
| Increase in frequency $f$ | Increases | No effect | No effect | Increases |
| Increase in irregularity (lower $m$) | Increases | Decreases | Decreases | Increases |
| Increase in humidity | Decreases (slightly) | Slight increase | Slight increase | Slight decrease |
| Increase in pollution (dust, smoke) | Increases | Decreases | Decreases | Increases |
| Increase in operating voltage $V$ | Increases | — | — | Increases quadratically |

## 3.3 Logical Reasoning Behind Each Effect

### Why does larger radius reduce corona?
- The surface area increases as $r$ increases.
- The total charge distributes over a larger surface, **reducing charge density**.
- Lower charge density → lower potential gradient → less corona.

### Why does higher humidity reduce corona?
- Water vapor molecules have **higher electron affinity** than dry air molecules.
- They capture free electrons, forming **heavy negative ions** with low mobility.
- These ions are **less effective at avalanche ionization** → corona is reduced.

### Why does pollution increase corona?
- Dust and smoke particles have **sharp edges** that act as points of field concentration.
- These particles also have **lower breakdown voltage** than air.
- They trigger premature ionization → corona begins at lower voltages.

### Why does frequency increase corona?
- At higher frequency, **half-cycle duration decreases**.
- Ions formed in one half-cycle have less time to **recombine** before the next cycle.
- Cumulative ion density builds up → stronger corona.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    B --> C{Field Strength > 30 kV/cm peak?}

    C -->|Yes| E[Free Electrons Gain Kinetic Energy]
    E --> F[Electron Collisions with Air Molecules]
    F --> G[Avalanche Ionization Begins]
    G --> H[Plasma Region Forms Around Conductor]
    H --> I{Visible Threshold Reached?}
    I -->|No| J[Inaudible Corona: Stage 1]
    I -->|Yes| K[Visible Glow plus Hissing Sound]
    K --> L[Ozone Generation plus Heat plus EMI]

    M --> N[Power Loss plus Communication Interference]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Questions are modeled after the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** with the standard 3-mark (Part A) and 14-mark (Part B) structure. Part B includes **internal choice** as per KTU regulations.

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Define corona. List the various factors affecting corona in transmission lines.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Corona** is the phenomenon of partial ionization of air surrounding a high-voltage transmission line conductor when the potential gradient at the conductor surface exceeds the dielectric strength of air (approximately 30 kV/cm peak). It manifests as a violet glow accompanied by a hissing sound and produces ozone.

**Factors affecting corona** are classified into three categories:

1. **Atmospheric factors**: Air density ($\delta$), temperature, humidity, dust, smoke, and pollutants.
2. **Conductor factors**: Radius of conductor ($r$), surface condition (irregularity factor $m$), spacing between conductors ($d$), and conductor geometry.
3. **Electrical factors**: Operating line voltage, supply frequency, and waveform shape.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`

**Differentiate between disruptive critical voltage and visual critical voltage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Disruptive Critical Voltage ($V_d$) | Visual Critical Voltage ($V_v$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Voltage at which corona just **initiates** (ionization begins) | Voltage at which corona becomes **visually observable** as a faint glow |
| **Numerical value** | Lower | Higher ($V_v > V_d$) |
| **Physical appearance** | Inaudible and invisible | Audible hissing, visible violet glow |
| **Physical interpretation** | Onset of partial ionization | Onset of light-producing discharge |
| **Energy requirement** | Lower energy | Higher energy required for photon emission |

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: Students often lose marks by (1) confusing $V_d$ and $V_v$ formulas, (2) forgetting the units in Peek's formula, (3) not relating factors directly to the **physical mechanism** of corona, and (4) missing the **engineering application** part. Always support theoretical statements with **physical reasoning**.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]`

**Explain the phenomenon of corona in transmission lines. Discuss the various factors affecting corona with their physical reasoning.**

#### Part (a): Phenomenon of Corona (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The phenomenon of corona occurs in high-voltage transmission lines when the electric field intensity at the conductor surface becomes high enough to ionize the surrounding air.

**Step 1: Initial Condition (No Corona)**

When a transmission line is energized at normal operating voltage, the electric field around the conductor is below the breakdown strength of air (30 kV/cm peak). Air molecules remain in their neutral state, and no discharge occurs.

**Step 2: Field Concentration**

The electric field is not uniform — it is **maximum at the conductor surface** and decreases radially outward. The field concentration increases with:
- Smaller conductor radius
- Higher operating voltage
- Reduced air density

**Step 3: Ionization Process**

When the surface gradient exceeds the dielectric strength of air, free electrons (from cosmic rays, etc.) gain sufficient kinetic energy. They collide with neutral air molecules, knocking out additional electrons in a chain reaction called **Townsend avalanche ionization**. This produces:
- Free electrons
- Positive ions
- Excited neutral molecules
- Photons (light)

**Step 4: Establishment of Corona**

A localized plasma region forms around the conductor. The discharge is **incomplete** (it does not bridge the gap to the other conductor) and remains confined to a thin envelope around the conductor surface.

**Step 5: Manifestation**

- **Light**: Violet-blue glow (mostly from excited nitrogen)
- **Sound**: Hissing noise (from ion movement and pressure waves)
- **Chemistry**: Ozone production via $3O_2 \rightarrow 2O_3$
- **Heat**: Continuous dissipation of energy

#### Part (b): Factors Affecting Corona (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Atmospheric Factors:**

- **Air Density ($\delta$)**: At higher altitudes, $\delta$ is low → easier ionization → **more corona**. Reference formula: $\delta = \dfrac{3.92b}{273+t}$.
- **Temperature**: Higher temperature → air expansion → lower density → **more corona**.
- **Humidity**: Water vapor molecules capture free electrons, reducing ionization efficiency → **less corona** (slightly).
- **Dust and Smoke**: Particles act as nucleation points → **more corona**.

**2. Conductor Factors:**

- **Radius ($r$)**: Larger radius → lower charge density → lower surface gradient → **less corona**.
- **Surface Condition ($m$)**: Polished surfaces ($m = 1.0$) produce less corona than rough surfaces ($m \approx 0.82$).
- **Spacing ($d$)**: Greater spacing reduces surface gradient at constant voltage → **less corona**.

**3. Electrical Factors:**

- **Operating Voltage**: Higher voltage → higher surface gradient → **more corona** (quadratic relationship).
- **Frequency**: Higher frequency → less recombination time → cumulative ionization → **more corona**.

**Practical Application**: For 400 kV and above, bundled conductors and minimum 30 mm diameter conductors are mandatory to suppress corona.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]`

**Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of corona. Explain the various methods adopted to reduce corona effects in transmission systems.**

#### Part (a): Advantages and Disadvantages of Corona (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of Corona:**

1. **Acts as a Safety Mechanism**: Corona dissipates excess energy from overvoltages (switching surges, lightning) as heat and light, thereby limiting the magnitude of transient voltages and protecting the line insulation from flashover.

2. **Reduces Surge Magnitude**: Lightning-induced surges on transmission lines are partially absorbed by the corona envelope surrounding the conductor, reducing their peak amplitude.

3. **Visual Indication in Laboratories**: In high-voltage laboratories, the corona glow serves as a clear visual warning of dangerously high electric fields.

**Disadvantages of Corona:**

1. **Corona Power Loss**: The most significant disadvantage. Corona represents a continuous dissipation of useful transmitted energy. For long EHV lines, this can be substantial (hundreds of kW per km).

2. **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)**: The corona discharge produces high-frequency electromagnetic noise that interferes with nearby radio receivers, television signals, and telephone communication lines (especially in audio frequency range).

3. **Audible Noise (AN)**: The hissing sound, combined with conductor vibration, produces noise that can be objectionable in residential areas — a major concern in the design of EHV lines near populated zones.

4. **Ozone Production**: Ozone ($O_3$) is highly corrosive and gradually damages the conductor surface and nearby metallic equipment (especially in substations).

5. **Non-Economic Conductor Design**: To avoid corona, conductors must be larger than what current-carrying capacity alone would require, increasing material and installation costs.

6. **Reduced Transmission Efficiency**: Continuous power loss decreases the overall efficiency of the transmission system.

#### Part (b): Methods of Reducing Corona (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The mitigation methods directly target the factors that promote corona:

**1. Use of Large-Diameter Conductors:**
The surface gradient is inversely proportional to conductor radius. Increasing $r$ reduces the gradient below the critical disruptive value. For 400 kV lines, ACSR conductors of at least 30 mm diameter are used.

**2. Use of Hollow Conductors:**
Hollow conductors provide a large effective radius without the material cost of solid conductors. They are used for very high voltages.

**3. Use of Bundled Conductors:**
Multiple sub-conductors per phase (2, 3, or 4 in a bundle) effectively increase the equivalent radius and lower the surface gradient. Standard practice for 400 kV (twin bundle) and 765 kV (quad bundle) lines.

**4. Use of Smooth and Polished Conductors:**
The irregularity factor $m$ is maximized for smooth surfaces (approaching 1.0), increasing the disruptive critical voltage. Polished conductors are used where feasible.

**5. Increasing Conductor Spacing:**
Larger spacing $d$ reduces the surface gradient at a given operating voltage. Tower design must balance this against the cost of wider right-of-way.

**6. Use of Corona Rings / Grading Rings:**
Metal toroids installed at the ends of insulator strings and at sharp protrusions of substation equipment. They distribute the electric field uniformly, preventing high-gradient points that trigger corona.

**7. Use of HVDC for Long Distances:**
DC transmission does not exhibit the cumulative ionization effect of AC, significantly reducing corona loss. This is one of the technical advantages of HVDC for long-distance bulk power transfer.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary for KTU Exam**

- **Corona** is partial ionization of air near high-voltage conductors, occurring when surface gradient exceeds **30 kV/cm peak**.
- Corona produces **light (violet glow), sound (hissing), heat, and ozone**.
- **$V_d$** (disruptive critical voltage) < **$V_v$** (visual critical voltage).
- **Factors INCREASING corona**: Higher voltage, higher frequency, smaller conductor radius, rough surface (low $m$), low air density (high altitude), high temperature, dust/pollution, narrow spacing.
- **Factors DECREASING corona**: Lower voltage, DC supply, larger radius, smooth surface, high density, low temperature, clean air, wider spacing.
- **Air density factor**: $\delta = \dfrac{3.92b}{273+t}$, with $\delta = 1$ at NTP.
- **Irregularity factor $m$**: 1.0 (polished), 0.93-0.98 (weathered), 0.82-0.87 (stranded).
- **Advantages of corona**: Acts as safety valve, reduces surge magnitude, visual indicator.
- **Disadvantages of corona**: Power loss, EMI, audible noise, ozone corrosion, higher conductor cost.
- **Methods to reduce corona**: Large/hollow/bundled conductors, smooth surfaces, wider spacing, corona rings, HVDC.
- **Peek's Law** (qualitative): $P \propto (V - V_d)^2 \cdot (f + 25) \cdot \sqrt{r/d} \cdot (1/\delta)$.
- **DC vs AC corona**: DC has lower corona loss because no cumulative ionization.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis**: **Qualitative understanding** only — no numerical calculations on corona in exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surge Impedance Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surge Impedance Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** of a transmission line is defined as the power delivered by a lossless transmission line to a purely resistive load whose resistance is equal to the **surge impedance (characteristic impedance)** of the line. At this loading condition, the line consumes no net reactive power; the reactive power generated by the line's shunt capacitance exactly equals the reactive power absorbed by the line's series inductance.

Mathematically, for a three-phase line:

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{(V_{L-L})^2}{Z_s}$$

where $V_{L-L}$ is the line-to-line RMS voltage in kV and $Z_s$ is the surge impedance in ohms. The resulting SIL is expressed in megawatts (MW).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surge Impedance** ($Z_s$ or $Z_c$) is the characteristic impedance of a lossless line:
> 
> $$Z_s = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \;\;\Omega$$
> 
> It depends **only on the line geometry and conductor configuration**, not on its electrical length.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Matched Water Hose" Intuition

Imagine a long garden hose connected to a tap. The hose has a natural resistance to water flow. If you let the water jet out freely into the air, the pressure inside the hose is high, but the water exits chaotically — energy is wasted in turbulence (reflections).

Now, if you attach a **nozzle whose resistance exactly matches the hose's natural impedance**, the water flows out smoothly, the pressure inside the hose equals the pressure at the nozzle, and **maximum useful power is delivered without reflections**.

The transmission line behaves identically:

- **Line's shunt capacitance** $C$ → stores energy like a stretched hose wall.
- **Line's series inductance** $L$ → resists changes in current like hose friction.
- **Matched resistive load** = $Z_s$ = $\sqrt{L/C}$ → eliminates standing waves.
- **Power at this match** = SIL.

At this "natural" loading, the line neither **generates** nor **absorbs** reactive power, and the sending-end and receiving-end voltages remain equal in magnitude. Engineers call this the *natural loading* of the line.

### 1.3 Standard / Typical Values (Memorize for KTU Exams)

| Line Voltage (kV) | Typical $Z_s$ ($\Omega$) | Approximate SIL (MW) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **132 kV** | **~ 400** | **~ 40 – 50** |
| **220 kV** | **~ 380 – 400** | **~ 120 – 130** |
| **400 kV** | **~ 250 – 300** | **~ 530 – 640** |
| **765 kV** | **~ 240 – 260** | **~ 2250 – 2450** |
| **1200 kV (UHVAC)** | **~ 230** | **~ 6000** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Facts to Remember:**
> - Surge impedance for overhead lines typically lies in the range **200 $\Omega$ to 400 $\Omega$**.
> - For underground cables, $C$ is much larger, so $Z_s$ is small (around **20 – 60 $\Omega$**) — this is why cables have very high SIL but poor voltage regulation.
> - For single-phase representation: $\text{SIL}_{1\phi} = \dfrac{V_{ph}^2}{Z_s}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Surge Impedance

A transmission line is a distributed parameter network with four primary constants:

- **R** (series resistance, $\Omega$/km)
- **L** (series inductance, H/km)
- **G** (shunt conductance, S/km)
- **C** (shunt capacitance, F/km)

For an **ideal lossless line** (R = 0, G = 0), the propagation constant becomes purely imaginary, and the characteristic impedance is purely real. This real value is the **Surge Impedance** $Z_s$.

### 2.2 Why is SIL Critical in Power System Design?

The operation of a transmission line relative to its SIL is the single most important factor determining its **voltage profile** and **reactive power behavior**:

| Loading Condition | Reactive Power Behavior | Voltage at Receiving End |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Loading < SIL (Light Load)** | Line **generates** net reactive power (capacitive dominance) | $V_R > V_S$ (Ferranti Effect) |
| **Loading = SIL (Natural Load)** | **No net** reactive power exchange | $V_R = V_S$ |
| **Loading > SIL (Heavy Load)** | Line **absorbs** net reactive power (inductive dominance) | $V_R < V_S$ (voltage drop) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A long EHV/UHV line (say, > 400 km at 400 kV) cannot be operated *below* SIL without risking dangerous over-voltages due to the **Ferranti Effect**. Hence, EHV lines are almost always designed to operate **at or near SIL**.

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" — Logic Breakdown

1. **Why does the line generate Vars when lightly loaded?**
   Because the shunt capacitance $C$ is distributed along the entire length. With little load current, the $I^2X_L$ consumption is small, but the $V^2/X_C$ generation from the line's capacitance dominates — the line becomes a net **Var generator**.

2. **How does SIL achieve perfect balance?**
   At SIL, the load current $I_{SIL} = V/Z_s$ is exactly large enough so that the inductive consumption of the series inductance equals the capacitive generation of the shunt capacitance. The line is *self-regulating*.

3. **Why is $Z_s$ independent of line length?**
   $L$ and $C$ are **distributed parameters per unit length**. The ratio $L/C$ remains constant regardless of the line's total physical length. Hence SIL is a *property of the line design*, not its size.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they are the most tested in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Z_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Surge / Characteristic impedance (lossless) | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $Z_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{z}{y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$ | General characteristic impedance | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $\text{SIL} = \dfrac{V_{L-L}^{\,2}}{Z_s}$ | Three-phase SIL | **MW** |
| 4 | $\text{SIL}_{1\phi} = \dfrac{V_{ph}^{\,2}}{Z_s}$ | Single-phase SIL | **MW** |
| 5 | $\text{SIL}_{p.u.} = 1.0$ | SIL expressed in per-unit of the base MVA | dimensionless |
| 6 | $I_{SIL} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{Z_s}$ | Current corresponding to SIL | kA |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{V^2}{Z_c} \sin\delta$ | Power transmitted over a lossless line (at SIL: $\delta \approx 0$ since flat voltage profile) | MW |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{V^2}{Z_c}\left(\cos\delta - \dfrac{Z_c}{X}\sin\delta\right) \approx 0$ | Reactive power at SIL loading | MVAR |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Series/Shunt Compensation Design:** Engineers compute SIL to decide *how much* series capacitance or shunt reactance to install.
- **Reactive Power Planning:** Power flow studies always benchmark line loading against SIL.
- **EHV Transmission Economics:** Lines operated near SIL minimize transmission losses and maximize corridor efficiency.
- **HVDC Equivalents:** A DC line's natural power is also a function of $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$, analogous to SIL.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Surge Impedance (Characteristic Impedance)

#### Starting Point — The Telegrapher's Equations

For a distributed transmission line, the voltage and current variations along a differential element $dx$ are:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = z \cdot I = (R + j\omega L) \cdot I$$

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = y \cdot V = (G + j\omega C) \cdot V$$

where $z$ and $y$ are the series impedance and shunt admittance per unit length, respectively.

#### Step 1 — Decouple the Equations

Differentiate the first equation with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = z \cdot \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute the Second Equation

Substitute $\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x} = y V$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = z \cdot y \cdot V$$

This is the **wave equation** for voltage along the line.

#### Step 3 — Define the Propagation Constant

Let $\gamma = \sqrt{zy} = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}$, known as the **propagation constant**. Similarly, the wave equation for current:

$$\frac{d^2 I}{dx^2} = \gamma^2 I$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for Characteristic Impedance

The general solution gives forward and backward travelling waves. The ratio of voltage to current for a single forward (incident) wave gives the **characteristic impedance**:

$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}} = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

#### Step 5 — Apply the Lossless Approximation

For an overhead line at power frequency, $R \ll \omega L$ and $G \ll \omega C$, so $R$ and $G$ can be neglected. This yields the **surge impedance**:

$$Z_s = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \;\;\Omega$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The mathematical transition from general $Z_c$ to $Z_s$ is achieved by setting $R = 0$ and $G = 0$ in the expression under the square root. Because $\omega$ cancels out (numerator and denominator both contain $\omega$ inside the $j$ term), $Z_s$ becomes independent of frequency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)

#### Step 1 — Establish the Receiving End Condition

Let the line be terminated by a load resistance equal to $Z_s$. Then the receiving-end voltage and current are related by:

$$V_R = Z_s \cdot I_R$$

#### Step 2 — Express Apparent Power at the Load

The complex power delivered to the load is:

$$S_R = V_R \cdot I_R^{*} = V_R \cdot \left(\frac{V_R}{Z_s}\right)^{*} = \frac{|V_R|^2}{Z_s}$$

Since $Z_s$ is purely real (lossless assumption), the apparent power is purely real — there is no reactive component.

#### Step 3 — Use Line-to-Line Voltage for Three-Phase Lines

For a three-phase balanced system, the line-to-neutral voltage relates to the line-to-line voltage by $V_{ph} = V_{L-L}/\sqrt{3}$. The total three-phase power is:

$$\text{SIL}_{3\phi} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^{\,2}}{Z_s} = 3 \cdot \frac{(V_{L-L}/\sqrt{3})^2}{Z_s} = \frac{V_{L-L}^{\,2}}{Z_s}$$

#### Step 4 — Units Conversion

If $V_{L-L}$ is in kV and $Z_s$ is in ohms, the result is in kW. To convert to MW:

$$\boxed{\text{SIL (MW)} = \frac{[V_{L-L} \text{ in kV}]^2}{Z_s \, (\Omega)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytic Verification — Reactive Power Neutrality

Using the standard ABCD model of a **lossless** line ($\gamma = j\beta$, $A = D = \cos\beta\ell$, $B = jZ_c \sin\beta\ell$):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_S &= V_R \cos\beta\ell + j I_R Z_c \sin\beta\ell \\
I_S &= I_R \cos\beta\ell + j \frac{V_R}{Z_c} \sin\beta\ell
\end{aligned}$$

For a load $V_R = Z_s \cdot I_R$ and using $Z_c = Z_s$ (lossless), substitute $I_R = V_R / Z_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_S &= V_R \cos\beta\ell + j \frac{V_R}{Z_s} \cdot Z_s \sin\beta\ell \\
V_S &= V_R \left(\cos\beta\ell + j \sin\beta\ell\right) \\
V_S &= V_R \, e^{j\beta\ell}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence $|V_S| = |V_R|$ — **voltage magnitudes are equal at both ends**, confirming flat voltage profile. The reactive power flow $Q_S - Q_R = 0$, confirming the line is *reactive-neutral*.

> **Physical Insight:** A line loaded exactly at SIL behaves like an infinite busbar — neither absorbing nor supplying vars, and maintaining a flat voltage profile end-to-end.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A three-phase, 400 kV, 50 Hz overhead transmission line has a series inductance of 1.0 mH/km per phase and shunt capacitance of 0.01 $\mu$F/km per phase. Calculate the surge impedance and SIL. Given line length = 300 km, find the total reactive power generated by the line under no-load.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Surge Impedance**

$$Z_s = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{10^5} = 316.23 \;\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute SIL**

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{(400)^2}{316.23} = \frac{160000}{316.23} \approx 506 \; \text{MW}$$

**Step 3 — Total Capacitance of the Line**

$$C_{total} = 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 300 = 3 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{F/phase}$$

**Step 4 — Total Capacitive Var Generation (No-Load)**

The total charging MVar generated by a long line under no-load is given by:

$$Q_C = V_{ph}^2 \cdot \omega C_{total} \cdot \ell$$

(For a uniformly distributed line, the effective value is half — but for the total generated, use full $C_{total}$.)

$$Q_C = \left(\frac{400{,}000}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 \cdot 2\pi(50)(3 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_C &= \left(\frac{400{,}000}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 \cdot 2\pi(50) \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6} \\
    &= 5.33 \times 10^{10} \cdot 9.4248 \times 10^{-5} \\
    &= 5.024 \times 10^{6} \; \text{var/phase}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Three-Phase Total**

$$Q_{C,3\phi} = 3 \times 5.024 \times 10^6 \approx 15.07 \; \text{MVAR}$$

This is a substantial reactive power generation — explaining why lightly loaded long EHV lines exhibit **Ferranti over-voltage**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Surge Impedance Loading Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Line Physical Parameters"]:::input --> B["Series Inductance L per km"]
    A --> C["Shunt Capacitance C per km"]
    B --> D["Compute Z_s = sqrt L over C"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Compute SIL = V_LL squared over Z_s"]
    E --> F{"Compare Actual Load P to SIL"}:::decision
    F -- P less than SIL --> G["Line Generates Vars"]:::branch
    F -- P equals SIL --> H["Flat Voltage Profile V_S = V_R"]:::branch
    F -- P greater than SIL --> I["Line Absorbs Vars"]:::branch
    G --> J["Ferranti Effect Risk"]
    H --> K["Ideal Operation"]
    I --> L["Voltage Drop at Receiver"]

```
