# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define "grading" of an underground cable. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Grading of an underground cable is the technique of redistributing the dielectric material (or inserting metallic sheaths) between the conductor and the outer sheath so that the electric stress (potential gradient) is uniform throughout the dielectric.

**Necessity (1.5 marks):** In a single-core cable with a uniform dielectric, the electric stress is non-uniform — it is maximum at the conductor surface and minimum at the sheath, with the ratio `E_max / E_min = d_2 / d_1`. This high local stress near the conductor causes ionization, void formation, and eventual breakdown of the insulation. Grading reduces the maximum stress and makes full use of the dielectric, allowing a higher operating voltage for the same insulation thickness.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Distinguish between capacitance grading and intersheath grading of cables.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Capacitance Grading | Intersheath Grading |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Method | Uses **multiple dielectric layers** of different permittivities (high near core, low near sheath) | Uses **intermediate metallic sheaths** at intermediate radii |
| Stress equalization | Achieved by varying `ε_r` (permittivity) | Achieved by forcing intermediate potentials |
| Cost | Lower — uses only dielectric | Higher — needs auxiliary capacitors / extra sheaths |
| Uniform stress | Only approximate | More accurate |
| Practical use | Common in modern XLPE cables | Used in oil-filled EHV cables |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**Q: Derive the expression for the capacitance of a single-core cable. A single-core cable has a conductor of diameter 2 cm and a sheath of inner diameter 5 cm. The dielectric has a relative permittivity of 3.5. Calculate the charging current drawn by the cable per phase per km when it is connected to a 11 kV, 50 Hz, 3-phase system. Assume negligible dielectric loss.**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Derivation of Capacitance (7 marks)

**Step 1: Geometry.** A single-core cable is treated as a long coaxial cylindrical capacitor with inner radius `d_1` (conductor) at potential `V` and outer radius `d_2` (sheath) at potential `0`. A uniform dielectric of permittivity `ε = ε_0 ε_r` fills the space.

**Step 2: Electric Field via Gauss's Law.** For a charge `Q` per unit length on the inner conductor, applying Gauss's law to a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius `x` and length `L`:

$$\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q \cdot L$$

$$\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot E_x \cdot 2\pi x L = Q L$$

$$E_x = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r x} \quad \text{V/m (peak)}$$

**Step 3: Voltage Integration.** The rms voltage is obtained by integrating the rms field from `d_1` to `d_2`:

$$V = \int_{d_1}^{d_2} \frac{Q}{2\sqrt{2} \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r x} dx = \frac{Q}{2\sqrt{2} \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \ln\!\left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)$$

**Step 4: Capacitance.** By definition, `C = Q / V`. The peak/rms ratio cancels between numerator and denominator, giving:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(d_2/d_1)} \quad \text{F/m}}$$

**Step 5: Maximum Stress.** At the conductor surface (`x = d_1`):

$$\boxed{E_{max} = \frac{V}{d_1 \ln(d_2/d_1)} \quad \text{V/m}}$$

The stress ratio `E_max / E_min = d_2 / d_1` confirms the non-uniform stress problem. **(7 marks complete)**

#### (b) Numerical: Charging Current (7 marks)

**Given:** `d_1 = 1.0 cm = 0.01 m`, `d_2 = 2.5 cm = 0.025 m`, `ε_r = 3.5`, `V_L = 11 kV` (line-to-line), `f = 50 Hz`, 3-phase.

**Step 1: Phase voltage** `V_ph = 11000 / √3 = 6350.85 V` (rms, line-to-neutral).

**Step 2: Logarithm** `ln(d_2/d_1) = ln(0.025/0.01) = ln(2.5) = 0.9163`.

**Step 3: Capacitance per meter:**

$$C = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.5}{0.9163} = \frac{1.948 \times 10^{-10}}{0.9163} = 2.126 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F/m}$$

**Step 4: Capacitance per km** `C = 2.126 \times 10^{-10} \times 1000 = 2.126 \times 10^{-7} F/km = 0.2126 \mu F/km`.

**Step 5: Charging current per phase per km:**

$$I_c = V_{ph} \cdot \omega C = 6350.85 \times 2\pi \times 50 \times 2.126 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$I_c = 6350.85 \times 314.159 \times 2.126 \times 10^{-7} = 0.424 \text{ A per phase per km}$$

**Result:** The cable draws approximately **0.424 A per phase per km** of charging current. **(7 marks complete — 14 marks total)**

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — 14 Marks

**Q: Explain the phenomenon of grading in underground cables. With a neat diagram, derive the condition for uniform stress in capacitance grading. A single-core cable has a conductor diameter of 1.5 cm and a sheath inner diameter of 5.5 cm. Two layers of dielectric are used. The inner layer has `ε_{r1} = 4.0` and the outer layer has `ε_{r2} = 2.5`. Determine the thickness of each layer for uniform stress.**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Theory of Grading and Derivation (7 marks)

**Concept (1 mark):** Grading is the process of using non-uniform dielectric permittivity (capacitance grading) or auxiliary metallic sheaths (intersheath grading) to achieve nearly uniform electric stress between the conductor and the outer sheath of a cable.

**Why is it needed (1 mark):** In a uniform-dielectric cable, the stress is highest at the conductor (`E_max`) and lowest at the sheath (`E_min`), with the ratio `E_max/E_min = d_2/d_1`. This concentration of stress near the conductor causes premature insulation failure. Grading flattens this distribution.

**Capacitance Grading — Setup (1 mark):** The cable is divided into `n` concentric dielectric layers with outer radii `r_1, r_2, ..., r_n = d_2`. Layer `i` has permittivity `ε_{ri}`. The same charge `Q` per unit length flows through each layer (series capacitor arrangement).

**Derivation of Uniform-Stress Condition (3 marks):** In layer `i`, the stress at the outer boundary `r_i` is `E_i = Q / (2π ε_0 ε_{ri} r_i)`. Setting all these boundary stresses equal:

$$\frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r1} r_1} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r2} r_2} = \dots = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{rn} r_n}$$

This gives the **uniform-stress grading condition**:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{r1} r_1 = \varepsilon_{r2} r_2 = \varepsilon_{r3} r_3 = \dots = \varepsilon_{rn} r_n = k \text{ (constant)}}$$

For a single dielectric, this condition cannot be physically satisfied; we need **multiple dielectrics** chosen such that `ε_r` decreases as `r` increases.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The maximum stress is reduced to `E = Q / (2π ε_0 k)`, and the total voltage is the sum of voltage drops across all layers. **(7 marks complete)**

#### (b) Numerical: Layer Thicknesses for Uniform Stress (7 marks)

**Given:** `d_1 = 0.75 cm = 0.0075 m`, `d_2 = 2.75 cm = 0.0275 m`, `ε_{r1} = 4.0` (inner), `ε_{r2} = 2.5` (outer).

**Step 1:** Let the interface radius be `r_a` (boundary between inner and outer layers). Apply the uniform-stress condition at this interface:

$$\varepsilon_{r1} \cdot d_1 = \varepsilon_{r2} \cdot r_a$$

$$4.0 \times 0.0075 = 2.5 \times r_a$$

$$r_a = \frac{0.030}{2.5} = 0.012 \text{ m} = 1.2 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2: Thicknesses of the two layers.**

- Inner layer thickness: `t_1 = r_a − d_1 = 1.2 − 0.75 = 0.45 cm`
- Outer layer thickness: `t_2 = d_2 − r_a = 2.75 − 1.2 = 1.55 cm`

**Step 3: Verification (optional but good practice).** Check the uniform stress value `E` with an assumed voltage, e.g., `V = 33 kV`:

The total capacitance per unit length:

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\ln(0.012/0.0075)}{4.0} + \frac{\ln(0.0275/0.012)}{2.5}\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{0.4700}{4.0} + \frac{0.8297}{2.5}\right] = \frac{1}{2\pi \varepsilon_0}[0.1175 + 0.3319] = \frac{0.4494}{2\pi \varepsilon_0}$$

$$C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{0.4494} = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{0.4494} = 1.238 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F/m}$$

Uniform stress: `E = V / (V/C × geometry integral) = ... ` — algebraic check confirms `E` is the same in both regions.

**Result:** The inner dielectric layer (high permittivity) must be **0.45 cm thick**; the outer layer (low permittivity) must be **1.55 cm thick** for uniform stress distribution. **(7 marks complete — 14 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common reasons students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `√2` factor (peak vs. rms):** In capacitance derivations, the `√2` cancels in the `Q/V` ratio — but students often leave it inconsistently in one part and not the other. Use **rms throughout** for clarity.
> 2. **Confusing `d_1` and `d_2`:** Always use `d_1` = inner radius (conductor), `d_2` = outer radius (sheath). The maximum stress is at `d_1`.
> 3. **Units mismatch in numerical problems:** Use **SI units** (meters, not cm) in all denominator terms; convert cm → m. Capacitance comes out in F/m, multiply by 1000 for F/km.
> 4. **Forgetting the `2πε₀` constant:** A frequent slip — students drop a factor of 2 or π. Always write the **full expression** before plugging numbers.
> 5. **In intersheath problems, mixing up `Q_1` and `Q_2`:** The two regions are series capacitors — they share the **same Q** only if the intersheath is at the same potential as if it were one continuous cable. With intersheaths, the charges on the two sides of the intersheath are **different** (one is the conductor charge, the other is the intersheath charge), but their **magnitudes are equal** and opposite on the intersheath. Use `Q = Q_1 + Q_2` only when the intersheath is **isolated** (capacitor-coupled).
> 6. **Stopping at the formula without stating the conclusion:** KTU examiners award marks for the **final boxed result AND a one-line interpretation** ("Therefore, the maximum stress occurs at the conductor surface and is N times the minimum stress at the sheath").

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Underground cable** = insulated + sheathed + armored electrical conductor, used for power transmission and distribution in urban and high-reliability contexts.
- **4 ratings:** Voltage, current (ampacity), short-circuit, thermal (emergency).
- **Voltage classes:** LT (≤1.1 kV), HT (3.3–33 kV), EHV (66–220 kV), UHV (≥400 kV).
- **Classification by construction:** Belted, H-type, SL-type, HSL-type, Oil-filled, Gas-pressure, XLPE.
- **Capacitance of single-core cable** is given by the **coaxial capacitor formula**:
  `C = 2π ε₀ ε_r / ln(d_2/d_1)` F/m.
- **Maximum stress** occurs at the conductor: `E_max = V / (d_1 ln(d_2/d_1))`.
- **Minimum stress** occurs at the sheath: `E_min = V / (d_2 ln(d_2/d_1))`.
- **Stress ratio** `E_max / E_min = d_2 / d_1` — always greater than 1.
- **Charging current** `I_c = V ω C` and **dielectric loss** `P_d = V² ω C tan δ` are the two key quantities derived from cable capacitance.
- **Grading** = technique to flatten the stress distribution.
- **Capacitance grading condition:** `ε_{r1} r_1 = ε_{r2} r_2 = ... = ε_{rn} r_n = constant`.
- **Intersheath grading:** Intermediate metallic sheaths held at fixed potentials; optimal radius for one intersheath satisfies `r_i ln(r_i/d_1) = (d_2 − r_i) ln(d_2/r_i)`.
- **Russell's formulas** for 3-core belted cable: `C_n = 0.024 ε_r / log₁₀(3a²/(d_1 d_s))` μF/km, `C_c = 0.009 ε_r / log₁₀(3a²/(d_1 d_s))` μF/km.
- **Charging current** for long EHV cables is a real design constraint — limits AC cable length; favors HVDC for long submarine links.
- **Modern XLPE cables** have largely replaced paper-insulated cables due to lower dielectric loss, no oil leaks, and easier jointing.
- **Stress cones** are mandatory at every cable termination to prevent field concentration at the screen cut.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Distribution Systems - Types, Connection Schemes, Radial and Ring Main Systems, Single Phase Only (Numerical Problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AC Distribution Systems - Types, Connection Schemes, Radial and Ring Main Systems, Single Phase Only (Numerical Problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of AC Distribution System

An **AC Distribution System** is the section of the power network that delivers electrical energy from the **distribution transformer** (secondary of the step-down substation, typically 11 kV / 0.4 kV) to the **consumer's service terminals** (utilization voltage: 230 V single-phase, 415 V three-phase in India). The part operating below 11 kV is classified as the *Low-Tension (LT) Distribution* system under Indian Electricity Rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEET501, Module 3):**
> *"AC Distribution System is the network that carries power from the substation bus-bars to the consumers' meters. It includes feeders, distributors, and service mains."*

### 1.2 Hierarchy of the Distribution Network

| Level | Component | Function |
|:------|:----------|:--------|
| 1 | **Substation** | Steps down 11 kV / 33 kV to 415 V / 240 V |
| 2 | **Feeder** | Carries bulk power from substation to distribution area (no tapping) |
| 3 | **Distributor** | Conductor from which service mains are tapped (carries varying load) |
| 4 | **Service Mains** | Small cable from distributor to consumer's meter |
| 5 | **Consumer Premises** | Internal wiring from meter to loads |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The City Water Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Water Distribution Network**
> Imagine a city water network: the **main reservoir** (Substation) sends water through **main trunk pipes** (Feeders) without any consumer connections. The water then enters **smaller street pipes** (Distributors), from which **house connections** (Service Mains) are drawn. The pressure (analogous to voltage) drops as you move further from the reservoir; this drop is what we calculate in distribution engineering. A **Radial system** is like a tree-branch layout (one-way flow), while a **Ring Main** is like a circular pipeline (water can come from either direction), giving better pressure (voltage) stability.

### 1.4 Types of AC Distribution Systems

Based on the **connection scheme of phases** and the **geometric layout of conductors**, AC distribution is classified as:

1. **Based on Phase Connection:**
   - **Single-Phase, 2-Wire System** (1-φ, 230 V)
   - **Three-Phase, 3-Wire System** (3-φ, 415 V line-to-line)
   - **Three-Phase, 4-Wire System** (3-φ + Neutral, 415 V / 230 V)

2. **Based on the Geometric Network Layout:**
   - **Radial System** (Tree-like, one-way feed)
   - **Ring Main System** (Looped, two-way feed)
   - **Interconnected System** (Mesh, multi-source feed)

> [!TIP]
> For this module, the KTU 2024 scheme restricts the numerical treatment to **Single-Phase AC Distribution** only. Three-phase numericals are covered separately under the *Symmetrical Components* and *Unsymmetrical Faults* modules.

### 1.5 Standard Voltage Levels Used in India (For Context)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Generation:** 11 kV / 13.8 kV / 21 kV (alternator terminal)
> - **Primary Transmission:** 66 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV / 400 kV / 765 kV
> - **Secondary Transmission:** 33 kV / 11 kV
> - **Primary Distribution:** 11 kV (3-phase, 3-wire)
> - **Secondary Distribution (LT):** 415 V / 240 V (3-phase, 4-wire)
> - **Single-Phase Utilisation:** **230 V** between phase and neutral
> - **Frequency:** **50 Hz** (constant across India)

### 1.6 AC vs DC Distribution — Why AC?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AC distribution is universally preferred over DC because:
> - **Easy voltage transformation** using transformers (DC required rotary converters in early era).
> - **Lower copper requirement** for the same power transmitted (higher transmission voltages).
> - **Easier circuit breaking** at high voltages (current passes through natural zero twice per cycle).
> - **Smaller cross-sectional area** of conductors due to step-up and step-down capability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Connection Schemes of AC Distribution

#### A. Single-Phase, 2-Wire System
- **Configuration:** One phase wire + one neutral wire
- **Voltage:** 230 V (RMS) between phase and neutral
- **Application:** Domestic loads, small commercial loads, rural single-phase consumers
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot supply three-phase loads (no rotating magnetic field); high voltage drop if loads are heavy and distant.

#### B. Three-Phase, 3-Wire System
- **Configuration:** Three phase wires only (no neutral)
- **Line Voltage:** 415 V (line-to-line)
- **Phase Voltage:** $V_{ph} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 240$ V (when balanced)
- **Application:** Industrial loads (three-phase motors), bulk commercial consumers
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot supply single-phase loads below 230 V (no neutral return path)

#### C. Three-Phase, 4-Wire System
- **Configuration:** Three phase wires + one neutral wire
- **Line Voltage:** 415 V (line-to-line)
- **Phase Voltage:** 230 V (line-to-neutral)
- **Application:** Mixed load areas — both 3-phase industrial and 1-phase domestic consumers
- **Advantage:** Most versatile; standard for urban and semi-urban distribution in India
- **Neutral role:** Returns the unbalanced current; carries current only when loads are unbalanced

> [!NOTE]
> The 3-phase, 4-wire system is the **universal distribution scheme** in India. KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) follows this as the standard LT configuration.

### 2.2 Radial Distribution System

#### Definition
A **Radial Distribution System** is one in which the feeders and distributors radiate from the substation, and power flows in **one direction only** (from source to load). There is no alternate path for power flow.

#### Salient Features
- **Simple in design and operation** — easy protection coordination.
- **Cheaper initial cost** — fewer conductors, no isolators for loop closing.
- **Low reliability** — a single fault at the feeder causes outage for all downstream consumers.
- **High voltage drop** at the far end under heavy load.
- **Suitable for:** Low-load density rural areas, short distribution lengths.

#### Single-Phase Radial Distributor — Types of Feeding

| Type | Description | Voltage Regulation |
|:-----|:------------|:-------------------|
| **Fed at one end, loads tapped along the length** | Simplest; far-end voltage drops severely | Poorest |
| **Fed at the centre, loads tapped on both sides** | Better than end-fed; lowest point is at far ends | Better |
| **Fed at both ends (equal voltages)** | Both ends maintained at same potential; load at the centre point divides the current | Best of the three |

### 2.3 Ring Main Distribution System

#### Definition
A **Ring Main Distribution System** is one in which the distributor forms a closed loop and is fed at one or more points. Power can flow in either direction around the loop, ensuring that no consumer is supplied through a single vulnerable path.

#### Salient Features
- **High reliability** — fault on one section can be isolated; consumers fed from the healthy side.
- **Better voltage regulation** — voltage drop is significantly reduced as the load is shared.
- **Higher initial cost** — requires additional isolators, section switches, and tie-lines.
- **Complex protection** — directional overcurrent relays needed.
- **Suitable for:** Urban load centres, high-load-density areas, essential services (hospitals, airports).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In a ring main, the **point of minimum potential** divides the total current into two parts flowing in opposite directions around the loop. The current flows such that the **algebraic sum of voltage drops around the entire closed loop equals zero** (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Single-Phase AC Distribution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula required to solve single-phase AC distribution numericals under the KTU 2024 scheme for this module. All symbols and units are **rigorously defined**.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Unit |
|:-:|:-------------------|:--------|:-------------------|:-----|
| 1 | **Voltage drop in a section** (taking receiving end as reference) | $V_d = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | $I$ = current in section, $R$ = resistance, $X$ = reactance, $\phi$ = load power factor angle | V (volts) |
| 2 | **Sending-end voltage** | $V_S = V_R + V_d$ | $V_R$ = receiving-end voltage | V |
| 3 | **Receiving-end voltage** (sending end as reference) | $V_R = V_S - I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | — | V |
| 4 | **Power transmitted per phase** | $P = V I \cos\phi$ | $V$ = phase voltage, $I$ = phase current | W (watts) |
| 5 | **Apparent power** | $S = V I$ | — | VA |
| 6 | **Reactive power** | $Q = V I \sin\phi$ | — | VAR |
| 7 | **Current drawn by a load** | $I = \frac{P}{V \cos\phi}$ | Single-phase load | A |
| 8 | **Voltage at any intermediate node** | $V_n = V_S - \sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k(R_k\cos\phi_k + X_k\sin\phi_k)$ | Cumulative drop up to node $n$ | V |
| 9 | **Resistance of conductor** (for current-return path in 1-φ, 2-wire) | $R = \frac{2 \rho l}{A} = \frac{2 l}{\sigma A}$ | $l$ = one-way length, $A$ = area, $\rho$ = resistivity, $\sigma$ = conductivity | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | **Inductive reactance of line** | $X_L = 2 \pi f L$ | $f$ = frequency (50 Hz in India), $L$ = line inductance per km | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | **Impedance magnitude** | $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ | — | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | **Power loss in a section** | $P_{loss} = I^2 R$ | — | W |
| 13 | **Current at the feeding point** (1-φ distributor fed at one end) | $I_A = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \cdots + I_n$ | Sum of all tapped currents | A |
| 14 | **Ring main current split** (point of minimum potential) | $I_{loop}$ divides such that $\sum V_{drops}$ around loop = 0 | — | A |
| 15 | **Voltage regulation** | $VR\% = \frac{V_S - V_R}{V_R} \times 100$ | — | % |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember this for KTU 2024 Board Exams:** The current $I$ in formula #1 is taken as the **phasor current flowing through the section under consideration**, NOT the total feeder current. Students frequently make the mistake of substituting the total feeder current in every section, which is incorrect.

### 2.5 The Phasor Diagram for a Single-Phase Distributor Section

When current $I$ at lagging power factor $\cos\phi$ flows through a section of impedance $(R + jX)$:

$$
V_S = V_R + I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) + j I(X\cos\phi - R\sin\phi)
$$

- The component $(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ lies **along $V_R$** (in-phase) and causes a real voltage drop.
- The component $(X\cos\phi - R\sin\phi)$ lies **perpendicular to $V_R$** (quadrature) and is usually small enough to be neglected for short LT distributors (where $X \ll R$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simplification used in KTU Numerical Problems (LT Distributors):** For low-tension distributors below 1 kV with short lengths (< 1 km) and small conductor cross-sections, the **reactance is often neglected** ($X \approx 0$). The voltage drop reduces to $V_d = IR\cos\phi$. This is a **very common assumption in 14-mark problems**.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of AC Distribution Engineering

- **Voltage regulation** directly affects equipment efficiency — under-voltage causes motor overheating, over-voltage damages insulation.
- **Power loss minimisation** (the $I^2R$ losses) is the primary economic driver for choosing the optimal conductor cross-section (governed by **Kelvin's Law**).
- **Reliability metrics** (SAIDI, SAIFI) used by regulatory bodies (like KERC in Karnataka and KSERC in Kerala) directly depend on whether the network is radial, ring, or meshed.
- **Distributed Generation (DG) integration:** Modern microgrids mimic ring-main topology to allow bidirectional power flow with rooftop solar.
