**Assumption for the stringing equation (parabolic case):**

The length of the conductor in a parabolic sag can be computed by integrating the arc length:

$$L = l + \frac{8 S^2}{3 l} = l + \frac{w^2 l^3}{24 T_0^2}$$

(The second term is the additional arc length due to curvature.)

**Initial condition (state 1):** Temperature $T_1$, horizontal tension $T_0^{(1)}$, sag $S_1$.

**Final condition (state 2):** Temperature $T_2$, horizontal tension $T_0^{(2)}$, sag $S_2$.

Temperature change: $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$ (positive for heating, negative for cooling).

**Equating the conductor lengths in the two states:**

The total change in conductor length = (thermal expansion) + (change in elastic elongation):

$$L_2 - L_1 = \alpha \cdot l \cdot \Delta T + \frac{T_0^{(2)} - T_0^{(1)}}{A \cdot E} \cdot l$$

Substituting the parabolic arc length expressions:

$$\frac{w^2 l^3}{24} \left[\frac{1}{(T_0^{(2)})^2} - \frac{1}{(T_0^{(1)})^2}\right] = \alpha l \Delta T + \frac{(T_0^{(2)} - T_0^{(1)}) \cdot l}{A \cdot E}$$

Multiplying both sides by $A \cdot E$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{A E w^2 l^2}{24} \left[\frac{1}{(T_0^{(2)})^2} - \frac{1}{(T_0^{(1)})^2}\right] = \alpha A E \Delta T + (T_0^{(2)} - T_0^{(1)})$$

After algebraic manipulation, the standard **stringing equation** is obtained:

$$\boxed{(T_0^{(2)})^2 \left[ T_0^{(2)} - T_0^{(1)} \right] = \frac{w^2 l^2 A E}{24} - \alpha A E \, \Delta T \cdot T_0^{(2)}}$$

Or equivalently, written in the alternative standard form:

$$\boxed{(T_0^{(2)})^2 = (T_0^{(1)})^2 + \alpha A E \Delta T \left( T_0^{(2)} + T_0^{(1)} \right) - \frac{w^2 l^2 A E}{24}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is a **cubic equation** in $T_0^{(2)}$ (the new tension). It cannot be solved by simple algebra — it must be solved by **trial and error** (iterative substitution) or graphically using a **stringing chart**. KTU problems will usually give you numerical values and ask you to compute the new tension iteratively.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Case-Wise Sag Formulas

| Case | Conditions | Sag Formula | Tension at Support |
|------|-----------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Equal level supports, no ice/wind | $S = \dfrac{wl^2}{8T_0}$ | $T = T_0 + wS$ |
| 2 | Unequal level supports, no ice/wind | $S_1 = \dfrac{wl_1^2}{2T_0}$, $S_2 = \dfrac{wl_2^2}{2T_0}$ | $T = T_0 + wS_1$ (at lower) |
| 3 | Equal level supports, with ice + wind | $S = \dfrac{w' l^2}{8T_0}$ where $w' = \sqrt{(w+w_i)^2 + w_w^2}$ | $T = T_0 + w'S$ |
| 4 | Effect of temperature change | Use stringing equation (cubic) | Recompute iteratively |
| 5 | Catenary method (long span) | $S = \dfrac{T_0}{w}\left[\cosh\!\left(\dfrac{wl}{2T_0}\right) - 1\right]$ | $T = T_0 + wS$ |
| 6 | Conductor suspended from a single support (free end) | $S = \dfrac{wl^2}{2T_0}$ (at the free end) | $T = T_0$ (no support tension) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Conductor Geometry with Force Analysis (Equal Level Supports)

This diagram illustrates the conductor geometry, the coordinate system, and the force balance at an arbitrary point P on the conductor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SUPPORT A<br/>Tension T at support<br/>Vertical component wl/2"] -->|"Span length l"| nodeB["LOWEST POINT O<br/>Horizontal Tension T0<br/>Tangent is horizontal<br/>y=0 at x=0"]
    nodeB -->|"Span length l"| nodeC["SUPPORT B<br/>Tension T at support<br/>Vertical component wl/2"]
    nodeD["WEIGHT w per unit length<br/>acting uniformly downward"] -.->|"Loads the conductor"| nodeB
    nodeE["ARBITRARY POINT P<br/>at distance x from O<br/>y = wx^2 divided by 2T0"] -->|"Slope dy/dx = wx/T0"| nodeB
    nodeF["SAG S at midspan<br/>S equals w times l squared<br/>divided by 8 T0"] -.->|"Maximum vertical drop"| nodeB
    nodeG["HORIZONTAL TENSION T0<br/>constant throughout span"] -->|"Equal at O and at P"| nodeE
    nodeH["RESULTANT TENSION T at P<br/>T = sqrt T0 squared plus wx squared"] -->|"Slope direction tangent to curve"| nodeE

    classDef supportNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef forceNode fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef geomNode fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeA,nodeC supportNode
    class nodeB,nodeG forceNode
    class nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeH geomNode
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Unequal Level Supports Geometry

This diagram shows the geometric relationship when the two supports are at different heights.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["SUPPORT A LOWER<br/>Height hA"] -->|"Horizontal distance l1"| nodeO["LOWEST POINT O<br/>Sag point of conductor"]
    nodeO -->|"Horizontal distance l2"| nodeB["SUPPORT B UPPER<br/>Height hB = hA + h"]
    nodeS1["S1 equals w l1 squared<br/>divided by 2T0<br/>sag from lower support"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeS2["S2 equals w l2 squared<br/>divided by 2T0<br/>sag from upper support"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeHdiff["Height difference h<br/>h equals S2 minus S1<br/>equals w over 2T0 times l2 squared minus l1 squared"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeConstraint["Span constraint<br/>l1 plus l2 equals l total"] -.-> nodeO

    classDef upperNode fill:#B5D5FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lowerNode fill:#FFB5B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef geoNode fill:#FFFFD0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeB upperNode
    class nodeA lowerNode
    class nodeO,nodeS1,nodeS2,nodeHdiff,nodeConstraint geoNode
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture for Sag-Tension Calculation

This block diagram represents the full **calculation flow** for an industrial-grade sag-tension analysis, integrating all the cases covered in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["START<br/>Input span geometry<br/>span length, support heights"] --> condLevel{"Supports at<br/>equal level?"}

    condLevel -->|YES| iceWind1{"Ice and wind<br/>present?"}
    condLevel -->|NO| unequalCalc["USE UNEQUAL LEVEL FORMULA<br/>Compute l1 and l2 from h and l<br/>S1 equals w l1 squared over 2T0<br/>S2 equals w l2 squared over 2T0"]

    iceWind1 -->|NO| equalSimple["S equals w l squared over 8T0<br/>T at support equals T0 plus wS"]
    iceWind1 -->|YES| iceWindCalc["COMPUTE wi and ww<br/>wi equals rho ice times g times pi times t times d plus t<br/>ww equals P times d plus 2t<br/>weff equals sqrt open w plus wi close squared plus ww squared<br/>S equals weff l squared over 8T0"]

    unequalCalc --> tempCheck
    equalSimple --> tempCheck
    iceWindCalc --> tempCheck

    tempCheck{"Temperature<br/>changed?"}
    tempCheck -->|NO| finalOutput["FINAL OUTPUT<br/>Sag and Tension values"]
    tempCheck -->|YES| stringing["STRINGING EQUATION<br/>Solve cubic iteratively for T0 new<br/>T0 new squared times open T0 new minus T0 old close<br/>equals w squared l squared AE over 24 minus alpha AE delta T times T0 new<br/>Recompute S2 with new T0"]

    stringing --> finalOutput

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef calcNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class startNode,finalOutput inputNode
    class condLevel,iceWind1,tempCheck decisionNode
    class unequalCalc,equalSimple,iceWindCalc,stringing calcNode
    class finalOutput outputNode
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Vector Force Resolution for Ice + Wind Loading

This diagram shows the orthogonal vector addition used to compute the effective weight per unit length.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vertWeight["VERTICAL COMPONENT<br/>w plus wi<br/>self weight plus ice weight"] --> pythag["PYTHAGOREAN ADDITION<br/>weff equals sqrt open w plus wi close squared plus ww squared"]
    horWind["HORIZONTAL COMPONENT<br/>ww equals P times d plus 2t<br/>wind force per unit length"] --> pythag
    pythag --> resultantAngle["DIRECTION<br/>tan theta equals ww over open w plus wi close<br/>theta is the tilt angle of resultant"]
    pythag --> modSag["MODIFIED SAG FORMULA<br/>S equals weff l squared over 8T0<br/>use weff in place of bare w"]
    resultantAngle --> modSag

    classDef vectorNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef resultNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class vertWeight,horWind,resultantAngle vectorNode
    class pythag,modSag resultNode
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms "Sag" and "Tension" as applied to overhead transmission line conductors. Show that the sag in a conductor suspended between two equal level supports is given by $S = \dfrac{wl^2}{8T_0}$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sag (S):** Sag is the maximum vertical distance (dip) of the conductor from the straight line joining the two points of support. It is measured at the midpoint of the span between two adjacent supports.

**Tension (T):** Tension is the longitudinal mechanical pull developed along the conductor when it is strung between two supports. The horizontal component of this tension, $T_0$, remains constant throughout the span length.

**Derivation:** Consider a uniform conductor of weight *w* per unit length suspended between two supports A and B at equal level with span *l*. Take the origin at the lowest point O. The slope at any point P at distance *x* from O is:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wx}{T_0}$$

Integrating, $y = \dfrac{wx^2}{2T_0}$. The supports are at $x = l/2$, so:

$$S = \frac{w(l/2)^2}{2T_0} = \frac{wl^2}{8T_0} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the effect of wind pressure and ice loading on the sag of a transmission line conductor? Write the expression for the resultant weight per unit length.**

**Model Answer:**

Under adverse weather conditions, ice accumulates on the conductor surface, adding a **vertical load** $w_i$ per unit length, and wind exerts a **horizontal force** $w_w$ per unit length on the conductor.

**Ice weight per unit length:**

$$w_i = \rho_{ice} \cdot g \cdot \pi \cdot t \cdot (d + t)$$

**Wind force per unit length:**

$$w_w = P \cdot (d + 2t)$$

Since the self-weight *w* and ice weight $w_i$ act vertically, and wind force $w_w$ acts horizontally, the **resultant effective weight per unit length** is obtained by vector (Pythagorean) addition:

$$w' = \sqrt{(w + w_i)^2 + w_w^2}$$

The modified sag formula becomes:

$$S = \frac{w' l^2}{8T_0}$$

This shows that ice + wind loading **increases the sag** and thereby **increases the tension** required to maintain safe mechanical limits, or alternatively, the tension must be re-adjusted for the same sag under adverse conditions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) (7 marks)** A transmission line conductor of weight 0.5 kg/m and diameter 1.2 cm is supported between two towers of equal height with a span of 200 m. If the maximum permissible tension in the conductor is 1500 kg, calculate:
   (i) The sag.
   (ii) The vertical component of tension at the support.
   (iii) The total tension at the support.

**(b) (7 marks)** If the above conductor has an ice coating of 1 cm thickness (density 915.5 kg/m³) and is subjected to a wind pressure of 40 N/m², calculate the new sag, assuming the horizontal tension remains the same.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a):**

**Given:** $w = 0.5$ kg/m, $l = 200$ m, $T_0 = 1500$ kg (since supports are at equal level, $T_0$ is the working horizontal tension).

**(i) Sag:**

$$S = \frac{w l^2}{8 T_0} = \frac{0.5 \times (200)^2}{8 \times 1500} = \frac{0.5 \times 40000}{12000} = \frac{20000}{12000} = 1.667 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{S = 1.667 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Vertical component of tension at the support:**

The vertical force at the support equals the weight of conductor from lowest point to the support, which is $w \cdot (l/2)$:

$$V = w \cdot \frac{l}{2} = 0.5 \times \frac{200}{2} = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{V = 50 \text{ kg}}$$

**(iii) Total tension at the support:**

$$T = \sqrt{T_0^2 + V^2} = \sqrt{(1500)^2 + (50)^2} = \sqrt{2250000 + 2500} = \sqrt{2252500}$$

$$T = 1500.83 \text{ kg} \approx 1501 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1501 \text{ kg}}$$

(Alternative using the shortcut formula: $T = T_0 + wS = 1500 + 0.5 \times 1.667 = 1500 + 0.833 = 1500.83$ kg. ✓ Matches.)

---

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $d = 1.2$ cm = 0.012 m, $t = 1$ cm = 0.01 m, $\rho_{ice} = 915.5$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P = 40$ N/m², $T_0 = 1500$ kg.

**Step 1: Compute ice weight per unit length:**

$$w_i = \rho_{ice} \cdot g \cdot \pi \cdot t \cdot (d + t) = 915.5 \times 9.81 \times \pi \times 0.01 \times (0.012 + 0.01)$$

$$w_i = 915.5 \times 9.81 \times 3.1416 \times 0.01 \times 0.022$$

$$w_i = 915.5 \times 9.81 \times 3.1416 \times 0.00022 = 6.21 \text{ N/m (approx)}$$

Converting to kg/m: $w_i = 6.21 / 9.81 = 0.633$ kg/m.

**Step 2: Compute wind force per unit length:**

$$w_w = P \cdot (d + 2t) = 40 \times (0.012 + 0.02) = 40 \times 0.032 = 1.28 \text{ N/m}$$

Converting to kg/m: $w_w = 1.28 / 9.81 = 0.1305$ kg/m.

**Step 3: Compute effective weight per unit length:**

$$w' = \sqrt{(w + w_i)^2 + w_w^2} = \sqrt{(0.5 + 0.633)^2 + (0.1305)^2}$$

$$w' = \sqrt{(1.133)^2 + (0.1305)^2} = \sqrt{1.284 + 0.01703} = \sqrt{1.301}$$

$$w' = 1.141 \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 4: Compute new sag:**

$$S_{new} = \frac{w' l^2}{8 T_0} = \frac{1.141 \times (200)^2}{8 \times 1500} = \frac{1.141 \times 40000}{12000} = \frac{45640}{12000} = 3.803 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{S_{new} = 3.803 \text{ m}}$$

This shows the sag has increased from 1.667 m to 3.803 m due to ice + wind loading, which is why engineers must allow for adequate ground clearance under worst-case conditions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) (7 marks)** A transmission line conductor is strung between two supports at different levels. The horizontal span is 250 m. The difference in height between the supports is 8 m, with the higher support at 8 m above the lower. The weight per unit length of the conductor is 0.85 kg/m. If the horizontal tension is 2000 kg, find:
   (i) The position of the lowest point of the conductor (i.e., $l_1$ and $l_2$).
   (ii) The sag from the lower support and from the upper support.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the effect of temperature change on the sag and tension of a transmission line conductor. Write down the stringing equation used to compute the new tension when the temperature changes by $\Delta T$.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a):**

**Given:** $l = 250$ m, $h = 8$ m (height difference), $w = 0.85$ kg/m, $T_0 = 2000$ kg.

**Step 1: Find the horizontal distance of the lowest point from each support.**

We have the system:
$$l_1 + l_2 = 250$$
$$l_2 - l_1 = \frac{2 T_0 h}{w l} = \frac{2 \times 2000 \times 8}{0.85 \times 250} = \frac{32000}{212.5} = 150.588 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $l_1$ and $l_2$:**

$$l_1 = \frac{250 - 150.588}{2} = \frac{99.412}{2} = 49.706 \text{ m}$$

$$l_2 = \frac{250 + 150.588}{2} = \frac{400.588}{2} = 200.294 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{l_1 \approx 49.71 \text{ m}, \quad l_2 \approx 200.29 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Compute sag from the lower support ($S_1$):**

$$S_1 = \frac{w l_1^2}{2 T_0} = \frac{0.85 \times (49.71)^2}{2 \times 2000} = \frac{0.85 \times 2471.08}{4000} = \frac{2100.42}{4000} = 0.5251 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{S_1 \approx 0.525 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4: Compute sag from the upper support ($S_2$):**

$$S_2 = \frac{w l_2^2}{2 T_0} = \frac{0.85 \times (200.29)^2}{2 \times 2000} = \frac{0.85 \times 40116.08}{4000} = \frac{34098.67}{4000} = 8.5247 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{S_2 \approx 8.525 \text{ m}}$$

**Verification:** $S_2 - S_1 = 8.525 - 0.525 = 8.0$ m = h. ✓ Correct.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** Because the lower support is closer to the lowest point ($l_1 < l_2$), the sag from the lower support is small (0.525 m), while the sag from the upper support is large (8.525 m). The lowest point lies closer to the **lower** support.

---

**Part (b): Effect of Temperature on Sag and Tension:**

When the temperature of a transmission line conductor changes, its length changes due to **thermal expansion or contraction**. Additionally, the conductor stretches elastically under tension. The combined effect alters both the sag and the tension.

**When temperature increases ($\Delta T > 0$):**
- The conductor tends to expand.
- For a fixed span length, this expansion manifests as **increased sag**.
- As sag increases, the **tension decreases** (since $S \propto 1/T_0$).

**When temperature decreases ($\Delta T < 0$):**
- The conductor contracts.
- The sag decreases.
- The tension increases — this is the **critical case for cold weather**, as excessive tension can snap the conductor or topple the tower.

**The Stringing Equation (Temperature-Stress Equation):**

The conductor length in the parabolic state is:

$$L = l + \frac{w^2 l^3}{24 T_0^2}$$

The change in conductor length between two temperature states is:

$$L_2 - L_1 = \alpha l \Delta T + \frac{(T_0^{(2)} - T_0^{(1)}) \cdot l}{A E}$$

Substituting the parabolic length expressions and rearranging:

$$\boxed{(T_0^{(2)})^2 = (T_0^{(1)})^2 + \alpha A E \Delta T \left( T_0^{(2)} + T_0^{(1)} \right) - \frac{w^2 l^2 A E}{24}}$$

This is a **cubic equation** in $T_0^{(2)}$, solved iteratively by trial substitution. In field practice, this equation is used to construct a **stringing chart** (also called a **tension chart** or **sag-tension chart**), which gives the relationship between temperature and tension (or sag) for a given span, conductor, and loading condition. This chart is used by field crews during stringing operations to set the correct tension at the prevailing temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention for $\Delta T$:** Always use $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$ (final minus initial). If the conductor cools, $\Delta T$ is **negative**, which **increases** $T_0$. Forgetting the sign costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Ice and wind are PERPENDICULAR — do NOT add them algebraically.** Use $w' = \sqrt{(w + w_i)^2 + w_w^2}$. Adding $w + w_i + w_w$ is a common error.
> 3. **For unequal level supports, the sag is NOT $wl^2/(8T_0)$.** You MUST use $S_1 = wl_1^2/(2T_0)$ and $S_2 = wl_2^2/(2T_0)$ separately, after finding $l_1$ and $l_2$.
> 4. **Stringing equation is cubic in $T_0$ — no algebraic closed-form solution.** Trial substitution is the expected method in KTU exams. State your initial guess and show 2–3 iterations.
> 5. **Units must be consistent.** If $w$ is in kg/m and $T_0$ in kg, then $S$ comes out in **meters**. Do NOT mix SI (N/m, N) with gravitational (kg/m, kg) in the same expression.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Calculation of Sag and Tension**

- ⚡ **Sag is inversely proportional to tension:** $S \propto 1/T_0$. Designers balance ground clearance against conductor tension limits.

- ⚡ **Equal level supports:** Use $S = wl^2 / (8T_0)$ — the most fundamental formula of the module.

- ⚡ **Unequal level supports:** Find $l_1$ and $l_2$ from the two equations $l_1 + l_2 = l$ and $l_2 - l_1 = 2T_0 h / (wl)$. Then use $S_1 = wl_1^2/(2T_0)$ and $S_2 = wl_2^2/(2T_0)$. The lowest point is **closer to the lower support**.

- ⚡ **Ice loading:** $w_i = \rho_{ice} \cdot g \cdot \pi \cdot t \cdot (d + t)$. The ice is assumed to form a uniform cylindrical coating of thickness *t*.

- ⚡ **Wind loading:** $w_w = P \cdot (d + 2t)$. Wind acts horizontally, so it is added **perpendicularly** (Pythagorean) to the vertical loads, not algebraically.

- ⚡ **Effective weight (ice + wind):** $w' = \sqrt{(w + w_i)^2 + w_w^2}$. Use $w'$ in place of $w$ in the sag formula.

- ⚡ **Tension at support:** $T = T_0 + wS$ (for equal level) or $T = \sqrt{T_0^2 + (w l/2)^2}$ (exact form). The support tension is always slightly greater than the horizontal tension.

- ⚡ **Catenary vs. Parabolic:** Catenary is exact (uses hyperbolic cosine) but reserved for very long spans. Parabolic is approximate (uses a simple quadratic) and is used for all normal transmission line spans where $S/l \leq 1/10$.

- ⚡ **Temperature effect (Stringing Equation):** Cubic in $T_0^{(2)}$, solved by trial substitution. Heating → more sag, less tension. Cooling → less sag, **more tension** (most critical case).

- ⚡ **Stringing Chart:** Graphical/tabular representation of the stringing equation, used by field crews to determine the correct tension during stringing at any given temperature.

- ⚡ **Standard KTU values to memorize:** $\rho_{ice} = 915.5$ kg/m³, wind pressure = 39.24 N/m² (sometimes 40 N/m² in problems), $g = 9.81$ m/s².

- ⚡ **For a conductor with one support at a free end (like a single guy wire from a tower to an anchor), the sag at the free end is $S = wl^2 / (2T_0)$ — exactly twice the midspan sag of a two-support system of the same span.**

- ⚡ **Common KTU exam sub-questions:** Compute sag, compute tension at support, find lowest point position for unequal level supports, compute new sag with ice/wind, solve the stringing equation iteratively for new tension due to temperature change.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Machine Learning in Power System Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Machine Learning in Power System Protection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Machine Learning (ML) in Power System Protection** is defined as the application of computational algorithms and statistical models that learn patterns, signatures, and hidden correlations from historical and real-time power system operational data (voltage, current, impedance, harmonics, travelling waves, etc.) to perform intelligent protective functions such as **fault detection, classification, localization, and isolation**, *without being explicitly programmed with deterministic rules for every operating scenario*. The formal mathematical framing of an ML-based protection system can be expressed as a mapping function: $$f_{\theta} : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$$ where $\mathcal{X}$ is the input feature space (relay measurements), $\mathcal{Y}$ is the output decision space (fault type, location, trip signal), and $\theta$ represents the learnable parameters of the model that are optimized by minimizing a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ over a labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Junior Relay Engineer > [!NOTE] > **The "Apprentice Engineer" Analogy** Imagine a freshly graduated protection engineer joining a substation. Instead of being handed a 500-page rulebook of pre-decided relay settings for every conceivable fault, the engineer is shown **thousands of past oscillography records** (COMTRADE files) of faults and disturbances on similar power systems. By observing the **waveform shapes, impedance trajectories, and harmonic content** of each event paired with the **correct relay action** that was eventually taken, the engineer's brain gradually builds an internal "intuition." After enough exposure: - A new waveform with a sharp negative-sequence spike → *engineer instantly thinks "Line-to-Ground fault on Phase A"* - A slowly developing voltage sag with frequency drift → *engineer recognizes "Generator under-excitation, not a fault"* This is **exactly** what an ML-based protection system does. The "engineer" is a neural network, the "past records" are the training dataset, and the "intuition" is the learned decision boundary in a high-dimensional feature space. ### 1.3 Why Traditional Protection is Reaching its Limits Conventional protection schemes (overcurrent, distance, differential) rely on **fixed thresholds and pre-calculated settings**. They struggle with: > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Inverter-based Resources (IBRs)**: Modern power systems have high penetration of solar PV, wind, and HVDC links whose fault current signatures are **non-conventional, low-magnitude, and angle-dependent** — well below the assumptions of classical overcurrent coordination. > - **Bidirectional Power Flow**: Distribution networks with prosumers experience reverse power flow, invalidating directional overcurrent settings. > - **Wide-Area Phenomena**: Cascading failures, sub-synchronous oscillations, and hidden failures require coordinated intelligence across substations. > - **Data Deluge**: Modern Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) generate **TB-scale streaming data** that humans cannot process in real time. ML offers a path to **adaptive, data-driven, and self-evolving** protection that addresses these gaps. ### 1.4 Key Terminology Established in this Module > [!NOTE] > | Term | Meaning | > |------|---------| > | **Feature** | A measurable variable extracted from raw signals (e.g., RMS current, THD, impedance magnitude) | > | **Label** | The ground-truth category the model must predict (e.g., "LG fault on Phase A at 40% line length") | > | **Training Set** | Labeled historical data used to optimize the model | > | **Testing Set** | Held-out data used to evaluate generalization performance | > | **Inference** | The act of the trained model making predictions on unseen data | > | **Generalization** | The ability of a model to perform well on *unseen* data, not just memorized training data | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning The protection engineer must internalize **three distinct learning paradigms**, each suited to different protection problems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **A. Supervised Learning** > - The model is trained on a **labeled dataset** where every input $x_i$ is paired with a known correct output $y_i$. > - Used when historical fault records with confirmed classifications are available. > - **Protection Applications**: Fault type classification, fault location estimation, transformer inrush vs. internal fault discrimination. > - **Representative Algorithms**: Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Support Vector Machines (SVM), Decision Trees, Random Forest, k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN). > [!IMPORTANT] > **B. Unsupervised Learning** > - The model finds **hidden structure** in unlabeled data — it never sees the "correct answer" during training. > - Used for anomaly detection, clustering of operating regimes, and identifying previously unseen fault patterns. > - **Protection Applications**: Cyber-attack detection in IEC 61850 substation networks, novel fault signature discovery, wide-area disturbance clustering. > - **Representative Algorithms**: k-Means clustering, DBSCAN, Autoencoders, Isolation Forest, Self-Organizing Maps (SOM). > [!IMPORTANT] > **C. Reinforcement Learning (RL)** > - An **agent** interacts with an **environment** (the power system), takes **actions** (relay trip / block / change setting), and receives **rewards** (selectivity maintained, fault cleared, equipment saved). > - Used for **adaptive protection** where optimal relay coordination must evolve as the grid topology changes (e.g., distribution reconfiguration with DERs). > - **Representative Algorithms**: Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network (DQN), Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). ### 2.2 The ML-Based Protection Workflow — A Step-by-Step Logical Chain The KTU examiner expects students to describe the **end-to-end pipeline**. The six steps are: 1. **Data Acquisition**: Collect three-phase voltage and current waveforms from relays, PMUs, and digital fault recorders (DFRs). Typical sampling rate: **3.2 kHz to 4.096 kHz** for 50/60 Hz systems per **IEC 60255-118-1** for PMU compliance, or higher (10–200 kHz) for travelling-wave-based schemes. 2. **Signal Preprocessing**: Apply anti-aliasing filters, remove DC offset, perform Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) to extract fundamental phasors, and normalize the data to zero mean and unit variance. 3. **Feature Engineering**: Extract discriminative features such as: - Sequence components ($I_0, I_1, I_2$) - Impedance trajectory characteristics - Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) - Wavelet packet energy coefficients - Sample-point values within the first quarter cycle 4. **Model Training**: Feed the (feature, label) pairs into a chosen algorithm and optimize the parameters $\theta$ by minimizing the loss $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ over many **epochs** (full passes through the dataset). 5. **Validation & Testing**: Use k-fold cross-validation to tune **hyperparameters** (learning rate, number of hidden layers, regularization coefficient) and a held-out test set to estimate real-world performance. 6. **Deployment & Inference**: Integrate the trained model into the Intelligent Electronic Device (IED) for real-time decision-making within the **tripping window** (typically **< 1 cycle = 20 ms** for 50 Hz systems, or **< 4 ms** for half-cycle ultra-high-speed schemes). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master this table — it covers ~70% of the numerical questions KTU examiners set on this topic.** | # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Engineering Use in Protection | |---|------------------|------------|-------------------------------| | 1 | **Accuracy** | $A = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness of fault/no-fault classification | | 2 | **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all "fault" alarms raised, how many were real? | | 3 | **Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all actual faults, how many were detected? | | 4 | **Specificity (True Negative Rate)** | $S_p = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Ability to *avoid* false tripping on normal operation | | 5 | **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of P and R)** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 P R}{P + R}$ | Balanced single-number metric for skewed datasets | | 6 | **Perceptron Weighted Sum** | $z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$ | Pre-activation of a single artificial neuron | | 7 | **Sigmoid Activation** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Squashes neuron output to (0,1) — probability-like | | 8 | **ReLU Activation** | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Default in modern deep networks — avoids vanishing gradient | | 9 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE) Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Loss function for regression (e.g., fault location in km) | | 10 | **Cross-Entropy Loss (Binary)** | $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum [y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Loss for binary fault/no-fault classification | | 11 | **Euclidean Distance (k-NN)** | $d(x, x_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - x_{i,j})^2}$ | Distance metric in feature space for nearest-neighbor lookup | | 12 | **SVM Decision Boundary** | $w^T x + b = 0$ | Linear hyperplane separating fault from non-fault regions | | 13 | **Gradient Descent Update** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | Rule for iteratively updating model parameters | | 14 | **Sampling Rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to capture highest fault harmonic | | 15 | **Trip Time Budget** | $t_{trip} \leq 20\,\text{ms}$ (1 cycle, 50 Hz) | Hard real-time constraint for protection decisions | Where: - $TP$ = True Positives, $TN$ = True Negatives, $FP$ = False Positives, $FN$ = False Negatives - $x$ = input feature vector, $w$ = weight vector, $b$ = bias, $z$ = pre-activation scalar - $\eta$ = learning rate (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$), $\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$ = gradient of loss w.r.t. parameters ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Substation Automation**: SEL, ABB, Siemens, and GE have all introduced **ML-augmented IEDs** for travelling-wave fault location and transformer dissolved-gas analysis (DGA) interpretation. > - **Wide-Area Monitoring**: The North American **WECC** and **ERCOT** use ML on PMU streams for real-time oscillation detection and remedial action. > - **Smart Distribution**: EU projects like **INTERPRETER** and **OneNet** have demonstrated RL-based adaptive protection in 100% inverter-based microgrids. > - **Defence & Reliability**: ML models provide **defence-in-depth** by catching faults that traditional relays miss in **high-impedance fault (HIF)** and **cross-country fault** scenarios. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Perceptron Learning Rule (Worked Step-by-Step) The perceptron is the **atomic unit** of an artificial neural network and the historical foundation of all modern deep learning. The KTU examiner loves to set a derivation on this. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Setup:** A single artificial neuron receives $n$ input features $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$, computes a weighted sum, passes it through an activation, and produces a binary output $\hat{y} \in \{-1, +1\}$ for a two-class protection problem (e.g., *Fault* vs. *No-Fault*). **Step 1: Define the input vector and parameter vector.** Let the input feature vector be $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ and the weight vector be $w = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)^T$, with bias $b$. **Step 2: Compute the pre-activation (net input).** $$z = w^T x + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$ **Step 3: Apply the activation (sign function) to produce the output.** $$\hat{y} = \text{sign}(z) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } z \geq 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } z < 0 \end{cases}$$ **Step 4: Define the loss / error signal for a single training sample.** If the true label is $y$ and the predicted label is $\hat{y}$, the error is: $$e = y - \hat{y}$$ This $e \in \{-2, 0, +2\}$ since both $y$ and $\hat{y}$ are $\pm 1$. **Step 5: Use the Perceptron Convergence Rule to update weights.** The perceptron learning rule, proven by Rosenblatt in 1958, states that weights are updated **only when a misclassification occurs**. The update is: $$w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \eta \cdot e \cdot x_i$$ $$b^{\text{new}} = b^{\text{old}} + \eta \cdot e$$ **Step 6: Justify the update direction using the loss-gradient logic.** Consider a simple squared loss $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$. Taking the gradient w.r.t. weight $w_i$: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_i} = -(y - \hat{y}) \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} \frac{\partial z}{\partial w_i} = -(y - \hat{y}) \cdot \sigma'(z) \cdot x_i$$ Applying gradient *descent* with learning rate $\eta$: $$w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_i} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \eta (y - \hat{y}) \sigma'(z) x_i$$ For the perceptron's sign activation, the gradient direction is captured by the term $(y - \hat{y})$ (proportional to $e$), and the $\sigma'(z)$ collapses into a sign convention. This **reproduces the perceptron update rule** above, confirming the rule is essentially a special case of gradient descent. **Step 7: Worked Numerical Example for a Protection Scenario.** Suppose we have extracted two features from a 33 kV line: - $x_1$ = zero-sequence current magnitude in Amperes (normalized): $0.6$ - $x_2$ = negative-to-positive sequence current ratio: $0.8$ A single training sample has: - True label $y = +1$ (Fault: Line-to-Ground) - Current weights: $w_1 = 0.1, w_2 = -0.2, b = 0.05$ - Learning rate: $\eta = 0.1$ Step 7a: Compute the pre-activation. $$z = (0.1)(0.6) + (-0.2)(0.8) + 0.05 = 0.06 - 0.16 + 0.05 = -0.05$$ Step 7b: Apply the activation. $$\hat{y} = \text{sign}(-0.05) = -1 \quad (\text{No-Fault prediction — WRONG})$$ Step 7c: Compute the error. $$e = y - \hat{y} = (+1) - (-1) = 2$$ Step 7d: Update the weights. $$w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.1 + (0.1)(2)(0.6) = 0.1 + 0.12 = 0.22$$ $$w_2^{\text{new}} = -0.2 + (0.1)(2)(0.8) = -0.2 + 0.16 = -0.04$$ $$b^{\text{new}} = 0.05 + (0.1)(2) = 0.05 + 0.2 = 0.25$$ Step 7e: Verify that the updated neuron now correctly classifies the same sample. $$z^{\text{new}} = (0.22)(0.6) + (-0.04)(0.8) + 0.25 = 0.132 - 0.032 + 0.25 = 0.35$$ $$\hat{y}^{\text{new}} = \text{sign}(0.35) = +1 \quad (\text{Correct!})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: The perceptron rotated its decision boundary in feature space by increasing $w_1$ (weight on the fault-indicative zero-sequence current) and increasing the bias, thereby shifting the "fault" region to correctly include this sample. ### 3.2 Confusion-Matrix Worked Example for a Protection Classifier > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem**: A 3-month field trial of an ML-based overcurrent relay on a 110 kV line yielded the following classification counts after processing 10,000 events: | | Predicted: Fault | Predicted: No-Fault | |---|---|---| | **Actual: Fault** | TP = 420 | FN = 30 | | **Actual: No-Fault** | FP = 60 | TN = 9,490 | **Compute the four primary performance metrics.** Step 1: Total positives (actual faults) and negatives. $$P_{\text{actual}} = TP + FN = 420 + 30 = 450$$ $$N_{\text{actual}} = FP + TN = 60 + 9490 = 9550$$ Step 2: Total predictions. $$N_{\text{total}} = 420 + 30 + 60 + 9490 = 10000 \quad \checkmark$$ Step 3: Accuracy. $$A = \frac{TP + TN}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{420 + 9490}{10000} = \frac{9910}{10000} = 0.991 = 99.10\%$$ Step 4: Precision. $$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{420}{420 + 60} = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.50\%$$ Step 5: Recall (Sensitivity). $$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{420}{420 + 30} = \frac{420}{450} = 0.9333 = 93.33\%$$ Step 6: Specificity. $$S_p = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{9490}{9490 + 60} = \frac{9490}{9550} = 0.9937 = 99.37\%$$ Step 7: F1-Score. $$F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.875 \times 0.9333}{0.875 + 0.9333} = \frac{1.6333}{1.8083} = 0.9033 = 90.33\%$$ Step 8: Engineering Interpretation for KTU. > [!NOTE] > - The **high accuracy (99.10%) is misleading** because the dataset is **heavily imbalanced** (only 450 faults in 10,000 events). A trivial "always say no-fault" classifier would achieve 95.50% accuracy. This is why F1-score and recall are **mandatory** in protection assessments. > - **Recall of 93.33%** means 30 out of 450 real faults were *missed* — unacceptable in critical protection. The threshold must be tuned to push recall > 99%. > - **Precision of 87.50%** means 60 false trips were raised on healthy operation — disruptive but tolerable in some industrial systems. ### 3.3 Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) Forward Propagation — Key Equations For a network with $L$ layers, the pre-activation and activation at layer $\ell$ are: $$z^{(\ell)} = W^{(\ell)} a^{(\ell-1)} + b^{(\ell)}$$ $$a^{(\ell)} = \sigma(z^{(\ell)})$$ where: - $W^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell} \times n_{\ell-1}}$ is the weight matrix of layer $\ell$ - $b^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}}$ is the bias vector of layer $\ell$ - $a^{(0)} = x$ (the input feature vector) - $a^{(L)}$ is the final output (e.g., class probabilities after a softmax) The total learnable parameter count is: $$N_{\text{params}} = \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} \left( n_{\ell-1} \cdot n_{\ell} + n_{\ell} \right)$$ **Example**: A 3-layer MLP with input dimension $n_0 = 10$, hidden layer $n_1 = 16$, second hidden layer $n_2 = 8$, and output $n_3 = 4$ (4 fault classes) has: $$N_{\text{params}} = (10 \times 16 + 16) + (16 \times 8 + 8) + (8 \times 4 + 4)$$ $$= 176 + 136 + 36 = 348 \text{ parameters}$$ This is **extremely lightweight** by modern standards and can be deployed on a substation-grade IED with a modest ARM Cortex-A53 processor. ## 4.
