KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]** Differentiate between **supervised**, **unsupervised**, and **reinforcement** learning. Give **one specific power system protection application** of each. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > | Paradigm | Definition | Protection Application | > |----------|------------|------------------------| > | **Supervised** | Learns from labeled input–output pairs $(x_i, y_i)$; minimizes a supervised loss. | **Fault classification** — training on labeled oscillography records (LG, LL, LLG, LLL) to predict the fault type of an unseen disturbance. | > | **Unsupervised** | Discovers hidden structure in unlabeled data; no ground truth labels. | **Cyber-attack detection** in IEC 61850 substation networks using autoencoders to flag anomalous GOOSE messages that deviate from learned normal behavior. | > | **Reinforcement** | An agent learns a policy by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards/penalties. | **Adaptive relay coordination** in a distribution feeder with high DER penetration, where the agent adjusts overcurrent pickup settings to maintain selectivity under dynamic topology. | **(3 marks: 1 mark for the three definitions + 2 marks for correctly mapping one application to each paradigm.)** --- > **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]** Explain the **confusion matrix** with reference to power system fault detection. Define **accuracy**, **precision**, and **recall** in this context. **Model Answer:** A **confusion matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ table that cross-tabulates the **actual** class (rows) against the **predicted** class (columns) of a binary classifier. For a protection relay: - **True Positive (TP):** Actual fault, model says fault. ✅ - **False Negative (FN):** Actual fault, model says *no-fault*. ❌ *(most dangerous — failure to operate)* - **False Positive (FP):** Actual no-fault, model says *fault*. ❌ *(unnecessary trip — costly)* - **True Negative (TN):** Actual no-fault, model says no-fault. ✅ The three metrics are: $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} \quad \text{(overall correctness)}$$ $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \quad \text{(of all trip commands raised, how many were real faults)}$$ $$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} \quad \text{(of all real faults, what fraction was detected — the safety-critical metric)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall**: Do **not** state accuracy alone as the only metric. In protection, the cost of a missed fault (FN) is orders of magnitude higher than a false alarm, so **recall must always be reported and maximized** (typically > 99% for transmission-class protection). **(3 marks: 1 mark for the matrix layout + 2 marks for the three correct metric definitions with protection interpretation.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INSTRUCTIONS TO STUDENT:** Answer **any ONE full question** from the following two alternatives. Each question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Treatment of ML Pipeline > **[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Model]** **(a)** *Describe, with a neat block diagram, the complete **pipeline of an ML-based protection system** from raw current/voltage acquisition to the final trip command. Clearly state the role of **feature engineering** and give **five examples of features** used in fault classification. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** The complete pipeline consists of **six sequential stages** as illustrated in the diagram from Section 4.1: **Stage 1 – Data Acquisition:** Three-phase voltages and currents are sensed by **Instrument Transformers (CTs/VTs)** at the substation bus or line terminal. For 50 Hz systems, currents are typically scaled down to 1 A or 5 A secondary, and voltages to 110/$\sqrt{3}$ V phase-to-neutral. **Stage 2 – Anti-Aliasing and Sampling:** A **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ (per Nyquist) precedes the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. Typical sampling rates: 3.2–4.096 kHz for phasor-based protection, or 100–200 kHz for travelling-wave schemes. **Stage 3 – Preprocessing:** DC offset is removed, DFT (or Goertzel algorithm) extracts **fundamental phasors** $\hat{V}_a, \hat{V}_b, \hat{V}_c, \hat{I}_a, \hat{I}_b, \hat{I}_c$ with magnitude and phase, and data is normalized to zero mean, unit variance. **Stage 4 – Feature Engineering (the critical intellectual step):** Raw waveforms are transformed into a compact, discriminative feature vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$. Five example features commonly used in fault classification are: 1. **Zero-sequence current ratio** $I_0 / I_1$ — discriminates ground faults 2. **Negative-sequence current ratio** $I_2 / I_1$ — discriminates unsymmetrical faults 3. **Apparent impedance magnitude** $\vert Z \vert = V_{LL} / I_{LL}$ — feeds distance relay logic 4. **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** of the faulted phase current — identifies inrush, arcing, or HIF 5. **Wavelet packet energy** in the d1–d6 detail bands — captures transient high-frequency components of the fault inception **Stage 5 – Model Inference:** The feature vector $x$ is fed to the trained ML model $f_\theta(x)$ which outputs class probabilities or a binary decision. **Stage 6 – Decision & Trip:** A threshold check (e.g., probability > 0.95) produces a trip pulse sent to the **circuit breaker** within the **20 ms / 1-cycle** budget. The event is logged and reported to **SCADA** and the cloud historian for periodic retraining. **(Mark split: 3 marks for the block diagram in words, 2 marks for feature engineering explanation, 2 marks for the five correct feature examples.)** --- **(b)** *A 220 kV transmission line is protected by an ML-based distance relay. During a 6-month trial, the relay processes **8,000 events**. The confusion matrix obtained is:* | | Predicted: Fault | Predicted: No-Fault | |---|---|---| | **Actual: Fault** | TP = 312 | FN = 8 | | **Actual: No-Fault** | FP = 22 | TN = 7,658 | *Compute the **(i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Recall, (iv) F1-Score**, and comment on whether the relay is suitable for deployment at 220 kV. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify the confusion matrix entries.** $TP = 312, \quad FN = 8, \quad FP = 22, \quad TN = 7658$ Total events: $N = 312 + 8 + 22 + 7658 = 8000$ ✓ **Step 2: Compute Accuracy.** $$A = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{312 + 7658}{8000} = \frac{7970}{8000} = 0.99625 = 99.625\%$$ **Step 3: Compute Precision.** $$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{312}{312 + 22} = \frac{312}{334} = 0.9341 = 93.41\%$$ **Step 4: Compute Recall (Sensitivity).** $$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{312}{312 + 8} = \frac{312}{320} = 0.975 = 97.50\%$$ **Step 5: Compute F1-Score.** $$F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.9341 \times 0.975}{0.9341 + 0.975} = \frac{1.8215}{1.9091} = 0.9542 = 95.42\%$$ **Step 6: Engineering Comment for 220 kV Deployment.** > [!IMPORTANT] > - The **overall accuracy of 99.625%** is high, but the dataset is **imbalanced** (only 320 actual faults in 8,000 events ≈ 4%). > - The **recall of 97.50%** means **8 out of 320 real faults were missed** — for a 220 kV EHV line carrying hundreds of MW, missing even 1 in 40 faults is **unacceptable**. Industry standards (IEC 60255, IEEE C37.2) demand **dependability > 99%** for transmission protection. > - The **precision of 93.41%** means 22 false trips were issued on healthy conditions, disrupting customers. > - **Verdict**: The relay is **NOT yet suitable** for 220 kV deployment. The model must be retrained with (a) more fault samples, (b) cost-sensitive learning that penalizes FN more heavily, and (c) a lower decision threshold to push recall > 99.5% before field commissioning. **(Mark split: 4 × 1 mark for each metric, 3 marks for the engineering commentary and verdict.)** --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Algorithm Deep-Dive Alternative > **[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Model Alternative]** **(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the **structure and working of a single artificial neuron (perceptron)**. Derive the **perceptron learning rule** using gradient descent. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **Structure (refer to Section 4.4 block diagram):** A perceptron consists of: - **$n$ inputs** $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ (the features) - **$n$ synaptic weights** $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$ (learnable) - **One bias** $b$ (shifts the decision boundary off the origin) - **A summation junction** that computes $z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = w^T x + b$ - **An activation function** $\sigma(\cdot)$ that maps $z$ to a bounded output (e.g., $\{-1, +1\}$ for sign, or $(0, 1)$ for sigmoid) - **One output** $\hat{y} = \sigma(z)$ Mathematically: $$z = w^T x + b, \qquad \hat{y} = \sigma(z)$$ **Derivation of the Learning Rule using Gradient Descent:** **Step 1:** Define the loss function (mean squared error over a single sample): $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$$ **Step 2:** Compute the gradient of the loss w.r.t. weight $w_i$ using the chain rule: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_i} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w_i}$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = -(y - \hat{y}), \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \sigma'(z), \qquad \frac{\partial z}{\partial w_i} = x_i$$ Therefore: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_i} = -(y - \hat{y}) \, \sigma'(z) \, x_i$$ **Step 3:** Apply the gradient descent update: $$w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_i} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \eta (y - \hat{y}) \sigma'(z) x_i$$ **Step 4:** For the sign activation used in classical perceptrons, the term $\sigma'(z)$ collapses to a non-zero constant convention (or is absorbed into $\eta$), yielding the canonical **Rosenblatt perceptron rule**: $$\boxed{w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \eta \, (y - \hat{y}) \, x_i}$$ $$b^{\text{new}} = b^{\text{old}} + \eta \, (y - \hat{y})$$ **Step 5: Convergence Property (Rosenblatt 1958):** If the training data is **linearly separable**, the perceptron learning rule is guaranteed to converge to a separating hyperplane in a finite number of steps — proven via the perceptron convergence theorem. This is the foundation on which modern deep learning is built. **(Mark split: 2 marks for the diagram and architecture, 3 marks for the gradient derivation, 1 mark for stating the update rule, 1 mark for the convergence note.)** --- **(b)** *Discuss the **role of feature selection** in ML-based protection. Compare **three feature selection methods** (filter, wrapper, embedded) with one example technique each. Explain why **sequence components ($I_0, I_1, I_2$)** are particularly powerful features for fault classification in a 3-phase system. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **Why Feature Selection Matters in Protection:** > [!IMPORTANT] > Raw waveform data is high-dimensional (a 4 ms half-cycle at 4 kHz sampling = 16 samples × 6 channels = **96 features per event**). Feeding all of them to an ML model leads to: (i) the **curse of dimensionality**, (ii) overfitting on noisy samples, (iii) increased training and inference latency that may violate the 1-cycle trip budget, and (iv) poor interpretability for relay engineers. Feature selection reduces dimensionality while **preserving discriminative information**. **Comparison of Three Feature-Selection Methods:** | Method | How it works | Example Technique | Pros | Cons | |--------|--------------|-------------------|------|------| | **Filter** | Ranks features using a **statistical measure** independent of the ML model. Selects top-$k$ before training. | **Pearson correlation, ANOVA F-test, Mutual Information** | Fast, model-agnostic, low computational cost | Ignores feature interactions | | **Wrapper** | Uses the **ML model itself** as a black-box evaluator; searches over feature subsets and picks the one that maximizes model performance. | **Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE), Forward/Backward Selection** | Captures feature interactions, often higher accuracy | Computationally expensive, risk of overfitting | | **Embedded** | Feature selection is **integrated into the model training** itself via regularization. | **L1 (Lasso) regularization, Decision Tree feature importance** | Balances accuracy and speed, single-stage | Tied to a specific model family | **Why Sequence Components ($I_0, I_1, I_2$) are Powerful for Fault Classification:** > [!NOTE] > Per **Fortescue's Theorem (1918)**, any unbalanced 3-phase system can be decomposed into three balanced symmetrical components: > - **Positive sequence $I_1$**: present in all healthy and faulted conditions > - **Negative sequence $I_2$**: present **only in unsymmetrical faults** (LG, LL, LLG) > - **Zero sequence $I_0$**: present **only when current returns through earth/neutral** (i.e., LG and LLG faults) > > Using ratios like $I_0/I_1$ and $I_2/I_1$ creates a **fingerprint** for each of the 11 standard fault types: > > | Fault Type | $I_0$ | $I_2$ | Discriminator | > |-----------|-------|-------|---------------| > | **LG** (Phase A) | Large | Moderate | $I_0 \gg 0$ AND $I_2 > 0$ | > | **LL** (A-B) | Zero | Large | $I_0 = 0$ AND $I_2 \gg 0$ | > | **LLG** (A-B-G) | Large | Large | $I_0 > 0$ AND $I_2 > 0$ AND high $I_1$ | > | **LLL** (symmetrical) | Zero | Zero | $I_0 = 0$ AND $I_2 \approx 0$ | > > This **decomposition collapses 6 correlated raw signals into 3 independent, physically meaningful, and low-noise features** — a textbook example of domain-driven feature engineering. **(Mark split: 2 marks for the importance of feature selection, 3 marks for the three methods with examples, 2 marks for the sequence-components explanation.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic** > > 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall** — These are swapped by ~30% of students. Mnemonic: *Precision = "of the positive predictions, how precise were they?"*; *Recall = "of all actual positives, how many did the model recall?"* > 2. **Reporting only Accuracy for imbalanced data** — Always accompany with F1 and recall; otherwise the examiner deducts 1–2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the 1-cycle (20 ms) trip-time budget** — A "perfect" offline classifier is useless if inference takes 500 ms. Always mention real-time constraints. > 4. **Confusing "Fault Classification" (categorical) with "Fault Location" (regression)** — Classification uses Cross-Entropy loss; Location uses MSE. Markers deduct 1 mark if the loss function is mismatched. > 5. **Citing Python code in a non-CS paper** — Strictly avoid. Write only mathematical formulations and algorithmic flow. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam** - [x] **Definition**: ML in protection = data-driven learning of relay decisions from labeled/unlabeled historical events. - [x] **Three Paradigms**: Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (no labels), Reinforcement (reward-based). - [x] **Six-Stage Pipeline**: Acquisition → Anti-aliasing → ADC → Preprocessing → Feature Engineering → Model → Decision → Trip. - [x] **Hard Real-Time Constraint**: Trip decision must arrive within **20 ms (1 cycle, 50 Hz)**. - [x] **Five Key Features**: $I_0, I_2/I_1$, impedance magnitude, THD, wavelet packet energy. - [x] **Confusion Matrix**: TP, FN, FP, TN — and the formulas for $A, P, R, F_1$ — **memorize the table**. - [x] **Perceptron Rule**: $w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \eta (y - \hat{y}) x_i$ — derived via gradient descent on MSE. - [x] **Activation Functions**: Sigmoid (output ∈ (0,1)), ReLU (output ∈ [0, ∞)), Sign (output ∈ {-1,+1}). - [x] **Loss Functions**: Cross-Entropy for classification, MSE for regression (fault location). - [x] **Gradient Descent Update**: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$. - [x] **Three Feature-Selection Methods**: Filter (e.g., Mutual Information), Wrapper (e.g., RFE), Embedded (e.g., L1/Lasso). - [x] **Sequence Components (Fortescue)**: $I_0 \Rightarrow$ ground involvement, $I_2 \Rightarrow$ asymmetry, $I_1 \Rightarrow$ always present. - [x] **Industry Standards**: IEC 60255-118-1 (PMU), IEEE C37.2 (device numbering), IEC 61850 (substation communication). - [x] **Common Applications**: Fault classification, location, HIF detection, inrush discrimination, adaptive coordination, cyber-attack detection. - [x] **Pitfall Avoidance**: Always report **recall** for protection; never quote only accuracy. > **End of Module 4 Topic — "Introduction to Machine Learning in Power System Protection"** > *Next study unit: Adaptive protection schemes and wide-area monitoring using PMUs.*

topics.title: Insulation Co-Ordination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insulation Co-Ordination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insulation Co-ordination** is the systematic process of correlating the insulation strength of electrical equipment (transformers, circuit breakers, bus bars, cables, switchgear) with the characteristics of protective devices (surge arresters, spark gaps, non-linear resistors) so that the system operates reliably and economically when subjected to overvoltages of various origins.

In more rigorous terms, insulation co-ordination is the **engineering discipline** of selecting the dielectric strength of equipment in relation to the **expected overvoltages** appearing at the terminals or within the network, ensuring a **predetermined margin of safety** against insulation failure while keeping the cost of insulation to an economically justifiable minimum.

The two key technical quantities governing insulation co-ordination are:

- **BIL (Basic Insulation Level)** — the rated lightning impulse withstand voltage, expressed as a **peak value in kV** (1.2/50 µs impulse wave).
- **SIL (Switching Impulse Level)** — the rated switching impulse withstand voltage, applicable for voltages above **300 kV**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Helmet and the Road":**
> Imagine a motorcyclist riding on a road full of potholes (these potholes represent **overvoltages**). The helmet worn by the rider represents the **insulation** of the equipment. A well-designed helmet must be strong enough to withstand moderate impacts, but we cannot make it infinitely thick (unaffordable, heavy, impractical). So the rider also travels at a safe speed (representing the **protective device** — the surge arrester — that limits the overvoltage magnitude). Insulation co-ordination is therefore the process of *matching* the helmet strength with the speed control, ensuring the rider survives in a **statistically safe** manner, with reasonable cost.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Lightning Impulse Waveform:** **1.2/50 µs** (front time / tail time to half value).
- **Standard Switching Impulse Waveform:** **250/2500 µs**.
- **Standard Reference Atmospheric Conditions:** Temperature = **20 °C**, Pressure = **101.325 kPa**, Humidity = **11 g/m³**.
- **Altitude Correction Factor (Kt):** Insulation derating above **1000 m** altitude.
- **Standard BIL values** for systems up to **245 kV** are tabulated in **IEC 60071-1 / IS 2165**.

### 1.4 Necessity of Insulation Co-ordination

The need arises due to:
1. **Diverse overvoltage stresses** — lightning, switching, temporary, very fast transient (VFTO).
2. **Economic constraints** — over-insulation is expensive and physically bulky.
3. **System reliability** — under-insulation leads to catastrophic failures and outages.
4. **Standardization** — common platform for design, testing, and procurement worldwide.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Overvoltages

Overvoltages are broadly classified into four types based on their **shape, duration, and origin**:

| Sl. No. | Type of Overvoltage | Cause | Duration | Magnitude (p.u. of peak) |
|:------:|:--------------------|:------|:--------:|:------------------------:|
| 1 | **Lightning Overvoltage** | Direct or indirect lightning stroke on line | Very short (µs) | Very high (up to 20 p.u.) |
| 2 | **Switching Overvoltage** | Line energization, load rejection, fault clearing | Short (ms) | Moderate to high (2–4 p.u.) |
| 3 | **Temporary Overvoltage** | Load rejection, Ferranti effect, earth faults | Long (s) | 1.1–1.5 p.u. |
| 4 | **Very Fast Transient (VFTO)** | Disconnector operation in GIS | Sub-µs | Up to 3 p.u. |

> [!NOTE]
> For **EHV systems (> 300 kV)**, switching surges dominate the insulation design, while for **HV systems (< 300 kV)**, lightning surges govern the BIL selection.

### 2.2 Types of Insulation

Insulation in any power system apparatus is classified as:

- **External Insulation:** The medium between live conductors and earth across **air, SF₆, or oil** outside the solid dielectric. Vulnerable to atmospheric conditions (humidity, pollution, temperature, altitude).
- **Internal Insulation:** The solid, liquid, or gaseous dielectric **enclosed within the equipment** (e.g., oil-impregnated paper in a transformer, epoxy resin in a bushing). Independent of atmospheric conditions.
- **Self-restoring Insulation:** Insulation that recovers its dielectric strength after a flashover (e.g., **air insulation**).
- **Non-self-restoring Insulation:** Insulation that loses its dielectric strength permanently after breakdown (e.g., **oil-impregnated paper, XLPE cable insulation**).

### 2.3 Volt-Time Characteristics (V-T Curves)

The **volt-time curve** represents the relationship between the **flashover / withstand voltage** of an insulation and the **time to flashover** for a given impulse waveform.

- The **right portion** of the curve (long times) gives the **power-frequency withstand voltage**.
- The **left portion** (short times) gives the **impulse withstand voltage**.
- The **knee point** separates switching impulse region from lightning impulse region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **co-ordination**, the protective device characteristic curve must lie **below** the insulation volt-time curve at all instants of time, leaving a **protective margin** between them.

### 2.4 Basic Insulation Level (BIL) and Standard Levels

The **BIL** is the **crest value of the standard lightning impulse (1.2/50 µs)** that the insulation must withstand without failure.

**Standard BIL values (selected, as per IEC 60071-1):**

| System Voltage (kV rms) | BIL (kV peak) | Power-frequency Withstand (kV rms) |
|:-----------------------:|:-------------:|:----------------------------------:|
| 11 | 75 | 28 |
| 33 | 170 | 70 |
| 66 | 325 | 140 |
| 110 | 450 | 230 |
| 132 | 550 | 275 |
| 220 | 900 / 1050 | 460 |
| 400 | 1425 / 1550 | 630 / 680 |
| 765 | 2400 / 2550 | 830 / 880 |

### 2.5 Protective Devices Used in Insulation Co-ordination

- **Spark Gaps (Rod, Sphere, Horn):** Simple, self-restoring, but with poor arc-quenching.
- **Non-linear Resistor Surge Arresters (ZnO / Metal Oxide):** Modern, with **highly non-linear V-I characteristic**, no gap, excellent protective margin.
- **Valve-type Arresters (SiC with gaps):** Older technology, replaced by ZnO.
- **Trigatron / Expulsion Arresters:** Used in distribution systems.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description |
|:-:|:----------------------|:------------|
| 1 | $V_{protect} \le V_{insulation} \times k_s$ | Fundamental coordination inequality; $k_s$ = safety factor |
| 2 | $k_s = \dfrac{V_{insulation}}{V_{protective\,level}}$ | Safety factor definition |
| 3 | $V_{LI} = V_{residual} \times k_s$ | Lightning impulse co-ordination |
| 4 | $V_{SI} = V_{residual}^{sw} \times k_s$ | Switching impulse co-ordination |
| 5 | $K_a = \dfrac{1}{1.0 - m \cdot \dfrac{H - 1000}{8150}}$ | Altitude correction factor (H in m, for H > 1000 m) |
| 6 | $V_{corrected} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{K_a}$ | Voltage derated for altitude |
| 7 | $\delta = \dfrac{b}{101.325} \cdot \dfrac{293}{273 + t}$ | Atmospheric density factor |
| 8 | $V_{ins} = V_{std} \cdot \delta^m$ | Insulation corrected for air density |
| 9 | $M = \dfrac{BIL - BPL}{BPL}$ | Protective margin in per-unit |
| 10 | $V_{crest} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot 1.1$ | Maximum system voltage to phase peak (1.1 p.u. factor) |
| 11 | $R_{prot} = \dfrac{BIL}{SIL_{arrester}}$ | Protection ratio of arrester |
| 12 | $V_{coord} = \max(V_{PF}, V_{LI}, V_{SI})$ | Coordinated withstand voltage (highest of three) |
| 13 | $RFOV = 1.4 \cdot V_{m}$ | Rated temporary overvoltage (factor × peak) |
| 14 | $S_{arrester} = I_n \cdot V_{res} \cdot t$ | Arrester energy absorption capacity |
| 15 | $V_{ins\,min} = K_s \cdot V_{TOV}$ | Minimum insulation for temporary overvoltage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These relationships are used by **design engineers in utilities, transformer manufacturers (BHEL, ABB, Siemens), and switchgear industries** to specify test voltages, select surge arrester ratings, and perform **insulation co-ordination studies using EMTP/ATP software** for substation design.

### 2.7 Step-by-Step Co-ordination Procedure (Conventional Deterministic Method)

1. Determine the **maximum system voltage** $V_m$ (typically 1.1 p.u. of nominal).
2. Identify all **sources of overvoltage** and their **expected magnitudes**.
3. Select a **protective level** (BPL — Basic Protective Level) from the arrester characteristic.
4. Add a **safety margin** (typically 15–25% for self-restoring, 20–30% for non-self-restoring).
5. Choose a **standard BIL** ≥ computed withstand value.
6. Verify that the **chosen BIL is available** in the standard tabulated list.
7. Apply **altitude and atmospheric corrections** if site elevation > 1000 m.
8. Confirm **co-ordination** by plotting arrester V-T curve and insulation V-T curve on a common graph and ensuring the gap is acceptable.

### 2.8 Statistical Method of Insulation Co-ordination

Used for **EHV/UHV systems** where the overvoltage magnitude is **statistically distributed** (e.g., switching surge distribution).

- The **risk of failure (R)** is defined as:

$$R = P(O > W) = \int_{W}^{\infty} f_o(V) \, dV$$

where $f_o(V)$ is the probability density of overvoltages, and $W$ is the insulation withstand.

- An **acceptable risk** of $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ per year is used in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Altitude Correction Factor

The dielectric strength of air decreases with increasing altitude because of reduced air density. The **IEC 60071-2** altitude correction factor is derived from the Paschen's law modification.

Starting from the assumption that the breakdown voltage of an air gap is proportional to the **air density** $\delta$:

$$V_b = V_{b,0} \cdot \delta^m$$

where $V_{b,0}$ is the breakdown voltage at standard conditions and $m$ is an exponent (m = 1 for homogeneous fields, m ≈ 0.5–0.8 for non-uniform fields, commonly taken as **m = 1.0** for clearance calculations).

The **air density** at altitude $H$ is:

$$\delta = \frac{p}{p_0} \cdot \frac{273 + t_0}{273 + t}$$

Assuming standard temperature (t = t₀), the pressure ratio alone is used:

$$\delta = \left(1 - \frac{L \cdot H}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{gM}{RL}}$$

Using the standard atmosphere model with $L = 0.0065\,K/m$ and substituting numerical values for constants, the IEC formula simplifies to:

$$K_a = \frac{1}{1.0 - m \cdot \dfrac{H - 1000}{8150}}$$

**For standard lightning impulse, m = 1.0:**

$$K_a = \frac{1}{1.0 - \dfrac{H - 1000}{8150}}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Altitude Correction

**Problem:** A substation is located at an altitude of **2500 m**. The standard BIL for a 220 kV system is **900 kV (peak)**. Find the derated BIL applicable at the site.

**Solution:**

Given: $H = 2500\,m$, $m = 1.0$ (for lightning impulse), Standard BIL = $900\,kV$.

Step 1 — Calculate the altitude correction factor:

$$K_a = \frac{1}{1.0 - \dfrac{2500 - 1000}{8150}}$$

$$K_a = \frac{1}{1.0 - \dfrac{1500}{8150}}$$

$$K_a = \frac{1}{1.0 - 0.1840}$$

$$K_a = \frac{1}{0.8160}$$

$$K_a = 1.2255$$

Step 2 — Apply the correction to the standard BIL:

$$V_{ins,site} = \frac{V_{ins,std}}{K_a} = \frac{900}{1.2255}$$

$$V_{ins,site} = 734.39\,kV\,(peak)$$

Step 3 — Interpretation: Since the derated BIL must be **higher** than the derated value, the **required** insulation level at the site would be **900 kV (the higher of the two)**. Hence, a higher-rated standard BIL must be selected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The corrected insulation must always be **greater than or equal to** the actual required withstand, ensuring the equipment survives the stress at altitude.

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum System Voltage (Phase Peak)

For a system with nominal rms line-to-line voltage $V_L$ and a 1.1 p.u. overvoltage factor:

$$V_{phase\,peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot 1.1$$

For a **400 kV** system:

$$V_{phase\,peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot 1.1 = 1.4142 \cdot 230.94 \cdot 1.1$$

$$V_{phase\,peak} = 359.27\,kV\,(peak)$$

This represents the **maximum continuous operating voltage** that the surge arrester must withstand thermally.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Coordination with Surge Arrester

**Problem:** A **132 kV** substation is to be protected by a ZnO surge arrester. The arrester has a **residual voltage of 290 kV (peak)** for a 10 kA, 8/20 µs lightning discharge. Determine the required **BIL** using a safety factor of 1.25 (for non-self-restoring insulation).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the protective level (BPL) from arrester:
$$V_{BPL} = V_{residual} = 290\,kV\,(peak)$$

Step 2 — Apply safety factor:
$$V_{insulation\,required} = k_s \cdot V_{BPL} = 1.25 \times 290$$

$$V_{insulation\,required} = 362.5\,kV\,(peak)$$

Step 3 — Select the next higher standard BIL from IEC 60071-1 table:
For 132 kV system, the next standard BIL ≥ 362.5 kV is **BIL = 450 kV (peak)** (also 550 kV is used for high pollution areas).

Step 4 — Verify protective margin:
$$M = \frac{BIL - BPL}{BPL} \times 100 = \frac{450 - 290}{290} \times 100 = 55.17\%$$

This margin is acceptable (typically ≥ 20% for non-self-restoring insulation).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Co-ordination Approaches

| Parameter | Deterministic Method | Statistical Method |
|:----------|:-------------------:|:------------------:|
| Overvoltage magnitude | Single fixed value (e.g., 2.5 p.u.) | Probability distribution |
| Insulation strength | Single value | Probability distribution |
| Margin | Fixed (e.g., 25%) | Risk of failure (e.g., $10^{-3}$) |
| Application | HV systems (< 300 kV) | EHV/UHV (> 300 kV) |
| Standard reference | IEC 60071-2 Annex A | IEC 60071-2 Annex B |
| Economy | Conservative | Optimum |

### 3.6 Step-by-Step Insulation Selection Algorithm

1. **Step A:** Compute the **highest system voltage** $V_m$:

$$V_m = k_1 \cdot V_n$$

where $k_1 = 1.1$ for systems up to 245 kV, $k_1 = 1.05$ for > 245 kV.

2. **Step B:** Determine the **temporary overvoltage level** (TOV):

$$V_{TOV} = k_2 \cdot \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

3. **Step C:** Determine the **slow-front (switching) overvoltage**:

$$V_{SI} = k_3 \cdot V_{TOV}$$

where $k_3 = 1.0$ to $1.5$.

4. **Step D:** Select arrester **rated voltage** $V_r$ such that:

$$V_r \geq \dfrac{V_{TOV}}{0.8}$$

5. **Step E:** Read the **residual voltage** of the arrester at the **switching current** (e.g., 1 kA or 2 kA).

6. **Step F:** Apply safety factor $k_s$ to obtain required insulation level.

7. **Step G:** Choose the **nearest higher** standard BIL from the table.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Insulation Co-ordination Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: System Voltage Level Vn] --> B[Determine Maximum System Voltage Vm]
    B --> C[Identify Overvoltage Sources]
    C --> D1[Lightning Surges]
    C --> D2[Switching Surges]
    C --> D3[Temporary Overvoltages]
    C --> D4[VFTO]
    D1 --> E[Select Surge Arrester Type and Rating]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Obtain Arrester V-T Characteristic and Residual Voltage]
    F --> G[Apply Safety Factor Ks]
    G --> H[Determine Required Insulation Withstand Level]
    H --> I[Select Nearest Standard BIL from IEC Table]
    I --> J[Apply Altitude and Atmospheric Correction]
    J --> K[Plot V-T Curves: Arrester and Insulation]
    K --> L{Protective Margin Satisfactory?}
    L -->|Yes| M[FINAL INSULATION LEVEL SPECIFIED]
    L -->|No| N[Re-select Higher BIL or Better Arrester]
    N --> E
```

### 4.2 Insulation Hierarchy in a Substation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transformer Windings: Non-self-restoring Insulation] --> A1[Internal Oil-Paper: Highest BIL Required]
    B[Switchgear: SF6 and Air Insulation] --> B1[Self-restoring: Moderate BIL]
    C[Current Transformers CT] --> C1[Oil-Paper: High BIL]
    D[Voltage Transformers VT] --> D1[Oil-Paper: High BIL]
    E[Busbars and Connectors] --> E1[Air Insulation: Moderate BIL]
    F[Surge Arrester] --> F1[Lowest Protective Level Sets the Reference]
    F1 -.->|Limits| A
    F1 -.->|Limits| B
    F1 -.->|Limits| C
    F1 -.->|Limits| D
    F1 -.->|Limits| E
```

### 4.3 Volt-Time Characteristics — Co-ordination Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Arrester V-T Curve] ---|Time axis 0.1 us to 1 s| B[Insulation V-T Curve]
    A --> A1[Protective Level Zone]
    B --> B1[Withstand Zone]
    A1 -.->|Protective Margin| B1
    C[Lightning Impulse Region] --> A
    D[Switching Impulse Region] --> B
    E[Power Frequency Region] --> B
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Insulation Level Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Air Clearances External Insulation] --> B1[Bushings]
    A2[Oil-Paper Internal Insulation] --> B1
    A3[SF6 Gas Insulation] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Equipment Specific BIL Rating]
    C1 --> D1[Coordinated Insulation Level]
    D1 --> E1[Reliable and Economical Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The surge arrester defines the lowest reference level, and progressively higher insulation strengths are designed for the connected equipment to ensure the arrester **always operates first** during an overvoltage event.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the term "Insulation Co-ordination" as applied to high-voltage power systems. State its necessity.**

**Model Answer:**
Insulation co-ordination is the process of selecting the dielectric strength of equipment in relation to the overvoltages that may appear in the system, ensuring a predetermined margin of safety against insulation failure while optimizing cost. It correlates the insulation characteristics of equipment with the protective device (surge arrester) characteristics. Necessity arises because of (i) variety of overvoltages — lightning, switching, temporary, (ii) economic constraints preventing over-insulation, (iii) need for system reliability and standardization across manufacturers and utilities.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is meant by Basic Insulation Level (BIL)? Mention the standard impulse wave used for BIL testing.**

**Model Answer:**
BIL is the crest value of the standard **1.2/50 µs lightning impulse voltage** that the insulation must withstand without flashover or puncture. The "1.2 µs" is the virtual front time and "50 µs" is the virtual time to half-value on the tail. BIL is expressed in kV peak.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Model Question Module 4 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the **volt-time characteristics** of a non-self-restoring insulation. How is the volt-time curve used in the co-ordination of insulation with surge arresters?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **220 kV** substation is protected by a ZnO surge arrester with a rated voltage of 198 kV rms. The arrester has a **maximum residual voltage of 470 kV (peak)** for a 10 kA, 8/20 µs lightning current. Calculate (i) the protection ratio, (ii) the required BIL with a safety factor of 1.20 for self-restoring insulation, and (iii) the protective margin in per-unit.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The volt-time curve of non-self-restoring insulation is plotted as **withstand voltage (kV peak) on the y-axis** versus **time to flashover (µs) on the x-axis** for various impulse front times.

For a non-self-restoring insulation (e.g., oil-impregnated paper in a transformer), once the breakdown occurs, the insulation is permanently damaged. Hence the curve represents the **withstand boundary**.

Key points:
- At very short times (< 1 µs), the insulation withstands very high voltages.
- The **knee of the curve** at around 1–10 µs separates the lightning impulse region from the switching impulse region.
- At long times (> 100 µs), the curve flattens to the **power-frequency withstand value**.

**Co-ordination use:** The surge arrester V-T curve is plotted on the same graph. The arrester curve must lie **below and to the left** of the insulation V-T curve. The vertical distance (in voltage) at any instant of time is the **protective margin**, ensuring the arrester conducts first, clipping the overvoltage before it reaches the insulation level.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- System voltage = 220 kV (rms)
- Arrester rated voltage = 198 kV (rms)
- Residual voltage $V_{res} = 470\,kV$ (peak)
- Safety factor $k_s = 1.20$

**(i) Protection Ratio:**

$$R_{prot} = \frac{V_{residual\,peak}}{V_{rated\,peak}} = \frac{470}{\sqrt{2} \times 198}$$

$$R_{prot} = \frac{470}{280.0} = 1.679$$

**(ii) Required BIL:**

$$BIL_{required} = k_s \cdot V_{residual} = 1.20 \times 470$$

$$BIL_{required} = 564\,kV\,(peak)$$

**Nearest standard BIL** (from IEC 60071-1 for 220 kV): **BIL = 900 kV (peak)** (other option 1050 kV).

**(iii) Protective Margin in per-unit:**

$$M = \frac{BIL - V_{residual}}{V_{residual}} = \frac{900 - 470}{470}$$

$$M = 0.914\,p.u.\; or \; 91.4\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Many students confuse the **rated voltage** (rms) with **residual voltage** (peak) of the surge arrester. Always convert rms to peak using $\sqrt{2}$ when computing the protection ratio.
> 2. Always select the **next higher standard BIL** from the IEC table — never use the calculated value directly.
> 3. The protection ratio is **dimensionless**; do not write units.
> 4. State assumptions explicitly (atmospheric conditions, altitude, type of insulation).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **classification of overvoltages** in a power system. Which overvoltage dominates the insulation design of (i) a 66 kV substation and (ii) a 400 kV substation? Justify.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 400 kV substation is located at an altitude of **1800 m**. The standard BIL for 400 kV is **1425 kV (peak)**. Calculate (i) the altitude correction factor, (ii) the equivalent insulation level at the site, and (iii) the next higher standard BIL to be used. State the standards reference.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Overvoltages are classified as:

1. **Lightning Overvoltages** — caused by direct or indirect lightning strokes; characterized by very steep wavefront (1.2/50 µs), high magnitude (up to 20 p.u.), short duration.
2. **Switching Overvoltages** — caused by line energization, load rejection, capacitor switching, fault clearing; characterized by 250/2500 µs wave, moderate magnitude (2–4 p.u.).
3. **Temporary Overvoltages** — caused by load rejection, single-line-to-ground faults, Ferranti effect; long duration (seconds), low magnitude (1.1–1.5 p.u.).
4. **Very Fast Transient Overvoltages (VFTO)** — caused by disconnector operation in GIS; sub-microsecond front.

**(i) 66 kV substation:** Lightning overvoltages dominate, as the system is in the **low overvoltage range** and switching surges are naturally limited by system impedance.

**(ii) 400 kV substation:** Switching overvoltages dominate, as at EHV levels the BIL for switching impulses can be higher than that for lightning impulses, due to the **longer duration** of switching surges stressing the insulation more.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** H = 1800 m, $V_{BIL,std} = 1425\,kV$, m = 1.0.

**(i) Altitude Correction Factor:**

$$K_a = \frac{1}{1.0 - \dfrac{1800 - 1000}{8150}}$$

$$K_a = \frac{1}{1.0 - \dfrac{800}{8150}}$$

$$K_a = \frac{1}{1.0 - 0.0982}$$

$$K_a = \frac{1}{0.9018} = 1.1090$$

**(ii) Equivalent Insulation Level at Site:**

$$V_{ins,site} = \frac{V_{BIL,std}}{K_a} = \frac{1425}{1.1090}$$

$$V_{ins,site} = 1284.94\,kV\,(peak)$$

**(iii) Next Higher Standard BIL:** Since the derated value (1284.94 kV) is less than 1425 kV, the chosen BIL is **1425 kV (peak)**, and it remains the recommended level. The next higher standard option in IEC 60071-1 for 400 kV is **1550 kV (peak)**.

**Standard reference:** **IEC 60071-1 / IS 2165 (Part 1)** — Insulation Co-ordination — Definitions, Principles and Rules.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Always mention the **value of m** used (m = 1.0 for lightning impulse, m = 0.5–0.75 for switching impulse) — students often forget this.
> 2. The **numerator in the altitude correction formula** is $(H - 1000)$ m, **not** H. Some students incorrectly use H directly.
> 3. Altitude correction is **not** applied below 1000 m (Kt = 1.0).
> 4. State the **standard reference** explicitly to score full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Examination:**

- **Insulation Co-ordination** = matching insulation strength with protective device characteristics for **reliability + economy**.
- **BIL** = peak value of **1.2/50 µs** lightning impulse withstand voltage (in kV).
- **SIL** = peak value of **250/2500 µs** switching impulse withstand voltage (in kV).
- **Switching surges dominate** for voltages **> 300 kV**; **lightning surges dominate** for voltages **< 300 kV**.
- **Self-restoring insulation** = air, SF₆; **Non-self-restoring** = oil-paper, XLPE.
- **Safety factor**: 1.15–1.25 for self-restoring, 1.20–1.30 for non-self-restoring insulation.
- **Altitude correction** is needed only for **H > 1000 m**; formula: $K_a = \dfrac{1}{1.0 - m \cdot (H - 1000)/8150}$.
- **Atmospheric density factor** $\delta$ also affects insulation, especially in **humid/tropical climates** of Kerala.
- **Surge arrester** provides the **lowest reference protective level (BPL)** — equipment is graded above this.
- **Standard waveforms**: Lightning (1.2/50 µs), Switching (250/2500 µs), Power frequency (50/60 Hz).
- **IEC 60071-1** = Insulation Co-ordination definitions and rules; **IEC 60071-2** = Application guide.
- **Statistical method** uses **risk of failure** $R = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ per year for EHV/UHV.
- **Protection ratio** $R_{prot} = V_{residual\,peak} / V_{rated\,peak}$ — should be as low as possible (≈ 1.6–1.8 for modern ZnO).
- **Protective margin** $M = (BIL - BPL)/BPL$ — must be ≥ 20% for non-self-restoring insulation.
- **Maximum system voltage** $V_m = 1.1 \cdot V_n$ (up to 245 kV) and $1.05 \cdot V_n$ (above 245 kV).
- Always **convert rms to peak** using $\sqrt{2}$ when comparing with BIL (which is in peak).
- **ZnO (Metal Oxide) arresters** are gapless and have highly **non-linear V-I characteristic**, offering superior protection compared to old SiC gapped arresters.
- For **Kerala grid scenarios**: Coastal humidity and hilly terrain (Western Ghats up to 2700 m) make **altitude + pollution corrections** critical.
- **Procedure to remember**: Identify $V_m$ → Identify overvoltages → Choose arrester → Apply safety factor → Pick standard BIL → Apply corrections → Plot V-T curves.
- **Common mistake**: Students forget the factor of $\sqrt{2}$ and $\sqrt{3}$ in phase-to-line voltage conversions.
- The **knee of the V-T curve** indicates the **critical time region** where insulation transitions from impulse to power-frequency behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Communication: PLCC, Fibre Optic, Introduction to IEC61850
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET501 - POWER GENERATION, TRANSMISSION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Communication: PLCC, Fibre Optic, Introduction to IEC61850

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Power Line Carrier Communication (PLCC)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> Power Line Carrier Communication (PLCC) is a wired communication methodology employed in high-voltage (HV) and extra-high-voltage (EHV) power systems, where the existing overhead shield wires or phase conductors are used as the transmission medium for superimposing high-frequency signals (typically in the band of **30 kHz to 500 kHz**) over the standard 50 Hz power frequency, enabling teleprotection, voice, data, and SCADA telemetry across substations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** PLCC is the oldest and most ubiquitous utility-grade communication medium for transmission-level protection signalling. It does not require a dedicated communication cable because the power line itself acts as the medium.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners):**
Imagine a busy two-lane highway where trucks carrying goods (the 50 Hz power) constantly travel. Now suppose a motorcycle (the high-frequency carrier signal) drives in the same lane, carefully synchronised so it does not collide with the trucks. The motorcycle carries letters (data, voice, trip commands) between cities (substations) without needing a separate parallel road. **PLCC works exactly like this** — the high-voltage line is doing two jobs simultaneously: transmitting bulk power AND carrying communication signals. Special devices called *Line Traps* prevent the motorcycle from accidentally entering substations and causing accidents.

### 1.2 Fibre Optic Communication in Power Systems

**Formal Definition:**
> Fibre Optic Communication is a dielectric, light-based transmission technology that uses extremely thin strands of high-purity silica glass (typical core diameter **8–62.5 µm**) to propagate modulated infrared light pulses (at wavelengths centred at **850 nm, 1300 nm, or 1550 nm**), offering immunity to electromagnetic interference, very high bandwidth (in the order of **GHz**), and extremely low attenuation (≈ **0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm**). In power systems, optical fibres are integrated with overhead ground wires (OPGW) or installed as standalone ADSS cables along transmission line corridors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The examiner often asks: "Why is fibre optic preferred over copper in EHV substations?" — The core answer is *Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) immunity* and *dielectric isolation* between high-voltage equipment and the control room.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of fibre optic cable as a long, thin, mirrored hallway. When you flash a torch down the hallway, the light bounces off the walls and reaches the other end, even if the hallway bends around corners. No matter how loud the surrounding construction noise (electrical noise from a 400 kV substation) becomes, the light signal is unaffected. **This is the core engineering appeal of fibre in a substation.**

### 1.3 IEC 61850 — Substation Communication Standard

**Formal Definition:**
> IEC 61850 is the international standard titled *"Communication networks and systems for power utility automation"*, published by the International Electrotechnical Commission. It defines a unified architecture for **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)**, data models, communication services, and engineering workflows, achieving semantic interoperability across multivendor substation equipment. The standard was first released in **2003** and continues to be revised (current edition broadly aligned with the **2024** framework).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** IEC 61850 is not just a "communication protocol" — it is a **complete information model** that standardises *what* data means (semantics), *how* it is structured (object modelling using Logical Nodes), and *how* it is exchanged (services like GOOSE, MMS, Sampled Values).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Before IEC 61850, imagine a marketplace where every vendor (Siemens, ABB, GE, Schneider) spoke a different dialect. You needed a personal translator for each one. IEC 61850 is like inventing a **universal language** (Esperanto for substation automation) where every device from every manufacturer uses the *same vocabulary* and *same grammar* to describe the same real-world things — a circuit breaker, a current transformer, a protection trip. Now a single engineer can talk to all devices seamlessly.

---

### 1.4 Summary Snapshot — The Three Pillars of Substation Communication

| Pillar | Medium | Frequency / Wavelength | Primary Use Case | Key Strength |
|--------|--------|------------------------|------------------|--------------|
| **PLCC** | HV overhead conductor | 30–500 kHz | Teleprotection, Voice | No new cable needed |
| **Fibre Optic** | Silica glass (light) | 850/1300/1550 nm | SCADA, IED networking, Protection | EMI-immune, high BW |
| **IEC 61850** | Logical (over Ethernet) | Application layer | Inter-IED data exchange | Vendor interoperability |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PLCC — Architecture and Functional Blocks

PLCC systems consist of four primary hardware blocks that work in concert:

1. **Line Trap (Wave Trap / Line Coupling Device):**
   - A parallel LC circuit tuned to the PLCC band (typically 80–500 kHz).
   - Function: Blocks the high-frequency carrier signal from entering the substation busbar, while allowing the 50 Hz power current to pass unimpeded.
   - Impedance in the blocking band: **> 1000 Ω**.
   - Impedance at 50 Hz: **< 0.1 Ω** (negligible).

2. **Coupling Capacitor (CC):**
   - A high-voltage capacitor (typical value **1000–5000 pF**, rated for line voltage).
   - Function: Blocks the 50 Hz power frequency but passes the high-frequency carrier into the PLCC terminal.
   - Acts as a low-pass filter to the substation and a high-pass path to the PLC.

3. **Plcc Terminal (PLC Transceiver):**
   - Modulates the baseband data (voice, telemetry, trip command) onto the carrier frequency.
   - Demodulates incoming carrier back to baseband.
   - Performs FSK (Frequency Shift Keying) or PSK (Phase Shift Keying) modulation.

4. **Line Matching Unit (LMU) / Drain Coil:**
   - A heavy-duty inductor connected between the coupling capacitor and the PLC terminal.
   - Provides a low-impedance path to ground for 50 Hz leakage currents and presents a high impedance to the PLCC carrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Coupling Modes:**
> - **Phase-to-Phase Coupling:** Carrier injected between two phase conductors — preferred for EHV (≥ 220 kV).
> - **Phase-to-Ground Coupling:** Carrier injected between one phase and ground — used in lower-voltage lines.

### 2.2 Fibre Optic — Engineering Foundations

**Why Fibre Optic in Power Systems?**

- **Attenuation Budget:** Even a 50 km link sustains < 10 dB loss at 1550 nm, enabling long-distance substation-to-control-centre links.
- **Bandwidth-Distance Product:** Single-mode fibre exceeds **100 GHz·km**, far beyond copper.
- **Dielectric Nature:** Provides galvanic isolation — critical when interfacing between 400 kV equipment and earthed control rooms.

**Types of Optical Cables in Transmission Corridors:**

| Cable Type | Full Form | Installation | Lightning Performance |
|------------|-----------|--------------|------------------------|
| **OPGW** | Optical Ground Wire | Top of tower (replaces shield wire) | Excellent (acts as shield) |
| **ADSS** | All-Dielectric Self-Supporting | Below phase conductors | Must be routed carefully |
| **OPPC** | Optical Phase Conductor | Replaces one phase conductor | Conducts load current |

**Spectral Bands Used:**

| Band | Wavelength (nm) | Typical Attenuation (dB/km) | Typical Use |
|------|------------------|------------------------------|-------------|
| O-band | 1260–1360 | ≈ 0.35 | Short-haul, LAN |
| C-band | 1530–1565 | ≈ 0.20 | Long-haul, DWDM |
| L-band | 1565–1625 | ≈ 0.25 | Long-haul, DWDM |

### 2.3 IEC 61850 — Conceptual Layers

IEC 61850 models the substation in **three functional levels**:

1. **Process Level:** Instrument Transformers (CT/PT with merging units), Circuit Breaker IEDs, Smart Sensors.
2. **Bay Level:** Protection & Control IEDs (one per bay).
3. **Station Level:** Bay Controller, SCADA Gateway, HMI, Engineering Workstation.

**Communication Services Defined:**

- **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification — ISO 9506):** Client-server messaging for configuration, reporting, control. Used between IEDs and SCADA. Operates over **TCP/IP**.
- **GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event):** Fast, multicast, peer-to-peer messaging for trip and interlock signals. Operates directly over **Layer 2 Ethernet** (no TCP/IP). **Latency < 4 ms** (Type 1A).
- **Sampled Values (SV):** Streaming of digitised analogue waveforms (current, voltage samples) from the merging unit to protection IEDs. Operates over Layer 2 multicast.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** GOOSE is *multicast, peer-to-peer, and bypasses TCP/IP* because TCP retransmission delay is unacceptable for protection tripping. MMS uses *TCP/IP* because it is non-time-critical (configuration, logs).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are board-exam-ready. They cover PLCC signal strength, fibre optic attenuation, signal-to-noise ratio, and IEC 61850 latency classes.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Fibre Attenuation | $A = \alpha L$ where $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in} / P_{out})$ | dB | $\alpha$ in dB/km, $L$ in km |
| 2 | Received Power | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | W or dBm | Used in link budget |
| 3 | Line Trap Impedance | $Z_{trap} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C_{tune}}$ (parallel LC, at resonance) | Ω | Maximised at carrier frequency |
| 4 | Coupling Capacitor Reactance at 50 Hz | $X_{C,50} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot C}$ | Ω | Should be very high to block 50 Hz |
| 5 | Coupling Capacitor Reactance at PLCC | $X_{C,PLC} = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_{PLC} \cdot C}$ | Ω | Should be very low to pass carrier |
| 6 | SNR (dB) | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{signal} / P_{noise})$ | dB | $P$ in W |
| 7 | Carrier-to-Noise Ratio | $C/N = 10 \log_{10}(C_{dBW} - N_{dBW})$ | dB | Used in PLCC link design |
| 8 | Maximum Latency (IEC 61850 Type 1A) | $t_{max} = 3$ ms | ms | Fastest GOOSE class |
| 9 | Type 1B Latency | $t_{max} = 10$ ms | ms | Other trip commands |
| 10 | Sampled Value Frame Rate | $f_{sample} = 4800$ samples/s (for 50 Hz, 80 samples/cycle) | Hz | Standard digital sampling per IEC 61850-9-2 |
| 11 | Channel Capacity (Shannon-Hartley) | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ | bits/s | Theoretical max for fibre channel |
| 12 | OPGW Effective Current Rating | $I_{short-time} = \frac{K \cdot A}{\sqrt{t}}$ | kA·s$^{1/2}$ | $K$ = material constant, $A$ = cross-section |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** In the attenuation table formula $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in} / P_{out})$, do **not** confuse it with the antenna-gain $G = 10 \log_{10}(P_{out} / P_{in})$. The numerator-denominator direction matters.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PLCC** is heavily used by **Power Grid Corporation of India (PGCIL)** for inter-state line teleprotection across the 765 kV and 400 kV network.
- **Fibre optic (OPGW)** is the **backbone of modern Wide-Area Monitoring Systems (WAMS)** and **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** at 400 kV/765 kV substations.
- **IEC 61850** is the global standard adopted by all major utilities for **greenfield digital substations** in India, with substations like the **Pavagada Solar Park** and the **Raichur 765 kV** station deploying it fully.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fibre Optic Attenuation & Link Power Budget

**Problem Context:** A fibre optic link of length **$L = 80 \text{ km}$** uses single-mode fibre operating at **1550 nm**, with an attenuation coefficient **$\alpha = 0.22 \text{ dB/km}$**. The transmitter launches **$P_{in} = 0 \text{ dBm}$** (i.e., **1 mW**). Two connector pairs introduce **0.5 dB loss each**, and a splice every 20 km introduces **0.1 dB loss**. Compute the **received power** $P_{out}$ in dBm and in mW.

**Step 1 — Convert $P_{in}$ to dBm.**

$$P_{in, dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{in}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1 \text{ mW}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right) = 0 \text{ dBm}$$

> *Logical interpretation:* 1 mW is the reference for dBm, so the input power is 0 dBm.

**Step 2 — Compute total fibre attenuation.**

$$A_{fibre} = \alpha \cdot L = 0.22 \text{ dB/km} \times 80 \text{ km} = 17.6 \text{ dB}$$

> *Logical interpretation:* The signal loses 0.22 dB for every kilometre it propagates. After 80 km, total loss is 17.6 dB.

**Step 3 — Compute connector losses.**

There are 2 connector pairs (one at transmitter end, one at receiver end):

$$A_{connector} = 2 \times 0.5 \text{ dB} = 1.0 \text{ dB}$$

> *Logical interpretation:* Connectors introduce small but discrete losses at junction points.

**Step 4 — Compute splice losses.**

Number of splices for 80 km with 20 km spacing: there are $\frac{80}{20} - 1 = 3$ splices.

$$A_{splice} = 3 \times 0.1 \text{ dB} = 0.3 \text{ dB}$$

> *Logical interpretation:* Splices are fusion joints between cable drums; 80 km typically arrives in 4 drum lengths of 20 km each, requiring 3 fusion splices.

**Step 5 — Compute total link loss.**

$$A_{total} = A_{fibre} + A_{connector} + A_{splice} = 17.6 + 1.0 + 0.3 = 18.9 \text{ dB}$$

> *Logical interpretation:* Sum all losses along the link.

**Step 6 — Compute received power in dBm.**

$$P_{out, dBm} = P_{in, dBm} - A_{total} = 0 - 18.9 = -18.9 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 7 — Convert back to mW.**

$$P_{out, mW} = 1 \text{ mW} \times 10^{P_{out, dBm}/10} = 1 \times 10^{-1.89} \approx 0.0129 \text{ mW} = 12.9 \text{ \mu W}$$

> *Final answer:* The receiver sees approximately **−18.9 dBm** or **12.9 µW**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity check:** A typical optical receiver sensitivity is around **−30 dBm**. Since we received −18.9 dBm, we have roughly **11.1 dB of margin**, indicating a healthy link.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line Trap Impedance Behaviour

**Problem Context:** A line trap has tuning inductor **$L_t = 0.5 \text{ mH}$** and tuning capacitor **$C_t = 0.1 \text{ \mu F}$**. Compute (a) the **resonant blocking frequency** and (b) the **reactance at 50 Hz**.

**Step 1 — Resonant frequency formula.**

For a parallel LC circuit, resonance occurs when $X_L = X_C$, giving:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L_t C_t}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{(0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.1 \times 10^{-6})}}$$

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-11}}}$$

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 7.071 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_r \approx \frac{1}{4.443 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 22,507 \text{ Hz} \approx 22.5 \text{ kHz}$$

> *Interpretation:* The line trap blocks signals around 22.5 kHz. For typical PLCC bands of 80–500 kHz, a multi-section line trap is used, often with two cascaded resonant stages.

**Step 3 — Inductive reactance at 50 Hz.**

$$X_L = 2 \pi f L_t = 2 \pi \times 50 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.157 \text{ \Omega}$$

> *Interpretation:* At 50 Hz, the inductor offers only 0.157 Ω — essentially transparent to power current. The 50 Hz power flows through with negligible opposition.

**Step 4 — Capacitive reactance at 50 Hz.**

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C_t} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 31,831 \text{ \Omega}$$

> *Interpretation:* The capacitor in the trap appears as an open circuit at 50 Hz — no power frequency is shunted through it.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PLCC vs Fibre Optic vs IEC 61850 Comparison

| Performance Attribute | PLCC | Fibre Optic | IEC 61850 (over fibre/Ethernet) |
|----------------------|------|-------------|----------------------------------|
| **Data Rate** | 1.2–9.6 kbps (voice + data) | 1 Gbps–100 Gbps | 100 Mbps–1 Gbps typical Ethernet |
| **Latency** | 5–15 ms (one hop) | ≈ 5 µs/km propagation | < 4 ms (GOOSE) |
| **EMI Susceptibility** | High (uses HV line itself) | **Zero (immune)** | Zero (over fibre) |
| **Distance without repeater** | 50–100 km | 100+ km (single mode) | Limited by fibre, ≈ 100 km |
| **Security against tapping** | Low (physically accessible line) | **High (requires physical breach)** | **High (encrypted via VLAN/GSE)** |
| **Cost of deployment** | Low (uses existing line) | **High (cable + laying)** | Software/license only |
| **Maintenance complexity** | Medium | Low | Medium (IED config) |
| **Reliability in fault** | Degrades during line fault | Excellent (line-independent) | Excellent |
| **Typical KTU exam focus** | Components, frequency, block diagram | Attenuation, link budget, OPGW | GOOSE, MMS, IEDs, latency classes |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Shannon Channel Capacity for an Optical Link

**Problem Context:** An optical fibre link has bandwidth **$B = 10 \text{ GHz}$** and an SNR of **40 dB**. Compute the maximum theoretical data rate.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.**

$$SNR_{linear} = 10^{SNR_{dB}/10} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000$$

> *Interpretation:* A 40 dB SNR means the signal is 10,000 times stronger than the noise.

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem.**

$$C = B \log_{2}(1 + SNR) = 10 \times 10^9 \times \log_{2}(1 + 10{,}000)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$\log_{2}(1 + 10{,}000) = \log_{2}(10{,}001) \approx 13.29 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

> *Interpretation:* Each Hz of bandwidth can carry up to ~13.29 bits per second.

**Step 4 — Compute capacity.**

$$C = 10 \times 10^9 \times 13.29 = 1.329 \times 10^{11} \text{ bps} \approx 132.9 \text{ Gbps}$$

> *Final answer:* Theoretical maximum throughput is about **132.9 Gbps**, which explains why modern DWDM systems push terabits per second over a single fibre pair.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PLCC Block Diagram — End-to-End Communication Path

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete signal path from one substation to another over a high-voltage line, showing every functional block (line trap, coupling capacitor, PLC terminal).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph subA[SUBSTATION A]
        A1[Protection Panel\nIED or Teleprotection]
        A2[PLCC Terminal A\nModulator / Demodulator]
        A3[Line Matching Unit\nDrain Coil]
        A4[Coupling Capacitor\n1000 to 5000 pF]
        A5[Line Trap A\nBlocks PLC freq]
    end

    HV[HV OVERHEAD LINE\n50 Hz Power plus\n30 to 500 kHz Carrier]

    subgraph subB[SUBSTATION B]
        B5[Line Trap B]
        B4[Coupling Capacitor B]
        B3[Line Matching Unit B]
        B2[PLCC Terminal B]
        B1[SCADA or Teleprotection IED]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> HV
    HV --> B4 --> B3 --> B2 --> B1
    A4 -.blocked by.-> A5
    B4 -.blocked by.-> B5
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The signal flow follows the **arrows**, while the dotted arrows represent the *blocking action* of the line trap (the trap prevents the carrier from leaking into the substation busbar).
- **A1 / B1** are the source and sink of the information (an IED or a teleprotection scheme).
- **A2 / B2** modulate/demodulate the baseband signal onto the carrier.
- **A3 / B3** are safety devices (drain coil) and impedance-matching units.
- **A4 / B4** are the high-voltage coupling capacitors that let the high-frequency signal pass into the line while blocking 50 Hz.
- **A5 / B5** are line traps that confine the carrier to the line section between the two substations.

---

### 4.2 IEC 61850 Functional Architecture — Substation Levels

The diagram below shows the three IEC 61850 levels and the communication services used between them.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stationLevel[STATION LEVEL]
        SCADA[SCADA Gateway\nHMI and EMS]
        ENGWS[Engineering Workstation\nIEC 61850 SCL Configurator]
        ARCH[Station Bus Ethernet\n100 Mbps Redundant]
    end

    subgraph bayLevel[BAY LEVEL]
        B1[Bay 1 IED\nProtection and Control]
        B2[Bay 2 IED]
        B3[Bay 3 IED]
    end

    subgraph processLevel[PROCESS LEVEL]
        MU1[Merging Unit 1\nCT and PT Digitiser]
        MU2[Merging Unit 2]
        CB1[Breaker IED 1]
        CB2[Breaker IED 2]
    end

    SCADA <-->|MMS over TCP IP| ARCH
    ENGWS <-->|SCL Files| ARCH
    ARCH <-->|MMS and GOOSE| B1
    ARCH <-->|MMS and GOOSE| B2
    ARCH <-->|MMS and GOOSE| B3
    B1 <-->|GOOSE Multicast| B2
    B2 <-->|GOOSE Multicast| B3
    B1 <-->|Sampled Values| MU1
    B2 <-->|Sampled Values| MU2
    B1 <-->|GOOSE Trip| CB1
    B2 <-->|GOOSE Trip| CB2
```

**Engineering Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- **Station Bus (top):** Handles non-time-critical data (configuration, logs, reports) using **MMS over TCP/IP**.
- **Bay-to-Bay GOOSE (middle):** Direct peer-to-peer, multicast, Layer 2 communication used for **interlocking and fast tripping** (no TCP/IP, no retransmission).
- **Process Bus (bottom):** Carries **digitised CT/PT samples** from merging units to protection IEDs using **Sampled Values (SV)** — also Layer 2 multicast.
- The **redundancy** is achieved through protocols like **HSR (High-availability Seamless Ring)** or **PRP (Parallel Redundancy Protocol)**.

---

### 4.3 Fibre Optic Cable Types — OPGW, ADSS, OPPC Visual Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph tower[TRANSMISSION TOWER CROSS SECTION]
        direction TB
        OC[Optical Ground Wire OPGW\nTop Position]
        PH1[Phase Conductor R]
        PH2[Phase Conductor Y]
        PH3[Phase Conductor B]
        ADS[ADSS Cable\nBelow Phase Conductors]
        OPC[OPPC Replaces One Phase Conductor]
    end
    OC -.contains.-> F1[Fibre Core]
    ADS -.contains.-> F2[Fibre Core]
    OPC -.contains.-> F3[Fibre Core]
```

**Visual Notes for the Examiner:**

- **OPGW** sits at the *very top* of the tower — it doubles as both the lightning shield and the communication medium.
- **ADSS** is *all-dielectric* (no metal) — it can be installed live without earthing concerns, but requires careful placement to avoid dry-band arcing.
- **OPPC** is a specialised phase conductor that contains embedded fibres — it is used when the line is to be reconductored and the engineer wants to embed fibre in one of the phase conductors.

---

### 4.4 Communication Protocol Stack — IEC 61850 Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Physical\nFibre Optic or Copper Ethernet]
    L2[Layer 2: Data Link\nEthernet MAC plus VLAN priority]
    L3A[Layer 3 to 4 for MMS: IP plus TCP]
    L3B[Layer 2 direct for GOOSE and SV]
    L5[Application Layer: MMS ISO 9506\nor GOOSE or SV]
    L6[Data Model: Logical Nodes\nLNs like XCBR, PDIS, MMXU]
    L7[Engineering: SCL Substation Configuration Language XML]

    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L3A --> L2 --> L1
    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L3B --> L2 --> L1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **GOOSE and SV bypass the network and transport layers** (IP/TCP) entirely. They use raw **Layer 2 Ethernet multicast** with **IEEE 802.1Q VLAN priority tags** to ensure deterministic, low-latency delivery — essential for substation protection.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)] — 3 Marks**

> *List the main components of a Power Line Carrier Communication (PLCC) system and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A PLCC system comprises the following components:

1. **Line Trap (Wave Trap):** A parallel LC circuit tuned to the PLCC carrier band. It blocks the high-frequency carrier signal from entering the substation busbar while allowing 50 Hz power to pass unimpeded.
2. **Coupling Capacitor (CC):** A high-voltage capacitor connected between the line and the PLC terminal. It blocks the 50 Hz power frequency and passes the high-frequency carrier signal to the PLC terminal.
3. **Line Matching Unit (LMU) / Drain Coil:** A heavy inductor that grounds any 50 Hz leakage currents and presents high impedance to the PLCC carrier, providing impedance matching and safety.
4. **PLCC Terminal (Transceiver):** The electronic device that modulates the baseband signal (voice, data, trip) onto the carrier frequency and demodulates incoming signals. Typically uses FSK or PSK modulation.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Listing the 4 components → 1.5 marks
- Stating the function of each correctly → 1.5 marks

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks**

> *Mention any three advantages of using fibre optic cable in EHV substations over conventional copper cables.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) Immunity:** Fibre is dielectric and is unaffected by the strong electromagnetic fields generated by EHV equipment, unlike copper which picks up severe induced noise.
2. **Galvanic Isolation:** Fibre provides complete electrical isolation between high-voltage equipment and the control room, eliminating ground loop and safety issues.
3. **Higher Bandwidth and Longer Distances:** Fibre supports multi-GHz bandwidth and can transmit over 100 km without repeaters, whereas copper attenuates rapidly beyond a few hundred metres in noisy substations.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark per advantage (with a brief, valid justification)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **PLCC system** used for inter-substation communication. State the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **IEC 61850 standard** in detail. Explain its layered architecture, the role of **IEDs**, and the functions of **GOOSE**, **MMS**, and **Sampled Values** services. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Part A Solution:**

The block diagram of a PLCC system (refer to SECTION 4.1) shows two substations connected by a high-voltage transmission line, with PLCC equipment at each end. The working is as follows:

1. **Signal Origination:** A teleprotection relay or SCADA RTU generates a baseband signal (digital command, voice, or telemetry).

2. **Modulation:** The PLCC terminal at the sending end modulates this baseband signal onto a high-frequency carrier (typically 30–500 kHz) using FSK or PSK.

3. **Injection into the Line:** The modulated signal passes through the **Line Matching Unit (LMU)** and the **Coupling Capacitor (CC)**, which blocks the 50 Hz power component but allows the carrier to flow into the HV line.

4. **Line Trap Function:** At both substations, the **Line Trap** (a parallel LC circuit tuned to the carrier band) presents a high impedance to the carrier, preventing it from entering the substation busbar. This confines the carrier to the transmission line section.

5. **Reception:** At the receiving end, the carrier passes through another coupling capacitor, is filtered and amplified, and then **demodulated** by the PLCC terminal back into baseband for the receiving IED or RTU.

The four main blocks and their functions are:
- **Line Trap:** Blocks carrier, passes 50 Hz.
- **Coupling Capacitor:** Passes carrier, blocks 50 Hz.
- **PLCC Terminal:** Modulation/demodulation.
- **LMU/Drain Coil:** Safety and impedance matching.

**(b) — Part B Solution:**

**IEC 61850 Overview:**
IEC 61850 is the international standard for **substation communication and integration**, first published in 2003 by the IEC. It standardises the *data models*, *communication services*, and *engineering process* to enable seamless interoperability between IEDs from different manufacturers.

**Layered Architecture (refer to SECTION 4.2):**

1. **Process Level:** Contains instrument transformers (CTs/PTs), merging units, and breaker IEDs. Digital samples of current and voltage are produced here.
2. **Bay Level:** Contains protection and control IEDs. Each IED performs bay-level functions (e.g., distance protection for one transmission line).
3. **Station Level:** Contains SCADA gateways, HMIs, and engineering workstations for the entire substation.

**Role of IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices):**
IEDs are microprocessor-based devices that perform **protection, control, monitoring, and metering**. Each IED has a **standardised data model** built from **Logical Nodes (LNs)** such as:
- `XCBR` — Circuit Breaker
- `PDIS` — Distance Protection
- `MMXU` — Measurement Unit
- `CSWI` — Switch Controller

**Communication Services:**

- **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification — ISO 9506):** Client-server protocol used for configuration, reporting, control, and event logging. Operates over **TCP/IP**. Used between IEDs and SCADA. Non-time-critical.
- **GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event):** Fast multicast, peer-to-peer service for **tripping and interlocking signals**. Operates directly over **Layer 2 Ethernet**, bypassing TCP/IP. Latency < 4 ms. Used for time-critical protection.
- **Sampled Values (SV):** Continuous streaming of digitised analogue samples (current/voltage) from merging units to protection IEDs. Standardised under IEC 61850-9-2. Sampling rate is typically 80 samples/cycle (4 kHz for 50 Hz systems, 4.8 ksamples/s).

**Engineering Workflow:** IEC 61850 uses **SCL (Substation Configuration Language)**, an XML-based language, to describe the entire substation — its IEDs, data models, and communication bindings. This enables vendor-agnostic engineering and configuration.

---

**Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **fibre optic communication** in power systems. Compare **OPGW and ADSS** cables in terms of construction, installation, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **three-level architecture of an IEC 61850-based substation**. List any **four Logical Nodes** used in IEC 61850 and explain their functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Part A Solution:**

**Principle of Fibre Optic Communication:**
Fibre optic communication uses **light pulses** (in the infrared range, 850–1550 nm) propagating through a thin silica glass core. The core is surrounded by a **cladding** of lower refractive index, which causes **total internal reflection** and confines light to the core. Data is encoded by modulating the light source (typically an LED or laser diode) — turning it on/off, or varying its intensity. At the receiving end, a photodiode converts the light signal back into an electrical signal.

In power systems, fibre cables are integrated with the **transmission line infrastructure** for SCADA, protection, and teleprotection.

**Comparison — OPGW vs ADSS:**

| Feature | OPGW (Optical Ground Wire) | ADSS (All-Dielectric Self-Supporting) |
|---------|---------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Construction** | Steel or aluminium-clad steel strands with embedded aluminium buffer tubes containing fibres | All-dielectric (no metal); aramid yarn reinforced; central buffer tube with fibres |
| **Installation Position** | Top of tower, replacing the conventional shield wire | Below phase conductors, on its own attachment hardware |
| **Earthing Required** | Yes (grounded at every tower) | No (fully dielectric) |
| **Lightning Performance** | Excellent (acts as shield wire) | Moderate (must be routed to avoid dry-band arcing) |
| **Live-line Installation** | Not possible (replaces grounded wire) | **Possible (no earthing needed)** |
| **Typical Span** | Up to 500 m | Up to 1000 m (longer spans possible) |
| **Application** | Greenfield EHV lines, long-haul backbone | Retrofit installations, distribution networks, river crossings |
| **Cost** | Higher (metallic components) | Lower (no metal) but more sensitive to sag-tension |

**(b) — Part B Solution:**

**Three-Level Architecture of IEC 61850 Substation (refer to SECTION 4.2):**

1. **Process Level:** Lowest level — contains the merging units (which digitise CT/PT outputs), circuit-breaker IEDs, and primary sensors. Communication here is over the **process bus** using **Sampled Values (SV)** and **GOOSE**.
2. **Bay Level:** Contains protection and control IEDs (one per bay). They perform functions like distance protection, overcurrent protection, breaker control, and metering. They communicate with the merging units and the station level.
3. **Station Level:** Top level — contains SCADA gateways, HMIs, and the engineering workstation. Communication between the station and bay levels is via the **station bus** using **MMS** and **GOOSE**.

**Four Logical Nodes (LNs) in IEC 61850:**

1. **`XCBR` — Circuit Breaker:** Models the physical circuit breaker with its status (open/closed), interlocking, and control attributes. Used to issue open/trip commands.
2. **`PDIS` — Distance Protection:** Models the distance protection function. Contains pick-up settings, impedance characteristics, and trip-decision data.
3. **`MMXU` — Measurement Unit:** Provides three-phase analogue measurements (current, voltage, active/reactive power, frequency) for metering and SCADA.
4. **`CSWI` — Switch Controller:** Models the switch controller that manages switching operations (e.g., isolator control) with synchronisation checks and interlocking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse GOOSE and MMS.** GOOSE is *Layer 2 multicast, used for fast protection trips*; MMS is *TCP/IP-based, used for non-critical SCADA traffic*. Many students mix these up and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Don't say "IEC 61850 is a protocol."** It is a *standard* that defines *protocols* (MMS, GOOSE, SV) and *data models* (Logical Nodes). Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 3. **For PLCC block diagrams:** Always draw and *label* the line trap, coupling capacitor, LMU, and PLC terminal. A diagram without labels is given only partial credit.
> 4. **For fibre questions:** State the wavelength in *nm* (not µm) — examiners deduct marks for unit errors.
> 5. **For attenuation calculations:** Watch the sign — $P_{out, dBm} = P_{in, dBm} - A_{total}$. Many students add the loss by mistake.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.

- [ ] **PLCC Frequency Band:** 30–500 kHz (carrier superimposed on 50 Hz power).
- [ ] **PLCC Components:** Line Trap → Coupling Capacitor → LMU → PLCC Terminal (sequence matters for the diagram).
- [ ] **Line Trap Function:** Blocks carrier, passes 50 Hz (parallel LC at resonance).
- [ ] **Coupling Capacitor Function:** Passes carrier, blocks 50 Hz.
- [ ] **PLCC Advantage:** No new cable needed; uses existing HV line.
- [ ] **Fibre Optic Standard Wavelengths:** 850 nm (short haul), 1300 nm (medium), 1550 nm (long haul, lowest loss ≈ 0.2 dB/km).
- [ ] **OPGW vs ADSS:** OPGW is metallic (acts as shield), ADSS is all-dielectric (live-line installable).
- [ ] **Fibre Advantage in Substations:** EMI immunity, galvanic isolation, high bandwidth, low attenuation.
- [ ] **IEC 61850 Year:** First published 2003 by IEC.
- [ ] **IEC 61850 Three Levels:** Process → Bay → Station.
- [ ] **MMS:** Client-server, TCP/IP, for SCADA/configuration/long reports.
- [ ] **GOOSE:** Peer-to-peer, Layer 2 multicast, < 4 ms latency (Type 1A), for tripping/interlocking.
- [ ] **Sampled Values (SV):** Streaming digitised CT/PT samples; 80 samples/cycle (4.8 ksamples/s for 50 Hz).
- [ ] **IEC 61850 Logical Nodes:** `XCBR` (breaker), `PDIS` (distance protection), `MMXU` (measurements), `CSWI` (switch controller).
- [ ] **IEC 61850 SCL:** Substation Configuration Language — XML-based engineering description.
- [ ] **Attenuation Formula:** $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$; always subtract loss from $P_{in}$ in dBm.
- [ ] **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — used in fibre bandwidth justification.
- [ ] **Key Mnemonic:** **P**ower **L**ine = **PLCC**; **G**OOSE bypasses **IP**; **M**MS rides on **M**anufacturing Message Specification.
